summary: Misrepresentation. Harris called for decriminalization, not legalization of drug possession, in 2019.    https://x.com/__Pfeiffer/status/1833295599317254393    With Decriminalization, it would remain illegal, but a person would not be prosecuted for the act.    With Legalization, it would no longer be illegal.    https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/decriminalization#:~:text=Decriminalization%20means%20it%20would%20remain,legal%20prohibitions%20against%20the%20act.    https://adf.org.au/talking-about-drugs/law/decriminalisation/overview-decriminalisation-legalisation/#:~:text=Decriminalisation%20is%20not%20legalisation.%20If%20drug%20possession%20and%20personal%20use%20are%20decriminalised%2C%20it%20is%20still%20illegal%20to%20possess%20and%20use%20drugs.    https://www.turnbridge.com/news-events/latest-articles/decriminalization-vs-legalization/#:~:text=Decriminalization%20is%20not%20synonymous%20with%20legalization.    https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6181739/#:~:text=Legalization%20of%20cannabis,article%2C%20or%20behavior.    https://adf.org.au/talking-about-drugs/law/decriminalisation/

On the right, a yellow notepad-style graphic presents a question: "Since drug use is better addressed as a public health issue (through treatment and other programming), will you support the decriminalization at the federal level of all drug possession for personal use?" Below the question, a large green checkmark is placed over the word "YES" in a red box. To the right of "YES", text reads "Harris in 2019 ACLU Candidate Questionnaire". 00:16 - 00:23: The video cuts back to the studio. The male anchor is now seated at a glass desk, facing a female anchor who is also seated across from him. The background is a bright, illuminated city skyline at night. The female anchor, wearing a light yellow jacket, is looking down and writing on papers. The male anchor is gesturing with his hands as he speaks. The text banners at the bottom remain the same, but the time is updated to "7:50 PM ET" and then "4:50 PM PT" at 0:19. *   **0:06-0:08:** Offset continues to point and speak, emphasizing his point. Kai Cenat brushes his teeth with an almost rhythmic, emphatic motion, looking serious.
*   **0:08-0:09:** Offset gestures with his left hand as he continues speaking. On-screen text reads, "Get to tasting like". Kai Cenat continues brushing and nods in agreement.
*   **0:09-0:10:** Offset speaks a word, and text "Mint and sh*t" appears on screen. Kai Cenat raises the toothbrush slightly and gestures with it while brushing.

*   **0:11-0:12:** Offset continues to gesture. Kai Cenat leans forward slightly, eyes wide, with an almost disgusted look, still holding the toothbrush.

*   **0:18-0:19:** Offset leans back in his chair, shaking his head, looking disgusted. Text "Eggs" appears, referring to the food.

**Audio Description:**

*   **0:00-0:02:** A male voice (Offset) asks, "You brush your teeth..."

*   **0:04-0:05:** A different male voice (Kai Cenat, off-screen initially) answers, "After breakfast." Offset immediately says, "Thank you."

A "TODAY" show banner is visible at the bottom of the screen, which states "DE NIRO SOUNDS OFF ROBERT TALKS VACCINES & TRIBECA FILM FESTIVAL".

At 0:00, Robert De Niro begins speaking, and the video cuts to a close-up shot of him. He is looking slightly off-camera to his right, speaking earnestly. The banner "DE NIRO SOUNDS OFF ROBERT TALKS VACCINES & TRIBECA FILM FESTIVAL" remains visible at the bottom.
De Niro says: "I still have mixed feelings about it. We, I wanted it to be in, we decided to pull it because I didn't want to pull the the festival in a direction."

At 0:10, the video cuts back to the wide shot of all three individuals.
De Niro continues: "But it was from filmmakers, uh, according to Jane, and so I said, let me just let it take it out. But I'm not 100% sure that was the right thing to do ultimately. Next time, I'll know, hopefully more clearly about when presented with a situation like this. But there is something definitely there with Vaxx."

At 0:27, the video cuts to a close-up of Al Roker, who nods in agreement as De Niro continues speaking off-camera.
Al Roker says: "Right."

At 0:28, the video cuts back to a close-up of Robert De Niro.
De Niro continues: "And there is something going on uh, with the CDC, the pharmaceutical industry."

At 0:36, the video cuts back to the wide shot. Al Roker is seen speaking, gesturing with his hands, while Jane Rosenthal begins to speak.
Jane Rosenthal says: "People should read the reviews of the film, because there are things in the film that put aside the point that you're making."

At 0:40, the video cuts to a close-up of Jane Rosenthal.
Jane Rosenthal continues: "There are some issues in the film from its consistency, from somebody saying one thing 20 minutes in and then reverses their point of view."

At 0:42, the video displays a graph titled "US Autism Prevalence." The x-axis shows years from 1975 to 2015, and the y-axis shows the prevalence of autism in children as a percentage. Data points are marked with "1 in 5,000" (1975), "1 in 2,500" (1985), "1 in 500" (1995), "1 in 166" (2005), "1 in 150" (2006), "1 in 110" (2008), and "1 in 68" (2015).

At 0:44, the graph transitions to a visual representation of a timeline extending into the future. It shows the year "2032" with a flag marking "1 in 2."

At 0:48, the video cuts back to a close-up of Jane Rosenthal, still speaking. She finishes her previous thought.

At 0:50, the video cuts back to a close-up of Robert De Niro.
De Niro responds: "Well, there are inaccuracies in any film, any documentary. Uh, and so I don't really, and I'm not defending it in that way. I don't, I don't know, but there are accuracies or things that you have to question, and that's my reason for having the film, for to question certain things or open it up for discussion, pros and cons."

At 1:10, De Niro continues: "There's something that is not quite kosher in all of this. That's all I can say." The video ends on this close-up of Robert De Niro.

summary: Vaccine doesn't cause autism.    https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8207024/
tweetText:
Donald Trump says he’s open to the possibility of eliminating childhood vaccines, suggests he agrees with the widely debunked theory that Vaccines cause autism, and says RFK Jr will investigate the matter: 

“Something is going on…People are looking at a lot of different things.”

caption.video0:
The video opens with a split screen, featuring two distinct scenes. On the left, Donald Trump is seated in a modern-looking grey armchair with a chrome frame, facing an interviewer who is out of frame. He wears a dark suit, a white shirt, and a gold tie, with a small American flag pin on his lapel. His expression is serious as he listens. The setting appears to be a large, brightly lit room with a polished marble floor and glass walls, reflecting overhead lights.

On the right side of the screen, an inset video shows Robert F. Kennedy Jr. (RFK Jr.) standing at a podium with a microphone, addressing an audience. He is wearing a dark pinstriped suit, a white shirt, and a black tie. The background is a plain blue curtain. A "Meet the Press Exclusive" logo is visible in the bottom right corner of this inset.

The audio begins with the interviewer asking Trump, "Let me ask you about RFK Jr. He has obviously talked about his skepticism of vaccines. He's expressed opposition to childhood vaccines. Do you want to see childhood vaccines eliminated?" (0:00-0:13).

Trump responds, "Uh, if they're dangerous for the children. Look." (0:13-0:14). The interviewer interjects, "So possibly?" (0:14-0:15).

Trump continues, "When you look at some of the problems, when you look at what's going on with disease and sickness in our country, something's wrong." (0:16-0:25).

The interviewer asks, "Are you talking about autism?" (0:25-0:26).

Trump replies, "Well, if you take a look at autism, go back 25 years. Autism was almost nonexistent. It was, you know, one out of 100,000. And now it's close to one out of 100." (0:26-0:38).

The interviewer acknowledges, "Well," but Trump continues, "I mean, what, what's happening? If they can find it. Now, I did something the other night that was a little unusual. At Mar-a-Lago, I called the drug companies, the top drug companies. And I called RFK Jr. and Dr. Oz and some of his people. And I said, let's all get together and let's figure out where we're going. Because we're going to do a lot of things. Number one, we're going to reduce prices because the middleman makes more money than the drug companies, in all fairness to the drug companies. There's a middleman that nobody even knows who they are. And you look at our drug prices, they're much higher than the prices for the same medicine for the same stuff. So, we met, and we were met for a long time. And we talked about pricing and we talked about vaccines, you know, in terms of what happens. We talked about pesticides, we talked about everything. And I think a lot of good things are going to come from him. And, he's, uh, he's not going to upset any system. He's not going to upset the system. He's not looking to, you know, to reinvent the wheel totally, but when you look at the numbers, we really don't have a very healthy country." (0:38-1:45).

The interviewer interjects again, "Sir, going back 25 years, studies show that there is no link between vaccines and autism. And yet, it sounds like you are open to the possibility of him looking at getting rid of them?" (1:45-1:55).

Trump responds, "I'm open to anything. I think somebody has to find out. If you go back 25 years ago, you had very little autism. Now you have it, I mean," (1:56-2:03).

The interviewer counters, "Well, they say because they're better at identifying it." (2:04-2:06).

Trump says, "Uh, one in 100,000 and now it's one in 100. That's, that's a pretty bad number." (2:08-2:15). The interviewer tries to speak about "childhood vaccines" but Trump cuts in, "I mean, something is going on. I don't know if it's vaccines. Maybe it's, maybe it's chlorine in the water, right? You know, people are looking at a lot of different things." (2:15-2:24).

The interviewer again attempts to speak about childhood vaccines, saying, "Well, you know, childhood vaccines have prevented about 4 million deaths around the world every year, something like that." (2:24-2:31).

Trump replies, "I think that's great. I'm all for it. I think it's great. Hey look, I'm not against vaccines. The polio vaccine is the greatest thing. If somebody told me get rid of the polio vaccine, they're going to have to work real hard to convince me. Uh, I think vaccines are, certain vaccines are incredible. But maybe some aren't. And if they aren't, we have to find out. But when you talk about autism, because it was brought up, and you look at the amount we have today versus 20 or 25 years ago, it's pretty scary." (2:32-3:00).

The interviewer makes one last attempt, "Well again, scientists say that's because they've gotten better at identifying it and there's no link in studies," (3:00-3:04).

Trump then states, "But the drug companies are going to be working with RFK Jr. and he's, you know, he's, uh, he's been an interesting guy to me. I've, I've watched him for 25 years. And he's been an interesting guy." (3:04-3:16). The video ends as Trump finishes his sentence, with the "Meet the Press Exclusive" logo still visible on the RFK Jr. inset.

caption.video0:
The video features a man presenting common "weeds" found in his driveway as valuable medicinal and edible plants.

Here's a detailed breakdown:

*   **0:00 - 0:04:** The video opens with a medium shot of a man wearing a gray baseball cap with an American flag patch and a dark blue hoodie with a white deer skull graphic (mirrored text "REALTREE"). He has a mustache and is looking directly at the camera, talking. Behind him is a gravel driveway, green grass, and trees under a clear blue sky. Text overlay on the screen reads: "What Do You See In The Driveway?" He asks, "What do you see when you see weeds in the driveway? Let me show you what I see."
*   **0:04 - 0:08:** The camera pans down to the gravel driveway. Patches of green grass and small yellow flowers are visible growing among the white and gray stones. A distant white vehicle (only the wheels are visible) is parked further down the driveway. The man's voice states, "When you look down a weedy driveway, I actually see an apothecary, or medicine cabinet."
*   **0:08 - 0:18 (Dandelion):** A close-up shot focuses on a cluster of dandelions with their bright yellow flowers and serrated green leaves growing on the gravel. Text overlay: "Dandelion". The man explains, "Take dandelion for instance. It's edible, highly nutritious, and has a slew of medicinal properties. It's a great detox herb. Every single part of the plant is edible and usable. So all in all, that's probably one of the best additions to your yard."
*   **0:18 - 0:34 (Broadleaf Plantain):** The camera shifts slightly to reveal another plant with broad, oval-shaped green leaves forming a rosette. Some leaves have small holes, likely from insects. The man's hand briefly touches one of the leaves. Text overlay: "Plantain". He says, "Right next to that, we have plantain. This is broadleaf plantain. If you remember the term 'plantain for pain,' this is a pain-relieving herb. It's edible, it can be eaten like spinach or raw in a salad. Highly nutritious, extremely good for your body. It's good for your digestion, they can help heal internal wounds, and of course, it's a pain reliever."
*   **0:34 - 0:48 (Sowthistle):** The camera moves to a plant with spiky, dandelion-like leaves, but larger and more elongated, growing in a patch of grass next to the driveway. The man's hand gently pinches a leaf to demonstrate its texture. Text overlay: "Sowthistle". He describes, "Just off the driveway, this is called sowthistle. Thistle have giant leaves or flowers just like a dandelion, and those thorns, when the when the plant is this small, those thorns aren't really very thorny. This plant is also super nutritious, it's a detox herb just like dandelion, and it shares many of the same properties."
*   **0:48 - 1:01 (Wild Violet):** A close-up shows a small plant with heart-shaped green leaves and a tiny purple flower peeking out from among dried grass and gravel. Text overlay: "Wild violet". The man elaborates, "A little bit of wild violet. Wild violet is a medicinal plant. It's good for coughs, it's good for pain. It can be used internally or externally, made into a tea or a capsule. This plant can also help break a fever. It's been used for thousands of years medicinally and it's actually a really good plant to have."
*   **1:01 - 1:15 (Creeping Charlie):** The camera focuses on another patch of gravel and dried grass with a small plant featuring rounded, scalloped leaves and small purple, trumpet-shaped flowers. Text overlay: "Creeping Charlie". He explains, "Wild violet, we have another purple flower. There, this Creeping Charlie. Creeping Charlie, also called ground ivy, is actually a member of the mint family. It is safe enough for young children and infants, strong enough for a full-grown man. It's really good for your digestion, it can be used for a wound healer, can also help break a fever, and it's also highly nutritious."
*   **1:15 - 1:37 (Chicory):** The shot displays a large rosette of green, somewhat ruffled and serrated leaves, growing directly on the gravel. Text overlay: "Chicory". The man states, "Close to our dandelion, we have chicory. Chicory is a great plant, very closely related to dandelion, though it has a purple flower and not a yellow one. Every part of this plant is also edible. And the root contains a compound called inulin, which your body absorbs just like insulin. Chicory also has the unique ability to be able to break the barrier in your brain that tells your brain you want more when people have addictions. They've actually used it for for for some pretty strong addiction-related illnesses."
*   **1:37 - 1:55 (Wintercress):** The camera shows a plant with clusters of small, bright yellow flowers and green leaves, growing in a patch of grass. Text overlay: "Wintercress". He says, "Further into the driveway, we have a plant that's called wintercress. Wintercress is popping up all over. It's one of the first edible plants that's available in the spring. The plant has a lot of medicinal properties. This is also a very good lung herb. It can help with pneumonia. It's antimicrobial, it's nutritious, and you can use it in any in many different ways. You can eat it raw, you can put it into a capsule, turn it into an extract, whatever works for you."
*   **1:55 - 2:09 (Burdock):** A shot of very large, broad, rough-textured green leaves forming a dense clump among the gravel and other greenery. Text overlay: "Burdock". He notes, "Some burdock that's back in the back of the driveway. This plant is also highly nutritious. The leaves are very nutritious and edible. The root is very healing, it's anti-inflammatory. It's actually a main ingredient for a compound called Essiac, which is an anti-cancer compound. Really healthy."
*   **2:09 - 2:19 (Narrow Leaf Plantain):** The camera focuses on a plant with long, narrow, ribbed green leaves growing upright. The man's finger points to the distinct parallel veins on a leaf. Text overlay: "Narrow Leaf Plantain". He explains, "Next to that, we have what's called longleaf plantain, narrowleaf plantain. It is exactly like the other plantain I showed you, plantain for pain. It can be eaten the same way, it has all the same nutrients, all in all, this is a pretty good plant too."
*   **2:19 - 2:34 (Yellow Dock / Curly Dock):** A dense cluster of large green leaves, some with slightly wavy or curly edges and reddish stems/veins, is shown. Small yellow dandelion-like flowers are also visible around it. Text overlay: "Yellow Dock". He states, "And then we have yellow dock or curly dock, it depends on what you want to call it. If the leaves are more curly, it's curly dock, but a dock is a dock, here dock, there dock, everywhere a dock dock. Yellow dock root has inulin in it, just like dandelion and chicory. It can be absorbed in your body just like insulin. It's anti-inflammatory, they're highly nutritious. You could cook the roots just like you would your carrots."
*   **2:34 - 2:50 (Black Eyed Susan):** The camera shows a young plant with oblong, somewhat hairy green leaves, growing among clover and other grass. The man's hand briefly touches a leaf. Text overlay: "Black Eyed Susan". He explains, "Last but not least, or what's going to be, Black Eyed Susans. These plants get very invasive at times. They are the large yellow and black flowers. Um, they are closely related to echinacea. They have the ability to increase your T-cell production. They are an immunomodulator. Every plant of this, every part of this plant is also edible and highly nutritious."
*   **2:50 - 3:00:** The man reappears in a medium shot, smiling at the camera, standing in the green grass beside the gravel driveway. Text overlay: "What did you think?". He concludes, "So that's what I see when I walk down the driveway. Maybe later, let's take a walk in the yard and I'll show you the apothecary that's in the yard. What do you think? Let me know in the comments. Have a good day."

tweetText:
ME, or Myalgic encephalomyelitis, is linked to your genetics, an early study by Decode ME has suggested.

They’ve found eight areas of genetic code in people with ME are ‘significantly different’ to the DNA of people without the disease.

The results still need to be reviewed, but the University of Edinburgh research team hope these results will pave the way for future research and the development of drugs.

caption.video0:
The video is a news report about a study linking Myalgic Encephalomyelitis (ME), also known as Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (CFS), to genetics.

**0:00-0:05** The video opens with an animated representation of a DNA double helix, glowing with blue and pink light, against a dark, starry background. The text "ME linked to your genetics - early study indicates" appears in a pink box in the center. In the top right corner, the "4 News" logo is visible. A male voice begins to speak.

**0:05-0:11** The scene transitions to a medium shot of a man with a beard, wearing a grey button-up shirt over a white t-shirt, sitting at a desk with a microphone in front of him. Behind him are purple-lit world maps. He states, "ME, or Myalgic Encephalomyelitis, is linked to your genetics, an early study suggests." The text "Decode ME" appears on screen. He continues, "Decode ME at the University of Edinburgh looked at the DNA of 15,500 people in the UK."

**0:11-0:17** The video cuts to a laptop screen displaying lines of code, with a source attribution: "Source: Decode ME, University of Edinburgh." This is followed by another screen showing a scientific graph with clusters of blue, yellow, and red data points, representing genetic information. The speaker's voice continues, "And scientists discovered eight areas of genetic code in people with ME that are significantly different to the DNA of people without the disease."

**0:17-0:20** The scene shows a group of people, presumably scientists, working on laptops around a round table. A large screen in the background displays charts and data, with "Decode ME" visible at the top. The source attribution remains.

**0:20-0:25** The video returns to the male speaker at the desk. He says, "It's the first piece of early evidence to suggest that genes contribute to a person's chances of developing ME."

**0:25-0:29** A shot of a person lying in bed, wearing a sleep mask and headphones, is shown. The person is covered by a white sheet. The speaker says, "ME is a debilitating disease."

**0:29-0:31** The video cuts back to the male speaker. A pink text box appears over him, stating "67 million worldwide." He explains, "...which is estimated to affect 67 million people worldwide."

**0:31-0:34** A woman in a motorized wheelchair is shown, wearing glasses and a patterned jacket over red trousers. She looks straight ahead. The speaker says, "It causes extreme fatigue..."

**0:34-0:39** The scene shows a protest sign being held up, partially obscuring the male speaker. The red sign reads "ME/CFS is a living death" in large white letters, with smaller text below it describing ME's impact on quality of life, and "MILLIONSMISSING.ORG" at the bottom. The speaker continues, "...after a small amount of physical or mental activity, as well as pain and brain fog."

**0:39-0:44** An older woman with grey hair and glasses, wearing a light-colored striped shirt, is seen sitting in a chair. She gestures with her hands as she speaks. She says, "It's like my body's in distress and it's screaming at me."

**0:44-0:47** A card with "Decode ME" and "The largest ever DNA study of ME/CFS" is displayed, with the question "Do you have myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue?" below. The male speaker is visible below the card, saying, "The new study could be a major breakthrough for people with ME."

**0:47-0:53** The video returns to the male speaker. He explains, "People with ME have often said that their condition has been ignored, overlooked and stigmatized."

**0:53-0:56** The speaker continues, "Now they're hoping that that could change."

**0:56-1:08** The older woman, identified as "Sarah Chadfield, Decode ME Participant," is shown again, smiling slightly. She says, "It does exist. It now exists on my DNA. It's not in here, and it's not, uh, I've not made it up, and I'm not, um, imagining it, it's real." She points to her head briefly, then smiles.

**1:08-1:19** The view is over Sarah Chadfield's shoulder, looking at a man in a light blue shirt and glasses sitting opposite her. This man is identified as "Professor Chris Ponting, Decode ME Chief Investigator." He states, "What I would love is for anyone who doesn't think it's a thing, that this is not an organic illness, for them to have in front of them the evidence that you have just told me."

**1:19-1:24** A close-up shot of several colorful pill organizers is shown, filled with various pills. The days of the week and times of day (Mon Night, Tue Noon, Thu Morn) are visible. The speaker narrates, "However, there is still no diagnostic test for ME or a cure."

**1:24-1:28** The male speaker returns to the screen, stating, "People are often diagnosed through ruling out other conditions first."

**1:28-1:32** An aerial shot of a city with buildings and trees under a cloudy sky is shown. The speaker says, "The scientists behind the study say that these findings won't bring about a cure or test immediately..."

**1:32-1:37** A wide shot shows many pairs of shoes of different styles and colors arranged on stone steps. People are gathered in the background, and a sign that reads "FUND RESEARCH" is visible, along with "MILLIONSMISSING." The speaker continues, "...and the study hasn't been fully reviewed by other scientists yet."

**1:37-1:42** The male speaker is back on screen. He concludes, "But the findings can offer clues to guide future research and drug development."

**1:42-1:58** The video returns to Professor Chris Ponting. He explains, "Now that we have narrowed down to these very small slices of of our DNA, we can then tell everyone in the scientific community, it is their job, it is up to them now to find out what has gone wrong." As he speaks, the source "Source: Malickusmusic/Quickenking/FMA/CCBY" is displayed at the bottom of the screen.

Here's a detailed breakdown of the video content and audio synchronization:

summary: "Disease X" is neither an actual disease nor new.   It is a generic placeholder name used for years in scientific fields in the analysis of potential worst-case scenarios, and defines a theoretical disease that may potentially trigger a global crisis.    https://www.unmc.edu/healthsecurity/transmission/2023/09/27/what-is-disease-x-how-scientists-are-preparing-for-the-next-pandemic/  https://www.forbes.com/sites/brianbushard/2024/01/11/what-is-disease-x-right-wing-circles-slam-hypothetical-pandemic/?sh=32a9551b828d  https://www.politifact.com/factchecks/2023/aug/07/tiktok-posts/conspiracy-theorists-falsely-claim-disease-x-is-th/  https://www.unmc.edu/healthsecurity/transmission/2023/08/29/disease-x/

caption.video0:
The video is a BBC News report about a new vaccine research facility for "Disease X," located at Porton Down in the UK.

**0:00 - 0:06**: The video opens with a female news anchor in a green sweater, speaking directly to the camera. The background is a blurred newsroom with multiple screens. A red banner at the bottom of the screen reads "New vaccine facility for 'Disease X'" with "BBC NEWS" and "ShadowofEzra" logos. The anchor states, "The British government has unveiled a new vaccine research facility where scientists are working to prevent future pandemics."

**0:06 - 0:12**: The screen splits. The anchor remains on the left, while the right side shows a close-up of a female scientist in a lab coat and orange gloves, working with a scientific instrument within a sterile enclosure. The anchor continues, "It's located at Porton Down, a high-security research facility best known for its work on chemical warfare."

**0:12 - 0:22**: The right screen shows a close-up of a multi-channel pipette being used to transfer a red liquid into vials. This transitions to another female scientist in a lab coat and orange gloves, looking intently at a computer screen displaying a circular image with small dots (likely cells or pathogens). The anchor explains, "Experts are preparing for what is known as Disease X, or the next pandemic virus." The scene then shows a hand in an orange glove manipulating a clear container, followed by a close-up of a female scientist looking through a microscope. The anchor introduces, "Our health correspondent Dominic Hughes was given rare access to the facility."

**0:22 - 0:27**: The video cuts to a wide shot inside a laboratory. A female scientist with a ponytail and yellow gloves is working inside a laminar flow hood, handling containers. The audio describes, "The delicate task of protecting the nation's health." Another shot shows her working with a pipette, again wearing yellow gloves.

**0:27 - 0:34**: A wider shot of the lab shows several scientists working at benches with various equipment. Some are seated at computers, while others are at laminar flow hoods. The audio states, "This is one of the laboratories at Porton Down where scientists are analyzing current threats, new variants of COVID for example..." A close-up shows a male scientist in a lab coat, then pans to a female scientist from the side, with another male scientist blurred in the background, working inside a hood.

**0:34 - 0:40**: A gloved hand (orange) is seen operating a computer mouse, followed by a shot of a female scientist in glasses and orange gloves looking at a computer screen that displays a grid of circular images, similar to petri dishes, with numbers in the corners. The audio continues, "...and trying to identify new ones."

**0:40 - 0:53**: A close-up on the computer screen shows a larger circular image with purple dots, and the number "22*" in the top left corner. The shot then focuses on the scientist's face as she observes the screen. Another gloved hand (orange) is shown handling a rectangular container under a microscope. A close-up of a female scientist looking through a "LEICA" microscope. A wider shot shows her at the microscope with another female scientist in orange gloves and a lab coat standing behind a glass partition. The audio explains, "Inside these purpose-built labs, more than 200 scientists, working for the UK Health Security Agency, are helping to develop and test vaccines against a range of diseases. It's vital work to keep us all safe."

**0:53 - 1:02**: Professor Isabel Oliver, Chief Scientific Officer of UKHSA, is shown being interviewed in the lab. She is wearing a lab coat and has long, light brown hair. In the background, other scientists are visible working in hoods. She says, "We've got in many respects the toughest job in the world, which is to protect health against infectious diseases and environmental hazards. And it is tough because we know that the risks of new and emerging infections, including those of pandemic potential, is increasing."

**1:02 - 1:09**: The video returns to split screen, with the news anchor on the left and various lab activities on the right, including a scientist at a microscope and a multi-channel pipette transferring red liquid.

**1:09 - 1:20**: The video briefly shows a man walking down a long, white hallway, then transitions to black and white archival footage. Scientists in white lab coats are seen working with glassware in an old-fashioned lab. Another shot shows one scientist helping another put on a face mask. Then, gloved hands are shown handling what looks like a biological sample. The audio notes, "For decades, scientists at Porton Down have been involved in medical research, as well as the work on chemical and biological warfare, for which the center is perhaps better known."

**1:20 - 1:30**: Dominic Hughes, the health correspondent, is now standing in the modern lab, speaking to the camera. Two scientists are visible working in separate laminar flow hoods in the background. He says, "During the pandemic, laboratories like this one played an absolutely vital role in assessing how effective the vaccines were in combating the coronavirus. And that work continues today."

**1:30 - 1:44**: The video cuts to a wide shot of the lab with two female scientists working, followed by a close-up of a hand in an orange glove using a pipette to transfer a substance. This is followed by a shot of a gloved hand (purple) with a "UK Health Security Agency" logo on the lab coat, indicating the agency's involvement. The audio mentions, "But as well as monitoring how effective existing vaccines are against new COVID variants, the new vaccine development and evaluation center is looking at emerging threats for which no vaccine yet exists."

**1:44 - 1:53**: Images of ticks are shown, first on a white cloth, then being handled with tweezers, and finally, several ticks inside a white bottle cap. The audio identifies one of the threats: "One of those is Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever, a virus that is spread by the bite of an infected tick and is fatal in about 30% of cases."

**1:53 - 2:02**: An "Emergency Room" sign in Arabic and English is shown, followed by a close-up of a person's bruised leg, and then an IV drip bag containing blood. A man is then shown lying in a hospital bed, receiving an IV. The audio states, "A serious outbreak occurred in Afghanistan in 2017. But the disease is widespread across Africa, Asia, the Middle East, and the Balkans."

**2:02 - 2:17**: The video shows an upward view of a city street with buildings, then a wider shot of people walking on a busy street. The audio discusses, "Changes to climate and the way we live mean viruses like this one could find a way to the UK. But equally the next pandemic could be caused by a virus that has yet to be identified, what experts call Disease X."

**2:17 - 2:27**: A gloved hand (purple) is shown working with a liquid in a container, followed by a close-up of an automated lab machine processing samples. The audio concludes, "Dangers known and unknown. These scientists will be working to make sure we're prepared for both. Dominic Hughes, BBC News, Porton Down."

**2:27 - 2:40**: The female news anchor returns, seated at her desk. She says, "On that very issue of Disease X, I've been speaking to Professor Dame Jenny Harries. She's the chief executive of the UK Health Security Agency. And I asked her, how scientists prepare for a threat when they don't know what that threat is."

**2:40 - 2:51**: Dame Jenny Harries is seen on a video call, wearing glasses and a teal blazer. Behind her is a plant and a blue banner with the "UK Health Security Agency" logo and text "We are Impactful. We are Insightful. Inclusive." She responds, "So that is the challenge which I think you heard our Chief Scientific Officer Professor Isabel Oliver describe. And but there are a number of steps that we can take. We can be better prepared in terms of surveillance, and we're doing that work separately."

**2:51 - 3:17**: The screen splits, with Dame Jenny Harries on the left and footage of the lab on the right. She continues, "But what we do in the UKHSA labs at Porton Down, which is separate, I might say, from the Ministry of Defense facilities for chemical warfare, is get ourselves ready both to isolate the new pathogens, to develop assays so that we can test them accurately, and to work with other partners, with academics and with pharmaceutical companies..." The right screen shows scientists working, a computer display of cell cultures, a gloved hand with a microscope, and a close-up of a scientist looking through a microscope.

**3:17 - 3:45**: Dame Jenny Harries continues, "...to test out prototypes for vaccines and then be ready to evaluate them. And the really exciting thing about the legacy from the pandemic is we now have 2,800 square meters of laboratory space we didn't have before, and we're using that as part of our new strategy to ensure we're as best prepared as we can be for new pathogens and some existing ones." The right screen shows the multi-channel pipette, scientists working with vials, and a gloved hand using a pipette. The anchor then asks, "What would you say is the most important lesson that was learned from the pandemic?"

**3:45 - 5:27**: Dame Jenny Harries responds, "So I think I was going to say partnership was the immediate work that comes to mind, and that's obviously we see that when we work with our directors of public health for example, in responding to all sorts of infectious disease and chemical and radiological incidents. But actually it applies very much to working with industry as well. The vaccine task force was the group which originally supported this investment in laboratory space at Porton Down at the UKHSA site there. Um and it is that continuing work which we are planning to continue going forward so that we are ready working together as soon as a pathogen appears. And actually obviously beforehand, so we're prepping as fast as we can." The right screen shows an exterior shot of a "Vaccine Test Facility" building under construction, followed by a close-up of a lab machine with samples, then a wider shot of a scientist at a machine. The anchor then asks about the 100-day vaccine deployment target. Dame Jenny Harries replies, "So that is an ambition, so to get from an identified pathogen to a vaccine and and to therapeutics within 100 days. I mean to put it in context, that's about a third of the time that it took us to get a COVID vaccine into people's arms, and that was an outstanding record. Usually, it takes about five to ten years for a new vaccine to to come through. So it is a really challenging ambition and it won't work for all viruses and bacteria. Some are different shapes, some are more difficult to incorporate quickly, but I do think it is possible for many."

The video ends with Dame Jenny Harries speaking, highlighting the ambitious goal and the importance of preparedness and partnership in developing vaccines for both known and unknown future disease threats.

And that's endometrial cancer, all kinds of blood cancers, lymphatic cancers, breast cancers, from these shots and we know the mechanism. It's not a guess." She uses hand gestures to show "explosion."
*   **2:50-3:27:** She cites new data: "New data came out just in the last two weeks that if a person is injected, they're 8.12 times more likely to be infected with Omicron. Again, suppression of your immune system, suppression of your white blood cells, ablation of your toll-like receptors. The more shots you get, the more, the more you destroy your immune system and the faster that happens. And it's anticipated, the German data says that by the end of 2022, every fully vaccinated person over the age of 30 may have the equivalent of full-blown, vaccine-induced, immune-suppressed AIDS." The phrase "Vax-induced, immune-suppressed acquired" appears in the subtitles as she speaks it.
*   **3:28-3:34:** She concludes, "This is government data. From Germany, this came out about two weeks." She looks down at notes briefly.

summary: The video suggests myocarditis after Covid vaccination is common. There is no evidence to support that.     Myocarditis and pericarditis after COVID-19 vaccination are rare.    https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/vaccines/safety/myocarditis.html

caption.video0:
The video is split into two sections: a video clip at the top and a static image at the bottom.

**Top Section (Video):**
The top section shows a brightly lit room, likely a medical examination room. A woman with light brown hair, wearing a red long-sleeved shirt, is seated and holding a distressed baby in a plaid onesie. The baby is crying intensely, with a red face and open mouth. To the left of the woman and baby, another woman in a white lab coat and blue scrubs is wearing gloves and appears to be administering something to the baby's left thigh, possibly an injection, using a small white object (perhaps a gauze pad). To the right of the woman and baby, a third woman in a red shirt, also wearing gloves, is holding the baby's right leg. The focus is on the baby's distress and the medical procedure.

**Bottom Section (Static Image):**
The bottom section displays a medical diagram of a human heart. The heart is depicted in dark red and blue, showing the different chambers and blood vessels. An arrow points to the muscular wall of the heart, where a highlighted red area indicates inflammation. The diagram is labeled "Myocarditis" at the top right, with a subtitle below it reading "Inflammation of heart muscle."

**Audio (Synchronized with Video):**
The audio features a phone conversation between a male caller and a female who identifies herself as a pharmacist.
*   **0:00-0:03:** The male voice asks, "Yeah. Hi. Are you pharmacist?"
*   **0:03-0:04:** The female voice replies, "Yes, how can I help?"
*   **0:04-0:17:** The male caller states, "Yeah, hey, I've got a question. My wife, um, against my wishes, brought my son, seven-year-old son in there yesterday, or sorry, a few days ago, for a COVID jab, and he's now in the hospital with myocarditis." (During this time, the crying baby in the video is being attended to, reinforcing the narrative of a child receiving a vaccination and experiencing a medical issue).
*   **0:17-0:24:** The male continues, "Um, and I was, obviously not very happy with you guys, or with my wife. She told me that she..."
*   **0:24-0:31:** He then asks, "That was a potential side effect. So, why wouldn't you have told her that? Okay," His voice is becoming increasingly agitated.
*   **0:31-0:38:** The female pharmacist hesitates and says, "Okay, um... Sorry. Uh, so it's quite a real, um, side effect as well." (Her tone is slightly apologetic and hesitant).
*   **0:38-0:50:** The male voice interjects, "Well, no, it's not. No, it's not because I've been doing research. It's common. In the US, there's tens of thousands of them reported to the CDC site. So why are you not telling parents this?" (His voice is now clearly confrontational and angry).
*   **0:50-0:58:** The female pharmacist responds hesitantly, "Umm. Oh, we might scare the parents and they don't want to get their child vaccinated." (This response appears to be a justification for withholding information).
*   **0:58-1:18:** The male caller becomes enraged, yelling, "Yeah, so, so. You don't want to scare the parents with something that is actually happening, that's happened to my kid. Are you out of your mind? Are you out of your mind? You don't want to scare them. You need to give them the right information so they can make a proper decision. What is wrong with you? You don't want to scare them." (He is visibly furious, his voice high-pitched and loud, emphasizing his disbelief and anger at the reasoning provided).

The video is intended to highlight a perceived issue of withholding information about vaccine side effects, specifically myocarditis in children, using the imagery of a crying baby receiving an injection and a medical diagram of the condition. The audio provides the narrative and emotional context for this message.

caption.video0:
The video opens with a medium shot of a man in a grey suit and a green and blue striped tie, standing behind a podium. He has grey hair and is wearing glasses. He holds a stack of papers in his hands and is looking down at them as he speaks. A microphone is positioned in front of him. To the left of the screen, a water bottle and a cup are visible on the podium. In the bottom left corner, a circular logo with the word "DEBATE" is displayed. Below it, a ticker reads "7:19 PM PERTH." A red banner at the bottom of the screen displays scrolling text that begins: "LIVE FROM THE PERTH CONVENTION AND EXHIBITION CENTRE | AUSTRALIA'S MOS..."

As the video progresses, the man continues to speak, mostly looking down at his papers, occasionally glancing up at the audience.

**0:00-0:06** The man begins speaking: "Australia has administered more than 60 million doses of these vaccines..."
**0:06-0:12** "...to over 20 million people. Australia's governmental health authorities..." He looks up briefly at "million people," then back down.
**0:12-0:19** "...have chosen not to monitor new or emerging cancer trends following the widespread use of these vaccines."
**0:19-0:25** "It is troubling that the Department of Health has not made data on cancer trends post vaccination..."
**0:25-0:32** "...publicly available. It is their responsibility to do so. This is a critical gap..." He glances up for a moment at "publicly available," then back down.
**0:32-0:38** "...in public health oversight. Given the massive contamination levels of rogue residual DNA..." He looks up for "oversight" and then down.
**0:38-0:47** "...in the Australian vials, we must expect an ongoing rise in cancers and other genetic disorders in Australia." He looks up again as he says "in Australia," then down at his papers.
**0:47-0:55** "This issue is a potential long-term health crisis waiting to happen."
**0:55-1:01** "This matter must be taken seriously by every Australian." He looks up at "every Australian" and his gaze sweeps across the audience before returning to his papers.
**1:01-1:07** "We must advocate for immediate public health responses. We need our health authorities..."
**1:07-1:10** "...to begin monitoring these trends." He finishes the sentence, then shuffles his papers, putting one stack aside and picking up another. The audience begins to clap lightly, though it's not yet a full applause.
**1:10-1:19** The man adjusts his papers, looking down and seemingly preparing for the next part of his speech. He appears to take a deep breath. A cough is heard at 0:1:16.
**1:19-1:26** He looks up, appearing to gather himself, and then speaks: "Without immediate action, we risk leaving Australians vulnerable..."
**1:26-1:32** "...to a wave of preventable diseases." He glances up at "preventable diseases."
**1:32-1:39** "That may devastate families..." He looks down, then up briefly, and then back down again.
**1:39-1:46** "...and strain our healthcare system." He finishes his sentence, pauses, looks up at the audience, and then a slight smile forms on his face as the audience begins to applaud loudly.
**1:46-1:59** The camera cuts to a wide shot of the audience, who are now standing and applauding enthusiastically. Some people are looking towards the stage, smiling and clapping. The man's voice is faintly heard saying something indistinct over the applause.
**1:59-2:06** The camera cuts back to the man at the podium, who is now looking directly at the audience, speaking with a more emphatic tone: "These gene-based vaccines should never have been considered." The applause continues but starts to die down.
**2:06-2:18** "The technology had never been proven to be effective in preventing coronavirus infections. The technology had been shown to be unsafe long before it was used in 2021." He pauses again, looking directly at the audience with a serious expression, and the video ends.

Throughout the video, the scrolling text in the red banner at the bottom of the screen updates at different points:
*   **0:00 - 0:10:** "...AUSTRALIA'S MOST DISTINGUISHED POLITICIANS, DOCTORS, RE..."
*   **0:10 - 0:20:** "...TINGUISHED POLITICIANS, DOCTORS, RESEARCHERS AND LEGAL MINDS COME TOGETHER TO DISCUSS THE MIS-& DIS-INFORMATION SU..."
*   **0:20 - 0:30:** "...FORMATION SURROUNDING THE DISTURBING DISCOVERY OF FRIGHTENING AND ILLEGAL LEVELS OF DNA CONTA..."
*   **0:30 - 0:40:** "...NING AND ILLEGAL LEVELS OF DNA CONTAMINATION IN AUSTRALIA'S MRNA COVID-19 INJECTIONS..."
*   **0:40 - 2:18:** "...IN AUSTRALIA'S MRNA COVID-19 INJECTIONS | THE GREAT DEBATE LIVE FROM THE PERTH CONVENTION AND EXHIBITION CENTRE"
The time displayed in the bottom left corner changes from "7:19 PM PERTH" to "7:20 PM PERTH" around 0:08 and then to "7:21 PM PERTH" around 1:07.

caption.video0:
The video opens with an abstract, swirling visual of glowing, translucent shapes resembling cells or particles against a dark, textured background. A white, stylized "RR" logo with a triangular outline appears and disappears with glitch effects. Text fades in below the logo: "REESEREPORT.COM".

At 0:06, the screen transitions to a split view. On the left, a man identified as Ray Kurzweil is speaking, wearing glasses and a striped shirt. The year "2008" is superimposed on the left. On the right is a blurred image of a speaker and a neon lightbulb sign.
Ray Kurzweil states: "If you go out even to 2045, that's only, you know, four decades from now. Most of our intelligence, of our civil... of our human civilization will be non-biological. We're gonna put this inside our bodies and brains. So we're going to become machines, but not... And if you say that and people go, 'well I don't want to become a machine.' Because they're thinking of machines as we knew them from the 19th century, which were much lesser than humans. And machines today are still lesser than humans. I'm talking about a new type of machine that's actually greater, more subtle, more supple, more intelligent, more creative, more beautiful than humans."

At 0:57, a title card appears: "Robert A. Freitas Jr. Nanomedicine, Nanorobotics, Nanofactories, Molecular Assemblers and Machine-Phase Nanotechnology." Below it reads: "‘Ray Kurzweil and Robert Freitas discuss nanotechnology’ Kurzweil interviews Robert Freitas: Bonus footage from The Singularity is Near movie." The abstract background with glowing circles (resembling cells) is visible beneath the text.

At 1:04, a close-up microscopic view of small, glowing circular structures, possibly cells or particles, is shown.

At 1:07, the scene cuts back to Ray Kurzweil, now identified with the year "2010" on the left. He is interviewing another man, Robert A. Freitas Jr., who is wearing a dark suit and a red tie.
Kurzweil asks: "Medical nano robots really have the potential to extend human life more or less indefinitely. So what's your view about the role of death, and do we need death?"
Freitas responds: "Death is something that is an end. It's an end to life. It's an end to progress, it's an end to thoughts. It's something to be cured. Aging is a disease. It's a curable disease. Nanomedicine is the cure for that disease."

At 1:37, a microscopic view of numerous small, circular, gold-rimmed structures, resembling blood cells, is shown, some moving slightly.

At 1:45, Freitas continues: "I expect that by the time nano robots are deployed, which will be sometime, perhaps in the 2020s to the 2030s, we will have a whole set of laws in place. Regulations. There will be things you can and cannot do."

At 1:57, a document titled "United States Patent US 10,703,789 B2" is displayed, dated "Jul. 7, 2020." The applicant is "ModernaTX, Inc., Cambridge, MA." The patent is for "MODIFIED POLYNUCLEOTIDES FOR THE PRODUCTION OF SECRETED PROTEINS."

At 2:02, another part of the patent document is shown, titled "Nucleic Acid Self-Assembled Nanoparticles," with text describing their precise control and use as "cancer-targeting nanodelivery carrier[s]" and for "self-assembly of short DNA fragments and therapeutic siRNAs."

At 2:06, a blurred shot of a person in a lab coat, wearing safety glasses and a face mask, holds up a small vial labeled "COVID-19 Coronavirus Vaccine."
At 2:09, a close-up of a person wearing a surgical cap and gloves, looking through a microscope.
At 2:12, another microscopic view of small, irregularly shaped translucent particles moving in a fluid.
The narrator states: "The laws were never put in place, but the technology was patented and deployed to billions of people without their knowledge in 2020. We know this because several independent labs have confirmed the presence of this nanotechnology in the Covid vaccines."

At 2:15, Bill Gates is shown on a video call, wearing glasses and a blue sweater, gesturing with his hands.
Gates states: "Making the mRNA is really easy and really cheap. And that's the magic of this thing. But there's no doubt in the next five years, we can, you know, we just need to mess around. There's a lot of lipid nanoparticles, and some are very self-assembling."

At 2:34, a document titled "NRC-CNRC Self-Replication and Self-Assembly for Manufacturing *" is displayed. The abstract discusses "nanoscale machines floating in a virtual liquid" that "self-replicate and self-assemble."
The narrator states: "Self-assembly and self-replication seem to be the same technology when it comes to nano robots."

At 2:41, a document titled "Nanotechnology and the Gray Goo Scenario: Narratives of Doom?" is shown.
The narrator continues: "And this was considered to be the greatest danger involved with the use of this technology."

At 2:46, the scene returns to Kurzweil and Freitas.
Kurzweil states: "Self-replication causes disease."
Freitas: "Nano robots are inherently much stronger than biological systems, being built of diamondoid, so if, if they self replicate that 'disease,' quote unquote, could be even a tougher problem than biological disease."
Kurzweil asks: "So first of all, what's the feasibility of self-replication in the nanotechnology world?"
Freitas responds: "As a general principle, you do not want to put self-replicating nanobots inside the human body. I suppose not everybody agrees with me on that, but, that is the way I think that we can best guarantee safety. If the robots... nano robots are able to replicate inside the human body, that means they are using some component of the human body as food. And we don't want them to be doing that."

At 3:33, a book cover is shown: "Engines of Creation: The Coming Era of Nanotechnology" by K. Eric Drexler.
At 3:39, a page from the book is displayed, with a section titled "THE THREAT FROM THE MACHINES" and "THE GRAY GOO THREAT MAKES ONE THING PERFECTLY CLEAR: WE CANNOT AFFORD CERTAIN KINDS OF ACCIDENTS WITH REPLICATING ASSEMBLERS." The phrase "there is no reason to value gray goo merely because it could spread. Indeed, if we prevent it we will thereby prove our evolutionary superiority" is also highlighted.
The narrator states: "In his 1986 book, 'Engines of Creation,' Kim Eric Drexler wrote about what he termed the gray goo scenario, a hypothetical catastrophic event caused by out of control self-replicating nanotechnology, which consumes the biomass of the host."

At 3:53, a title card appears: "Humanity United Now - Ana Maria Mihalcea, MD, PhD Astonishing Darkfield Live Blood Footage Of Nano/Microrobots In C19 Unvaccinated Blood And What did Ray Kurzweil Say About Nanorobots And the Singularity?" Below it is a darkfield microscopic image of many small, bright, shimmering particles forming a large, irregular cluster, surrounded by more distinct circular shapes.
At 3:57, a gloved hand holds a glass vial filled with white/pinkish fibrous material submerged in a clear liquid.
At 4:00, a close-up of dark reddish-brown, twisted, fibrous material on a grey surface, with some thin, white strands.
At 4:02, another darkfield microscopic image of small, bright particles clustered together against a dark background with faint circular outlines.
The narrator states: "And this is exactly what independent researchers are finding. It explains the large, so-called blood clots being found in the dead. And it's spreading. Evidence shows that the vaxed are shedding this to the unvaxed."

At 4:07, the scene returns to Kurzweil and Freitas.
Kurzweil asks: "How do we prevent a terrorist or someone who's bent on destruction from creating such a self-replicating system?"
Freitas responds: "There will be some terrorist acts because that's what humans do, unfortunately. So what we're going to be needing in this era of nano factories is something equivalent to the fire department. Hopefully the incidents will be very few, but we will have an emergency regime which is set up to deal with that... that type of event."

At 4:37, a screen capture of a Bloomberg article headline: "More Than 12.7 Billion Shots Given: Covid-19 Tracker."
At 4:40, the microscopic image of glowing circular structures returns, with the Bloomberg headline partially visible in the background.
The narrator states: "But the event is worldwide and there is no agency set up to put out this fire. Our governments are not even discussing the problem."

At 4:46, a World Health Organization (WHO) logo is shown, with a headline: "WHO Member States agree to resume negotiations aimed at finalizing the world's first pandemic agreement."
At 4:49, a text slide: "DISEASE X is coming, and with it the next Global Pandemic." Below, text describes Disease X as a "probability rather than a possibility" that "could come from a million yet undiscovered viruses" and be "twenty times more deadly than coronavirus."

At 4:53, a row of several humanoid robots, some with exposed internal mechanisms, are seated at a long table, their hands placed on the surface. They appear to be in various stages of completion or design, with some resembling human busts. The background is a brightly lit exhibition space with large blue and red screens.
The narrator concludes: "And the perpetrators are planning a second round of nanobot deployment with another fake pandemic. If we the people cannot unite and stand together now, then what exactly are we? Reporting for Info Wars, this is Greg Reese."

The video ends with the stylized "RR" logo and triangular outline animating with colorful glitch effects and fading to black. Accompanying electronic music plays throughout the video, intensifying towards the end.

caption.video0:
The video begins at 00:00 inside a house, showing a plain white wall and a white electrical switch panel. The sound of a door creaking open can be heard.

At 00:01, a wooden door slowly opens to reveal a brightly lit suburban street on a sunny day. A female paramedic, identified by her dark blue short-sleeved uniform with insignia on the shoulders (one visible reads "Laura"), stands at the threshold, positioned just inside what appears to be the property line. She has blonde hair tied back in a ponytail and is wearing black boots and dark blue trousers with reflective strips at the cuffs. Her left arm is not clearly visible.

At 00:03, Laura is speaking. Behind her, another female paramedic (wearing a similar uniform, with "Sarah" on her name badge) walks into frame. Sarah has dark hair in a high bun and is holding a clipboard and pen, appearing to take notes. An "Emergency Ambulance" vehicle is parked on the street behind them. The house's exterior features light grey tiled pillars and a low brick fence.

From 00:04 to 00:08, the person filming (whose voice is heard but who remains unseen, except for their feet later in the video) asks Laura to stop recording, claiming she is on private property. Laura calmly refutes this, stating she is at the "front" (implying the property line or public access point). The unseen individual insists it's their property.

At 00:10, Laura explains to the unseen individual that she has just spoken with "Mallory from Headspace" (a mental health service) and that they are "really concerned about your welfare." She mentions "behaviors that you've displayed today in conjunction with information from Mallory and your dad."

At 00:25, Laura informs the individual, "that we're going to place you under a Section 20 under the Mental Health Act today." The individual immediately protests, stating, "I haven't done anything." Laura explains that their concern stems from "everything that's happened over the past month since you've been overseas."

At 00:36, the individual mentions having "an animal to take care of," a cat. Laura assures them that the cat can be fed by their dad. The individual becomes more agitated, saying, "I'm not interested. I will call a lawyer." Laura calmly responds, "That's okay."

At 00:53, the individual asserts that they have done "nothing" and simply "left Headspace 'cause I'm not interested in Headspace anymore." They accuse the paramedics of coming to their home and not explaining the situation. Laura reiterates that Headspace called them.

At 01:28, Laura mentions that Mallory described the individual as "very disengaging." The individual quickly interjects, "I'm not disengaging. I'm communicating." Laura mentions concerns about their interactions with their dad and their "overall functional wellbeing." The individual states they've been talking to their dad and are "fine." Laura counters that the dad reported not seeing them for a month and that they retreat when approached. The individual explains their dad works long hours and is often home late, suggesting they are asleep when he returns. Laura asks if the individual works, and they respond "No" because they "don't want to."

At 01:46, Laura repeats that the individual has been placed under a "Section 20 under the Mental Health Act." The individual vehemently refuses to be "dragged into the mental hospital." Laura explains they will initially go to "Westmead Emergency" for a medical clearance and assessment by mental health clinicians.

At 02:00, the individual calls the situation "inappropriate" and demands justification for placing them under Section 20. Laura cites "showing signs of paranoia." When asked why they are paranoid, Laura admits she isn't sure, which is why she wants to have a conversation. The individual accuses Laura of "dragging me out of my own comfort" and "telling me I'm misbehaving." Laura clarifies, "I'm not saying you're misbehaving, I'm saying your mental health has declined." She adds, "This is not your fault, it's a chemical imbalance." The individual maintains they are fine and have been taking their medication. Laura states they haven't had a new script since February.

At 03:13, the individual demands a warrant from the police to take them. Laura explains that under the Mental Health Act, as a registered paramedic, she is allowed to apprehend. The individual refuses to be touched and asserts their rights as a citizen. Laura states that her job is to look after them, and when they are not well, it's her duty. The individual denies Laura's right to take away their freedom.

At 03:40, Laura asks about the individual's overseas holiday, which happened a month or two ago. The individual is evasive about the destination. Laura tries to get more information, implying the details are relevant to their mental state. The individual expresses concern about being "kept in a mental hospital again for three, four weeks, up to a month" and having a cat to care for. Laura assures them their dad will care for the cat.

At 04:08, the individual becomes more accusatory, saying Laura knows "a lot about my life" and is "happy with engaging with me and asking a lot about my personal life." Laura responds that this is her "first time here today." The individual accuses her of "antagonizing" them and "returning" after they closed the door. Laura explains she came back for more information. She then reiterates the request for compliance to go to the hospital.

At 04:30, the individual demands police involvement. Laura states, "They are coming." She then asks if the individual wants to bring anything with them, like a phone charger or wallet.

At 04:47, the individual asks Laura what makes her think they are paranoid. Laura says it's because when she was standing there earlier, the individual was "looking behind me, as if there was somebody standing behind me." The individual says they are "not interested in talking."

At 05:22, Laura again tries to get information about the holiday, specifically the destination. The individual remains evasive about the country they visited, simply repeating "Overseas." Laura asks why they needed a "large sum of money" for this trip. The individual eventually reveals it was "about eight grand" and that they "ran out of money" during the trip. Laura then directly asks, "Did you buy drugs while you were overseas?" The individual emphatically states, "No, I did not."

At 06:12, Laura, now with a stern expression, asks the individual if they traveled with anyone or were alone. The individual pauses, then appears to decide not to answer. Laura then directly offers water, stating, "I have some water out here, you're welcome to." The individual replies, "You know what's funny? I wasn't offered water by the police when I was put into the mental hospital." They continue, "And now when you want to take me, now when you want to forcefully come to my home when I've finally found comfort in my life. When I've finally become mentally happy, when I'm finally just stable, you've decided to come in. You've decided to go, 'Hey, you know what? No, you're not. Go back to the now.'"

At 07:18, the individual gets up and walks away from the door, moving back into the house, and the camera pans down, following them, showing the paramedics still standing outside. The last few seconds of the video show Laura continuing to speak, appearing frustrated but determined, while Sarah continues to take notes. The person filming has closed the door.

The video ends at 07:53 with the door closing as the individual goes back inside, while the two paramedics remain outside. Laura is still speaking, looking concerned and trying to explain, and Sarah is still writing on her clipboard.The video begins at 00:00 inside a house, showing a plain white wall and a white electrical switch panel. The sound of a door creaking open can be heard.

At 00:01, a wooden door slowly opens to reveal a brightly lit suburban street on a sunny day. The individual accuses Laura of "dragging me out of my own comfort" and "telling me I'm misbehaving." Laura clarifies, "I'm not saying you're misbehaving, I'm saying your mental health has declined." She adds, "This is not your fault, it's a chemical imbalance." The individual maintains they are fine and have been taking their medication. Laura states they haven't had a new script since February.

At 03:13, the individual demands a warrant from the police to take them. Laura explains that under the Mental Health Act, as a registered paramedic, she is allowed to apprehend. The individual refuses to be touched and asserts their rights as a citizen. Laura states that her job is to look after them, and when they are not well, it's her duty. The individual denies Laura's right to take away their freedom.

At 03:40, Laura asks about the individual's overseas holiday, which happened a month or two ago. The individual is evasive about the destination. Laura tries to get more information, implying the details are relevant to their mental state. The individual expresses concern about being "kept in a mental hospital again for three, four weeks, up to a month" and having a cat to care for. Laura assures them their dad will care for the cat.

At 04:08, the individual becomes more accusatory, saying Laura knows "a lot about my life" and is "happy with engaging with me and asking a lot about my personal life." Laura responds that this is her "first time here today." The individual accuses her of "antagonizing" them and "returning" after they closed the door. Laura explains she came back for more information. She then reiterates the request for compliance to go to the hospital.

At 04:30, the individual demands police involvement. Laura states, "They are coming." She then asks if the individual wants to bring anything with them, like a phone charger or wallet.

At 04:47, the individual asks Laura what makes her think they are paranoid. Laura says it's because when she was standing there earlier, the individual was "looking behind me, as if there was somebody standing behind me." The individual says they are "not interested in talking."

At 05:22, Laura again tries to get information about the holiday, specifically the destination. The individual remains evasive about the country they visited, simply repeating "Overseas." Laura asks why they needed a "large sum of money" for this trip. The individual eventually reveals it was "about eight grand" and that they "ran out of money" during the trip. Laura then directly asks, "Did you buy drugs while you were overseas?" The individual emphatically states, "No, I did not."

At 06:12, Laura, now with a stern expression, asks the individual if they traveled with anyone or were alone. The individual pauses, then appears to decide not to answer. Laura then directly offers water, stating, "I have some water out here, you're welcome to." The individual replies, "You know what's funny? I wasn't offered water by the police when I was put into the mental hospital." They continue, "And now when you want to take me, now when you want to forcefully come to my home when I've finally found comfort in my life. When I've finally become mentally happy, when I'm finally just stable, you've decided to come in. You've decided to go, 'Hey, you know what? No, you're not. Go back to the now.'"

At 07:18, the individual gets up and walks away from the door, moving back into the house, and the camera pans down, following them, showing the paramedics still standing outside. The last few seconds of the video show Laura continuing to speak, appearing frustrated but determined, while Sarah continues to take notes. The person filming has closed the door.

The video ends at 07:53 with the door closing as the individual goes back inside, while the two paramedics remain outside. Laura is still speaking, looking concerned and trying to explain, and Sarah is still writing on her clipboard.

summary: Parasites in wild-caught salmon are normal and expected. The FDA and CDC note that wild-caught fish often carry anisakis worms. They’re killed by proper cooking (145°F) or freezing. The video opens with a wide shot of a congressional hearing room. The room features dark wood paneling on the walls and blue drapes. A large American flag stands prominently at the center back, flanked by two framed portraits on the wall. Numerous individuals are seated at desks arranged in a semi-circular fashion, with additional rows of seating behind them. In the foreground, several people are seated at a long table, some using laptops. A man on the far left of this table is actively typing on a laptop.

At 0:00, a male voice begins speaking, saying, "Okay, there's a lot to address here. A lot of things said. No facts given."

At 0:08, the camera zooms in on a man speaking at the foreground table. He is wearing a dark suit, a white shirt, and a red tie with white polka dots. He has an eyepatch over his right eye and is wearing glasses. A small pin is visible on his left lapel. A silver laptop with an Apple logo is positioned in front of him, and two microphones are on the table. A nameplate in front of him reads "MR. CRENSHAW TEXAS," and another to his right says "MR. PENCE INDIANA." A man in a light blue shirt and striped tie is partially visible to his left.

From 0:08 to 0:12, the man, Mr. Crenshaw, continues speaking: "A lot of things said. I've been called cruel multiple times. Um, so let's address that first. I am not the one who is cruel."

From 0:12 to 0:29, he elaborates, using hand gestures to emphasize his points. He occasionally glances down at notes to his left. He states, "Doing a double mastectomy on a 12-year-old girl, that's cruel. Castrating a 10-year-old boy, that's cruel. Putting them on permanent hormone therapy and puberty blockers that could prevent them from ever having children, permanently changing their physiology..."

Between 0:29 and 0:34, still in a close-up, he adds with strong emphasis, "There's a cruelty to that for sure. We are not the crazy ones here."

From 0:34 to 0:50, he continues, "All right, I've been really. Five minutes ago, none of y'all believed this. Let's be honest. Let's be honest. This has been called a manufactured culture war." He briefly shrugs at "really" and then resumes emphatic hand gestures.

Finally, from 0:50 to 1:23, he concludes his statement, pointing his finger to stress certain words: "So we're the ones that if it's a manufactured culture war, then why are we even debating it? Why do you care if it's banned? So it's not manufactured. We are responding to what is inherently a social contagion. In the UK, referrals for these kind of transitions went up by a factor of 33 between 2009 and 2018. In the US, in 2007, there was only one pediatric gender clinic that would do this. Now there's over 70. This has increased by 45,000% in many hospitals. We're not making this up. We are responding to it." He finishes his remarks, still in the close-up shot.

This video features a split screen. The top portion displays an animated diagram of human lungs, with a horizontal red line above them, marked with 'A' and 'B' at different points. The bottom portion shows a man with short brown hair and a beard, wearing a light grey t-shirt, looking directly at the camera.

The video begins with the text overlay "If you can hold your breath from A to B, your lungs are good ✅💪" at the top of the screen.

**From 0:00 to 0:02**, the word "Inhale" appears above point A on the red line. A red dot moves from the left end of the red line towards point A, stopping at A. Simultaneously, the animated lungs expand slightly. The man in the bottom screen has a neutral expression, looking at the camera. We hear a soft, consistent heartbeat sound in the audio, along with what sounds like deep inhaling.

**From 0:02 to 0:03**, the word "Inhale" disappears, and a red box with "hold your breath" inside it appears, centered above the lungs, between points A and B. The red dot on the line remains at point A. The lungs remain expanded. The man still has a neutral expression. The inhaling sound stops, and the heartbeat continues steadily.

**From 0:03 to 0:31**, the red dot slowly moves from point A towards point B along the red line. The lungs remain expanded, and the text "hold your breath" stays visible. The man's expression gradually changes. He starts with a neutral smile, then looks up slightly, his eyebrows raising, and a wider smile spreads across his face, indicating a sense of challenge or amusement. The heartbeat continues at a steady pace.

**From 0:31 to 0:34**, the red dot reaches and passes point B, moving to the right end of the red line. The text "hold your breath" disappears, and "exhale" appears briefly above point B. The animated lungs quickly deflate. The man takes a deep breath in and then exhales with a relieved expression, his eyes closing slightly and then reopening with a smile. The heartbeat sound remains consistent throughout this final segment, with a distinct exhale sound heard as the man exhales.

caption.video0:
The video captures Barack Obama delivering a speech at a podium adorned with a blue sign that reads "VOTE FOR FREEDOM" in large white letters, with "MILWAUKEE, WI" and "HARRIS WALZ" in smaller text below. Obama, with his signature grey hair and wearing a light blue button-up shirt, stands behind the podium, speaking into two microphones. The background is blurred, showing a crowd of people.

Obama begins by saying, "Let's talk about health care." (0:00-0:02) He points his finger, looking serious, and continues, "If you ask Donald Trump what he's going to do to make health care more affordable, he's got one answer—and that is end, repeal, the Affordable Care Act." (0:03-0:16) The crowd reacts with a mix of cheers and groans.

He then points out, "And he's not by himself. The other day, the Speaker of the House, the Republican Speaker of the House, Mike Johnson, said there would be, quote, 'No Obamacare' if Trump wins." (0:16-0:27)

At this point, some members of the audience start to boo. Obama immediately interjects, "Now, do not boo—vote." (0:27-0:31) The booing quickly turns into louder cheers at his suggestion to vote.

Obama smiles and throws his hands up in a gesture of exasperation as he says, "Now, neither of them can really explain why they want to end it, except for the fact that I did it." (0:31-0:40) This comment elicits loud laughter and applause from the crowd.

He then becomes more emphatic, stating, "The problem they have is, guess what? The Affordable Care Act works." (0:40-0:48) The audience cheers. He continues, raising his voice and gesturing broadly, "Almost 50 million people have gotten health care because of it. Another 50 million had the security of knowing insurance companies can't deny them coverage because of a preexisting condition." (0:48-1:01) The crowd roars with applause and cheers.

Obama beams at the crowd, raising his hands in a celebratory gesture, saying, "The more people benefit, the more popular it has become." (1:01-1:06) He then brings his hand to his mouth, making a humorous munching gesture, as if eating popcorn while watching the political drama unfold. (1:09-1:11)

He shifts his focus, saying, "Which is why a couple weeks ago, some of y'all saw this, Trump's running mate, Mr. Vance—" (1:11-1:19) The crowd begins to boo again, but Obama quickly corrects them, "do not boo, vote—" (1:19-1:21) He pauses for a moment as the crowd murmurs.

Obama resumes, "Mr. Vance had the nerve to say Donald Trump, quote, 'salvaged the Affordable Care Act.'" (1:22-1:29) Obama looks around with a look of humorous disbelief, mouthing "What?" (1:30-1:36) The audience laughs.

With a wide smile and a wave of his hand, Obama exclaims, "Donald Trump spent his entire presidency trying to repeal the Affordable Care Act, trying to tear it down. And he couldn't even do that right." (1:36-1:46) The crowd erupts in laughter and cheers.

Finally, Obama concludes, "And now, eight years after he was elected, he was asked, well, if you did repeal it, what would you replace it with? And he looked, and he kind of fumbled around, and then he said, 'well, I've got "concepts of a plan" for how to replace it.'" (1:46-2:04) Obama uses fumbling hand gestures to mimic Trump's hesitation and a dismissive hand wave for the "concepts of a plan," drawing more laughter from the audience.
