The video features two speakers, Senator Bernie Sanders and Robert F. Kennedy, Jr., at what appears to be a congressional hearing or public forum, discussing the topic of vaccines and corruption. The video includes a "Bernie Sanders U.S. Senator for Vermont" logo in the top right corner throughout the speaking portions, and ends with a full-screen logo for Bernie Sanders.

Here is a detailed breakdown of the video's content:

0:00 - 0:01: The video opens with a shot of a well-lit, long hallway with light-colored walls and ceiling. A line of people is visible on the right, some of whom appear to be security personnel in dark uniforms engaged in conversation. On the left side of the hallway, a man in a dark suit walks forward, followed by other individuals. A blue wheelchair accessibility sign is visible on the wall. Faint, indistinct murmuring sounds are heard.

0:01 - 0:04: The scene cuts to a close-up of Robert F. Kennedy, Jr., seated at a wooden table. He has gray hair, is wearing a dark suit, a light blue collared shirt, and a patterned tie. He is initially looking down and to his right, holding a pair of glasses in his left hand. A microphone is positioned in front of him. A placard on the table identifies him as "The Honorable Robert F. Kennedy, Jr." He then lifts his head and puts on his glasses. Bernie Sanders' voice begins, "You know, what I find a little bit weird in this discussion..."

0:04 - 0:10: The video cuts to Senator Bernie Sanders, who has white hair, is wearing glasses, a dark suit, and a blue and gray striped tie. He is seated at a wooden table with a microphone in front of him, looking directly at the camera with a serious expression. A name placard in front of him reads "MS. SMITH," and another partially visible one to his right indicates "MR. SA." He uses his right hand to gesture while speaking. He continues, "...and I'm hearing it over and over again this morning, Mr. Chairman, is we've got the entire medical community on one side."

0:10 - 0:22: Bernie Sanders continues his speech, gesturing emphatically with his left hand, palm open. His expression remains intense and focused. Another man wearing glasses is visible to his left, looking straight ahead, listening. Bernie Sanders states, "You got the American Medical Association (AMA) representing hundreds of thousands of doctors, the American Academy of Pediatrics, the American Public Health Association,"

0:22 - 0:36: Sanders continues to speak, making a "hands open" gesture with both hands for emphasis, then points with his left index finger. The man to his left maintains his focused gaze. Sanders says, "all of these organizations are telling us that the Covid vaccine and vaccines in general are safe and effective. You are casting doubt on that."

0:36 - 0:45: Sanders leans forward slightly, gesturing again with his left hand, specifically pointing with his index finger, reinforcing his point. His expression is very direct. He asks, "Who are your scientific Who are the organizations that are agreeing with you and casting aspersions on vaccines?"

0:45 - 0:57: The scene cuts back to Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. He is now looking slightly up and to his right, speaking into the microphone, and gesturing with his right hand. He has a serious expression. He responds, "The uh— the primary advisers are Marty Makary, Jay Bhattacharya, Dr. Oz—" Bernie Sanders then interjects, "So, you've got a few doctors who agree with you."

0:57 - 1:01: The video cuts back to Bernie Sanders. He is raising his left hand, palm facing forward, as if to interrupt the other speaker, then points with his index finger. He states, "Not a few doctors. What you're talking about,"

1:01 - 1:10: Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. is shown again, speaking animatedly. He forms a fist with his right hand, then gestures to his right, his eyes wide. He clarifies, "is there's a big difference, Senator, between established science and the scientific establishment, which has been co-opted by the pharmaceutical industry."

1:10 - 1:24: Bernie Sanders is back on screen, pointing his right index finger, then raising his hand and shaking it as he speaks with a raised voice. The man to his left is still visible, observing. Sanders challenges, "Hold on. So, you're telling me, you're telling the American people that the American Medical Association, representing hundreds of thousands of people, have been co-opted and that they should not trust their doctors. And the American Academy of Pediatrics—"

1:24 - 1:35: Sanders continues his passionate address, pointing with his right index finger, then raising both hands, then pointing again. He adds, "And by the way, just for the record, every single Republican, and I don't mean to be political here, Mr. Chairman, has received PAC money from the pharmaceutical industry. Are they all corrupt as well?"

1:35 - 1:45: The scene cuts back to Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. He takes a sip from a white coffee cup, looking towards Bernie Sanders, then listens with a slight frown. He says, "And I'm telling you, the American Heart Association has been corrupted by the food industry." Bernie Sanders then retorts, his voice rising, "Everybody but you, sir. But you know what, when you ran for President— You know, we have a corrupt campaign finance system."

1:45 - 2:07: Bernie Sanders is shown still speaking directly at the camera, pointing vigorously with his right index finger, then raising his hand for emphasis. He continues, "Maybe you will agree with me on that. Okay, you were running for President. You got a billionaire behind you. You received $300,000 from people, not from the industry. 2:07 - 2:11: The scene cuts back to Robert F. Kennedy, Jr., who is looking to his left, presumably at Bernie Sanders, with a contemplative, somewhat resigned expression. Bernie Sanders concludes his statement, "...but you? Is that what we're looking at? I don't think so."

The man in the center, Dr. Martin Makary, is speaking into a microphone. He is wearing a dark suit with a striped tie. To his left is Dr. Martin Kulldorff, and to his right is Dr. Jay Bhattacharya. The fourth man is not fully visible. In the background, there are several individuals seated, including a woman in a grey top and another in a patterned white shirt.

From 00:00 to 00:06, Dr. Martin Makary states, "The greatest perpetrator of misinformation during the pandemic has been the United States government."

Between 00:06 and 00:10, he continues, "Misinformation that COVID was spread through surface transmission." During this time, Dr. Makary is looking directly ahead, emphasizing his point with hand gestures.

From 00:10 to 00:15, he adds, "That vaccinated immunity was far greater than natural immunity." He maintains eye contact with the audience and continues to use his hands to illustrate his speech.

At 00:15, Dr. Makary proclaims, "That masks were effective." He pauses briefly, looking down at his notes, then continues.

From 00:15 to 00:27, he states, "Now we have the definitive Cochrane review. What do you do with that review? Cochrane is the most authoritative evidence body in all of medicine and has been for decades. Do you just ignore it? Not talk about it?" During this segment, Dr. Makary raises his hands in a questioning gesture, seeking an answer to his rhetorical question.

Between 00:29 and 00:33, Dr. Makary claims, "That myocarditis was more common after the infection than the vaccine." He gestures with his hands to underscore this assertion.

From 00:33 to 00:39, he refutes, "Not true. It's 4 to 28 times more common after the vaccine." He looks serious and engaged.

At 00:39, Dr. Makary mentions, "That young people benefit from a booster." He gestures with his hand as if to dismiss the claim.

From 00:40 to 00:54, he states, "Misinformation, our two top experts on vaccines quit the FDA in protest over this particular issue, pushing boosters in young, healthy people. The data was never there." During this part of his speech, Dr. Makary appears to be making a strong, emphatic point.

Between 00:54 and 01:03, he continues, "That's why the CDC never disclosed hospitalization rates among boosted Americans under age 50." He gestures with his right hand while making this point.

From 01:03 to 01:10, Dr. Makary says, "The vaccine mandates would increase vaccination rates. The George Mason University study shows it didn't." He shakes his head slightly to emphasize his disagreement.

At 01:10, he looks down, then up at the audience, stating, "It did one thing."

Between 01:11 and 01:21, he explains, "It created never-vaxxers. Over and over again, we've seen something that goes far beyond using your best judgment with the information at hand. We've seen something..." He looks down at his notes again, then back up.

From 01:22 to 01:29, Dr. Makary concludes, "...which is unforgivable, and that is the weaponization of medical research itself." He gestures with his hand to emphasize the gravity of his statement.

At 01:29, he states, "The CDC putting out their own shoddy studies."

Between 01:30 and 01:37, Dr. Makary explains, "Like their own study on natural immunity, looking at one state for two months." He uses hand gestures to clarify his points.

From 01:37 to 01:57, he adds, "When they had data for years on all 50 states, why did they only report that one sliver of data? Why did they salami-slice the giant database? Because it gave them the result they wanted. Same with masking study. Well, the data has now caught up in giant systematic reviews, and public health officials were intellectually dishonest." He ends this segment with a direct and critical tone.

At 01:57, Dr. Makary states, "They lied to the American people. Thank you." He finishes his statement, looking at the audience, and then looks down at his notes as someone walks by in the background.

The video features an interview or podcast with a man (let's call him Man 1, who is Robert F. Kennedy Jr.) speaking passionately about vaccine effectiveness, interspersed with reactions from two hosts (a man, Man 2, and a woman, Woman 1). The setting for Man 1 is a dark room with a black wall and a large, black and white circular design in the background. He is seated on a black leather couch, wearing a blue collared shirt, and speaks into a microphone on a stand. The setting for Man 2 and Woman 1 is in front of a large American flag backdrop. They are both wearing headphones and seated at a table with microphones.

Here's a detailed breakdown:

*   **00:00-00:01**: Man 1 begins speaking, gesturing with his right hand while saying, "They said, what they're saying out of 22,000 people there was one COVID death..." He looks slightly to his right.
*   **00:01-00:03**: The scene cuts to Man 2 and Woman 1. Man 1's voice continues, "...among the vaccinated and there was two COVID deaths among the unvaccinated, 22,000 people." Man 2, wearing glasses and a black jacket over a graphic tee, listens intently, resting his chin on his hand. Woman 1, wearing glasses and a striped sweater, also listens, looking straight ahead with a laptop in front of her. An American flag with stars on the left and red and white stripes on the right forms the background.
*   **00:03-00:15**: The camera returns to Man 1, who explains, "So and that that allowed them to claim it's 100% effective." He gestures with both hands to emphasize his point.
*   **00:15-00:17**: The scene switches back to Man 2 and Woman 1. Man 2 interjects, "Effective for what? You can't use the word effective." He gestures with his right hand as he speaks.
*   **00:17-00:21**: Man 1 is shown again, vehemently responding, "No, no, you remember they kept saying it's 100% effective." He extends his right hand with an open palm.
*   **00:21-00:23**: Man 2 is back on screen, disagreeing, "No, they just said it it'll negate the severity." He makes a small gesture with his right hand.
*   **00:23-00:27**: Man 1 refutes, "I can tell you if you go on the internet, you can see Tony Fauci..." He gestures with his right hand, pointing.
*   **00:27-00:29**: Man 2 interrupts, "But the word effective didn't say you won't I never heard you won't get COVID." He uses his hands to make his point.
*   **00:29-00:48**: Man 1 responds, "Oh yeah, well this is a different subject but yeah, in fact I saw a tape yesterday of them all of Biden, Fauci, Gates, uh, uh, you know, Burla, all of them saying if you take the vaccine, Rachel Maddow, if you take the vaccine, you can't get COVID, you can't pass COVID." He becomes more animated, gesturing broadly with both hands as he lists the names and makes his argument.
*   **00:48-00:50**: Man 2 replies, "I didn't see that." He looks away slightly.
*   **00:50-00:53**: Man 1 then asks, "Okay, well, you know what? Can I play it for you?" He pulls out a smartphone and holds it up in his left hand.
*   **00:53-00:57**: The final shot shows Man 2 and Woman 1 again. Man 2 responds to Man 1's offer, "Well, well yeah, but here it doesn't matter what Rachel Maddow says." He gestures with his hands as Woman 1 continues to listen attentively.

caption.video0:
The video features two men, alternating speakers, in what appears to be a podcast setting.

**00:00 - 01:42**: A man with short, salt-and-pepper hair, wearing black over-ear headphones, a dark navy zip-up cardigan over a light-colored collared shirt, is speaking into a black microphone. He has a serious expression and frequently uses hand gestures to emphasize his points. The background behind him is a dark red, pleated curtain. To his left, a golden, decorative skull sits on the table.
The man begins by explaining the business model of the drug industry, stating, "it is to get as many people taking as many drugs as possible, for as long as possible." He recounts an event in 2018 where he was asked by the BMJ (British Medical Journal) to debate at the Cambridge University Union with AstraZeneca. He debated with the CEO of AstraZeneca, and the motion they put forward was, "we need more people taking more drugs." He reiterates, "That was their motion." He then tells Joe that people need to understand "what we're up against here" and that this business model is not a solution for good health. He points out that an "overmedicated population now is a big, it's a public health crisis," even pre-pandemic. He references an estimate from Peter Gøtzsche, a co-founder of the Cochrane Collaboration, published in the BMJ, which suggests that "the third most common cause of death now globally, after heart disease and cancer, is prescribed medications." He clarifies that this is "mainly because of avoidable side effects" and that these side effects are avoidable because "the decision-making in the prescription often doesn't involve informed consent." He explains that if people were told the "full benefits and harms in absolute terms of drugs," they would "mostly... be more conservative" and "less likely to take the pills." He concludes by stating that "the information that doctors are using to make clinical decisions are based upon these industry-sponsored trials where they keep their data commercially confidential, which ultimately means that the safety and the benefits are grossly exaggerated."

**01:42 - 02:00**: The video cuts to Joe Rogan, a bald man with a beard, wearing black over-ear headphones and a black hoodie. He is also speaking into a black microphone, looking down as he talks. Behind him is a wooden wall decorated with a large neon sign that reads "THE JOE ROGAN EXPERIENCE" in yellow and red lights.
Joe says, "Yeah, John Abramson explained to me how peer review is done on trials that are coming straight from pharmaceutical drug companies. That you don't really get access to the data itself, you get access to the pharmaceutical company's analysis of that data."

**02:00 - 02:03**: The video cuts back to the first man, who confirms, "Yes, that's absolutely true. So, so..." Joe Rogan then interjects, "That sounds insane." The first man responds, "It is, completely insane."

claim_or_not:
Cleaned text: “The third most common cause of death now globally, after heart disease and cancer, is prescribed medications.”

The tweet expresses a claim about a healthcare-related topic.

Yes.

**Russell Brand (00:00):** So what you're saying, Dave, is that at the inception of the WHO, they granted themselves somehow amnesty. And because it was a standard of care during an emergency, you could kill people. This video shows military personnel establishing a temporary medical facility, likely at Shifa Hospital, as indicated by the on-screen text. The audio is a continuous low-frequency hum throughout the video, possibly from electrical equipment or ambient background noise, with no discernible speech or music.

Here's a detailed breakdown of the video content:

*   **00:00-00:04:** The scene opens with a soldier, whose face is blurred for anonymity, wearing military tactical gear and a helmet with a night vision mount. He is standing in what appears to be a hospital room. The room has white walls, an air conditioning unit mounted high on the wall, and a barred window on the right. An IV pole with two bags hanging from it is visible on the left. The soldier is holding a plaid blanket (blue, white, yellow, and grey) and preparing to place it on a bed. The bed has a light blue sheet and a pink pillow. The text overlay reads: "Establishing Temporary Infrastructure for Medical Treatment at Shifa Hospital."

*   **00:04-00:09:** The camera angle shifts slightly, showing the soldier carefully spreading the plaid blanket onto the bed. To the right of the bed, a white plastic cabinet holds several pieces of medical equipment, including what look like portable monitors or defibrillators with numerous wires. A wooden desk with a blue bag and some wires is visible on the far left. The room appears somewhat spartan and possibly in a state of disrepair, with some scuffs or marks on the walls.

*   **00:09-00:11:** The shot changes to a close-up of a soldier's gloved hands. He is connecting wires or tubes to a blue medical device, possibly an oxygen concentrator or a portable ventilator, which is sitting on a surface near the barred window. Sunlight streams in from the window.

*   **00:11-00:15:** Another close-up shows the soldier, still wearing tactical gear and a helmet (partially visible), attaching two IV bags to an IV stand. He carefully hangs the first bag, then the second, onto the hooks of the stainless steel stand. The IV bags contain clear liquid and have labels, but the text is not legible.

*   **00:15-00:22:** The video concludes with a wide shot of the room, which now appears to be largely set up as a temporary ward. There are multiple cot-like beds arranged around the room.
    *   On the left, there's a bed with a pink sheet, and another with a green sheet covered by a plaid blanket (the one seen being spread earlier).
    *   In the middle, two beds are covered with red sheets. One has a light blue blanket and a pink pillow, while the other has a plaid blanket and a blue medical device on it.
    *   Another bed with a light pink sheet is partially visible on the far right.
    *   Several IV stands are placed next to the beds, some with IV bags hanging. The walls show signs of wear and tear, and the floor is concrete. The overall impression is a functional, temporary medical setup in a basic environment.

    *   **Text Overlay:** "THE ANSWER IS EASY. NO." (0:09-0:10), "NO MATTER WHAT STATE YOU LIVE IN," (0:11-0:12), "YOU SHOULD BE VOTING FOR DONALD TRUMP." (0:13-0:14)

*   **00:14 - 00:20 (RFK Jr. Speaking):**
    *   **Visual:** RFK Jr. continues speaking, gesturing with both hands.
    *   **Audio:** RFK Jr. says, "And let me tell you why. That's the only way that we can get me and everything I stand for into Washington, D.C., and fulfill the mission that motivated my campaign."
    *   **Text Overlay:** "AND LET ME TELL YOU WHY." (0:15), "THAT’S THE ONLY WAY THAT WE CAN GET ME AND EVERYTHING I STAND FOR" (0:16-0:19)

A child in a cowboy hat sits on a man's shoulders, holding a red sign (partially visible, appears to be a "Kennedy 24" sign). A stage is set up with American flags and a large banner displaying "KENNEDY 24 DECLARE YOUR INDEPENDENCE." Large screens on either side of the stage show a speaker.

    *   **Text Overlay:** "RIGHT NOW, FREE SPEECH IS UNDER WITHERING AND RELENTLESS ATTACK IN AMERICA AND ACROSS THE GLOBE. BUT AT LEAST WE STILL HAVE ENOUGH OF IT HERE THAT YOU CAN WATCH THIS VIDEO." (0:24-0:34)

*   **00:34 - 00:37 (Rally Scene):**

This transitions to a live-action shot of Trump, also in a suit and red tie, walking through a hallway, flanked by what appear to be law enforcement officers and other men. He maintains a serious expression.

summary: In male-to-female vaginoplasty surgery, a neo-vagina is created where no such cavity previously exists. As such, it is packed with gauze while healing so the cavity doesn't collapse. caption.video0:
The video opens with a medium shot of a woman speaking into a microphone, surrounded by people, some in uniform.

**Visual Description:**
The woman, identified as "अंबिका शुक्ला एनिमल एक्टिविस्ट" (Ambika Shukla Animal Activist) by a text overlay at the bottom of the screen, has shoulder-length brown hair and is wearing a light yellow and white striped kurta with a matching dupatta. She has large, distinct earrings. She is holding a red microphone with the text "THE RED MIKE" on it. The background shows a busy outdoor setting with many people, including police personnel in uniform, and trees. The overall atmosphere appears to be a public gathering or protest.

**Text Overlays:**
*   **0:00 - 0:40:** At the top of the screen, a red banner displays the Hindi text: "'रेबीज़ एक हल्का वायरस है, साबुन से धोने पर वायरस मर जाता है'" (Rabies is a mild virus, washing with soap kills the virus). A small red logo with a white microphone and "लाल माइक" (Lal Mike) in Hindi is in the top right corner.
*   **0:00 - 0:02:** Below the woman, the Hindi text "रेबीज़ जो वायरस" (Rabies, the virus) appears.
*   **0:04 - 0:07:** This changes to "इन्फेक्शन, सलाइवा खून तक पहुंचती है" (Infection, saliva reaches the blood).
*   **0:08 - 0:09:** This changes to "बस इसका माध्यम ये है" (This is just its medium).
*   **0:10 - 0:11:** This changes to "मगर ये इतनी डेलिकेट वायरस है" (But it is such a delicate virus).
*   **0:11 - 0:14:** This changes to "कि अगर आप साबुन से भी ज़ख्म को धो दो" (that even if you wash the wound with soap).
*   **0:15 - 0:16:** This changes to "तो रेबीज़ वायरस मर जाती है" (then the rabies virus dies).
*   **0:17 - 0:20:** This changes to "इसीलिए आप देखेंगे हमारे देश में जहां डेढ़" (That's why you will see in our country where one and a half).
*   **0:20 - 0:22:** This changes to "बिलियन लोग हैं" (billion people are there).
*   **0:22 - 0:24:** English text "ONE AND A HALF BILLION PEOPLE" appears.
*   **0:25 - 0:26:** This changes to "रेबीज़ का तादाद क्या है?" (What is the number of rabies cases?).
*   **0:27 - 0:28:** This changes to "54 बस, क्यों इतना कम है" (Only 54, why is it so less).
*   **0:29 - 0:39:** This changes to "क्योंकि एक तो रेबीज़ बहुत RARE DISEASE है दूसरी फैलती नहीं है आसानी से और सच में कुत्ते काटते नहीं हैं जितना बनाया जा रहा है" (Because firstly, rabies is a very RARE DISEASE, secondly, it doesn't spread easily, and truthfully, dogs don't bite as much as it's made out to be).

**Audio Transcription and Translation (with synchronization):**

*   **0:00 - 0:05:** (Woman speaking) "रेबीज़ जो वायरस फैलती है वो केवल जब इंफेक्शन, सलाइवा खून तक पहुंचती है"
    *   (Translation) "The rabies virus, when it spreads, it only spreads when the infection, saliva, reaches the blood."
*   **0:07 - 0:10:** (Woman speaking) "बस इसका माध्यम ये है मगर ये इतनी डेलिकेट वायरस है"

*   **0:11 - 0:16:** (Woman speaking) "कि अगर आप साबुन से भी ज़ख्म को धो दो तो रेबीज़ वायरस मर जाती है।"
    *   (Translation) "that even if you wash the wound with soap, the rabies virus dies."
*   **0:17 - 0:28:** (Woman speaking) "इसीलिए आप देखेंगे हमारे देश में जहां डेढ़ बिलियन लोग हैं, हा, वन एंड अ हाफ बिलियन पीपल रेबीज़ का तादाद क्या है? 54 बस, क्यों इतना कम है?"
    *   (Translation) "That's why you will see in our country where one and a half billion people are there, huh, one and a half billion people, what is the number of rabies cases? Only 54. Why is it so less?"
*   **0:29 - 0:39:** (Woman speaking) "क्योंकि एक तो रेबीज़ बहुत रेयर डिजीज है दूसरी फैलती नहीं है आसानी से और सच में कुत्ते काटते नहीं हैं जितना बनाया जा रहा है।"
    *   (Translation) "Because firstly, rabies is a very rare disease, secondly, it doesn't spread easily, and truthfully, dogs don't bite as much as it's made out to be."

The video concludes with a white screen displaying a red "R" logo with a white microphone and the text "THE RED MIKE" and "लाल माइक" in Hindi.The video features Ambika Shukla, an animal activist, being interviewed outdoors. She is seen from the chest up, wearing a light yellow and white striped kurta and a matching dupatta, with her brown hair styled to her shoulders. She has distinctive, large earrings. She speaks directly into a red microphone bearing "THE RED MIKE" logo. In the background, there is a bustling public area with many people, including police officers in uniform, and trees.

Throughout the interview, Hindi text overlays appear at the bottom of the screen, identifying the speaker as "अंबिका शुक्ला एनिमल एक्टिविस्ट" (Ambika Shukla Animal Activist). A red banner at the top of the screen displays a key message in Hindi: "'रेबीज़ एक हल्का वायरस है, साबुन से धोने पर वायरस मर जाता है'" (Rabies is a mild virus, washing with soap kills the virus), along with a small red logo featuring a microphone and "लाल माइक" (Lal Mike) in the top right corner.

**Synchronized Description of Audio and Visuals:**

*   **0:00 - 0:02:** Ambika Shukla begins speaking, with the text "रेबीज़ जो वायरस" (Rabies, the virus) appearing below her. She states that the rabies virus spreads only when the infection, specifically saliva, reaches the blood.
*   **0:04 - 0:07:** The text changes to "इन्फेक्शन, सलाइवा खून तक पहुंचती है" (Infection, saliva reaches the blood) as she continues to explain the transmission route.
*   **0:08 - 0:09:** She emphasizes that "बस इसका माध्यम ये है" (This is just its medium), referring to the transmission method.
*   **0:10 - 0:11:** She further describes rabies as a "मगर ये इतनी डेलिकेट वायरस है" (But it is such a delicate virus).
*   **0:11 - 0:14:** She explains that "कि अगर आप साबुन से भी ज़ख्म को धो दो" (that even if you wash the wound with soap).
*   **0:15 - 0:16:** She concludes this point by saying "तो रेबीज़ वायरस मर जाती है" (then the rabies virus dies).
*   **0:17 - 0:22:** She then highlights the low incidence of rabies in India, stating, "इसीलिए आप देखेंगे हमारे देश में जहां डेढ़ बिलियन लोग हैं" (That's why you will see in our country where one and a half billion people are there), while the text "बिलियन लोग हैं" (billion people are there) is shown.
*   **0:22 - 0:24:** An English text overlay appears: "ONE AND A HALF BILLION PEOPLE," reiterating the large population figure.
*   **0:25 - 0:26:** She then asks, "रेबीज़ का तादाद क्या है?" (What is the number of rabies cases?).
*   **0:27 - 0:28:** She answers her own question, "54 बस, क्यों इतना कम है?" (Only 54. Why is it so less?).
*   **0:29 - 0:39:** She provides reasons for the low numbers: "क्योंकि एक तो रेबीज़ बहुत RARE DISEASE है दूसरी फैलती नहीं है आसानी से और सच में कुत्ते काटते नहीं हैं जितना बनाया जा रहा है" (Because firstly, rabies is a very RARE DISEASE, secondly, it doesn't spread easily, and truthfully, dogs don't bite as much as it's made out to be). The final part of her statement about dogs biting is met with agreement from someone off-screen.

The video then transitions to a white screen displaying a red "R" logo with a white microphone and the text "THE RED MIKE" and "लाल माइक" in Hindi.

caption.video0:
The video features a man with short, light-colored hair, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and dark tie, standing behind a wooden podium. The podium bears the inscription "HILLSDALE COLLEGE Pursuing Truth • Defending Liberty Since 1844." To the speaker's left is an American flag, and to his right is a blue banner with a gold seal (likely the Michigan state seal). The background is a dark blue curtain. A white heart icon with "83.7K" next to it is visible in the bottom right corner, indicating likes.

Here's a detailed breakdown:

*   **0:00-0:05:** The man speaks passionately, gesturing with both hands open, palms up, then points his right index finger forward, emphasizing his words. He states, "I was saying not one of the 72 vaccines mandated for children has ever been safety tested..."
*   **0:05-0:10:** He nods slightly while resting his hands on the podium, continuing, "...in pre-licensing placebo-controlled trials. Not one."
*   **0:10-0:15:** He raises his right hand, palm open, then brings it back down to the podium, shaking his head. He narrates, "Tony Fauci was saying he's lying."
*   **0:15-0:20:** He points his right index finger forward once more, then gestures with open palms as if explaining. He recalls, "In 2016, President Trump appointed me to run a vaccine safety commission."
*   **0:20-0:25:** He makes a circular motion with his right index finger, then points towards his right, before resting his hands. He continues, "And he ordered Fauci and Collins to meet with me with White House counsel present."
*   **0:25-0:30:** He points his right index finger to his right again, then brings both hands up, palms facing each other, then points to himself with both thumbs. He states, "And I said to Fauci at that meeting, you say I'm lying."
*   **0:30-0:35:** He points to himself again, then spreads his hands wide, palms up, with an exasperated expression. He challenges, "For eight years you've been saying I'm lying about this. Show me the study."
*   **0:35-0:40:** He looks to his right, then back, shrugging, then points to his right again. He mimics Fauci's response: "And he said, 'Well, I don't have it here. It's back in Bethesda.'"
*   **0:40-0:45:** With hands resting on the podium, he then gestures with his right hand, palm up. He concludes, "And I'll send it to you. I never got it."
*   **0:45-0:50:** He gestures with his left hand, then makes a forceful punching motion with his right fist. He says, "So I sued him. Me and Aaron Siri and..."
*   **0:50-0:55:** He points to his right, then gestures widely with both hands open, palms up. He recounts, "...after stonewalling us for a year, they..."
*   **0:55-1:00:** He continues wide, open-hand gestures, then rests his hands on the podium, nodding. He finishes the story: "...their lawyers met us on the courthouse steps and said, 'Yep, you're right. We never had any study.'"
*   **1:00-1:05:** He uses his right thumb to count off points on his left hand's fingers. He advises, "And they gave it to us in writing, you can go to the CHD website, my website, and you can see that letter from HHS."
*   **1:05-1:10:** He continues the counting gesture, then briefly spreads his hands before resting them. He explains, "There's no downstream liability, there's no front-end safety testing that says 'saves them a quarter billion dollars.'"
*   **1:10-1:15:** He repeats the counting gesture, then spreads his hands wide. He adds, "And there's no marketing and advertising costs."
*   **1:15-1:20:** He points with his right index finger to his right. He continues, "Because the federal government is ordering them to get..."
*   **1:20-1:25:** He maintains the pointing gesture, then rests his hands on the podium, shrugging. He states, "...ordering 78 million school kids to take that vaccine every year."
*   **1:25-1:30:** He spreads both hands wide, palms up, looking at the audience, then brings them down. He rhetorically asks, "What better product could you have? And so..."
*   **1:30-1:35:** He brings both hands up, palms facing each other, then spreads them wide. He describes, "...they uh there was a gold rush to add all these new vaccines to the schedule."
*   **1:35-1:40:** The video momentarily lightens, as if a filter is applied, then returns to normal. He continues gesturing widely. He asserts, "And we don't need, most of these vaccines are unnecessary."
*   **1:40-1:45:** He continues to gesture with open hands, looking intently. He clarifies, "Many of them are for diseases that are not even casually contagious."
*   **1:45-1:50:** He brings his hands together, then points his right index finger. He reiterates, "It was a gold rush. Because if you get onto that schedule..."
*   **1:50-1:55:** He rests his hands, then makes a fist and moves it forward, then rests it again. He explains the financial incentive: "...the recommended schedule, it's a billion dollars a year for your company."
*   **1:55-2:00:** He gestures with his right hand, then brings both hands together palms up, then rests them. He adds, "And in many cases, NIH is clearing the royalties."
*   **2:00-2:05:** He spreads his hands wide, then uses his right index finger to count on his left hand. He states, "So we got all of these new vaccines, 72 shots, 16 vaccines, and now even more because we're doing the HPV vaccine."
*   **2:05-2:10:** He makes a punching gesture with his right hand, then rests it and begins counting on his left hand again. He declares, "And that year, 1989, we saw an explosion in chronic disease in American children."
*   **2:10-2:15:** He spreads both hands, palms down, then resumes counting on his left hand. He lists, "The neurological diseases suddenly exploded in 1989. ADHD, ADHD, sleep..."
*   **2:15-2:20:** He continues counting on his left hand. He adds, "...disorders..."
*   **2:20-2:25:** He finishes counting, then spreads his hands wide. He continues the list: "...uh language delays, ASD, autism..."
*   **2:25-2:30:** He continues wide gestures, then brings his hands together, then rests them on the podium. He concludes the list: "...Tourette's Syndrome, ticks, narcolepsy. These are all things that I never heard of."
*   **2:30-2:35:** He points to himself with his right thumb, then back to himself, then rests his hands. He provides a statistic: "Autism went from 1 in 10,000 of my generation, according to the CDC data..."
*   **2:35-2:40:** He looks around, then straight ahead, resting his hands on the podium. He finishes, "...to 1 in every 34 kids today."
*   **2:40-2:43:** He keeps his hands on the podium as the video abruptly cuts off. He says, "For many years..."

Here's a detailed breakdown:

*   **0:00 - 0:01:** The video opens with a medium close-up shot of the man. He has grey hair, is wearing glasses, a dark grey suit jacket over a maroon sweater, and a white collared shirt. He is sitting back in his chair, looking slightly downwards and to his right, with a pensive expression. His hands are resting on his lap.
    *   **Audio:** "...Covid has also changed our relationship..."

*   **0:01 - 0:02:** The shot widens to include both the woman and the man. They are seated opposite each other with a small, round, dark table between them. On the table are two clear glass bottles. The woman is on the left, wearing a black blazer over a dark top, and black pants with black ballet flats. Her blonde hair is styled in a bob. The man is on the right, in the same attire.
    *   **Audio:** "...to healthcare. What what does that mean for..."

*   **0:02 - 0:08:** The camera focuses on the woman. She is speaking, gesturing with her hands. Her left hand is resting on her right forearm, and her right hand is moving, forming a cupped shape as if holding something small, then opening her palm slightly. She has a gold rose-shaped brooch pinned to her lapel and a delicate necklace.
    *   **Audio:** "...vaccination hesitancy in developing countries and develop nations."

*   **0:08 - 0:10:** The shot returns to the man. He is looking at the woman, listening intently, with a slight nod.
    *   **Audio:** (Woman finishes speaking, a brief pause)

*   **0:10 - 0:21:** The camera remains on the man as he begins to speak. He leans slightly forward, then sits back, gesturing with his right hand, making an open-palm gesture towards the right. His expression is serious as he talks.
    *   **Audio:** "Well, the, you know, the misinformation about vaccines, uh, and associating certain people like myself or having an intent with vaccines..."

*   **0:21 - 0:28:** The man continues speaking, still gesturing with his right hand, which is now extended upwards and outwards, then slightly downwards.
    *   **Audio:** "...That was most acute in the United States. But, uh, the pandemic..."

*   **0:28 - 0:38:** He becomes more animated, using both hands to emphasize his points. He spreads his arms wide, then brings them in, clenching his fists slightly, then opening them again. His voice is firm.
    *   **Audio:** "...which you would have thought, wow, global health research, talking about health, being ready for the next pandemic. You know, when you've got millions of deaths..."

*   **0:38 - 0:40:** He continues gesturing with both hands, bringing them down and together, with a more somber expression.
    *   **Audio:** "...isn't that, you know, it's sad, it's tragic, but isn't it at least a benefit that..."

*   **0:40 - 0:46:** The shot widens again to show both individuals. The man is still speaking and gesturing with his right hand, pushing it slightly forward. The woman is listening, her hands clasped on her lap.
    *   **Audio:** "...health is on the agenda. Sadly, it's a topic nobody wants to talk about because it was painful, you know, it's over..."

*   **0:46 - 0:51:** The camera is again on a medium close-up of the man. He continues his gesture, moving his right hand in a circular motion, then lets it rest. His expression softens slightly as he concludes his thought.
    *   **Audio:** "...uh, let's move on from that."

caption.video0:
The video opens with an animated scene depicting several people sitting on a long bench or seat, possibly on public transportation. On the left, a person with dark skin and dark hair is partially visible, wearing a brown top and holding a light-colored object near their mouth. Next to them is a woman with dark hair in a bob, wearing a red dress and a light blue face mask. She has her arm around a small child with curly brown hair, wearing a light blue shirt and shorts, who is looking up at her with a smile. To the right of the woman and child, a woman with blonde, curly hair, wearing a green tank top and dark green pants, is smiling and talking to another woman with long brown hair, wearing a light blue dress. Neither of these two women is wearing a mask. The text "If you are in crowded places with poor ventilation" appears at the bottom of the screen as the narration begins, "If you are in crowded places with poor ventilation, such as public transportation,".

At the 0:04 mark, the scene changes slightly, now showing a different group of people on what appears to be the same public transportation. On the far left, a bald man in a suit is wearing a light blue mask. Next to him, a man with glasses and a yellow sweater, holding a brown stick, is also wearing a light blue mask. To his right, a blonde-haired person with headphones and a black sweater is looking at a phone. They are not wearing a mask. On the far right, a person with dark skin and a light blue hoodie is standing, coughing into their elbow, and not wearing a mask. The narration continues, "such as public transportation," while the blonde-haired person puts on a light blue face mask at 0:06.

At 0:07, the scene shifts back to the previous group of women and the child. The blonde woman, who was previously talking and not wearing a mask, is now holding a tissue to her nose as if sneezing or blowing her nose. The narration continues, "wear a mask because it keeps you and your community safe from COVID-19, flu, and other respiratory illnesses." As the narration explains the benefits of masks, the blonde woman, the woman in the light blue dress, and the child all put on light blue face masks between 0:07 and 0:09, aligning with the "wear a mask" instruction. By 0:09, everyone visible in this group is now wearing a mask.

The scene then transitions back to the second group of people on the public transportation at 0:09. The person in the light blue hoodie, who was previously coughing, is now wearing a light blue mask. All four individuals in this shot are now wearing light blue masks. The narration concludes at 0:13 with "from COVID-19, flu, and other respiratory illnesses."

The video then fades to a solid dark blue background at 0:13. White text appears on the screen in two lines: "Live your life" followed by "and stay safe and healthy" appearing below it at 0:16. Finally, at 0:18, the logo for the "World Health Organization Western Pacific Region" appears in white below the text, featuring the WHO emblem (a serpent coiled around a staff within a globe, surrounded by olive branches). The video ends with this logo and text on the dark blue background at 0:22. There is no background music throughout the video, only the narration.

caption.video0:
The video starts with a medium shot of a man wearing a red and black plaid shirt and black-rimmed glasses sitting at a wooden table. He has a dark beard and his hair is neatly combed back. A black mug with "DAILY WIRE +" and another black tumbler with "LEFTIST TEARS" are visible on the table. Behind him is a large window overlooking a lush, green landscape, suggesting an outdoor or nature-inspired setting. A banjo is visible in the background to the left. To his right, there's a bookshelf with various books and decorative items, including a small shopping cart.

At 00:00, the man begins speaking, stating, "In my opinion, thanks to the psychiatric industry, what we're dealing with is a giant category error." As he says this, he holds his hands up, palms open, then brings them together, interlocking his fingers as he continues speaking.

At 00:08, his hands remain interlocked as he says, "And at this point, they have taken nearly every negative emotion, experience, behavior," He gestures with his hands, moving them slightly apart and then bringing them back together with each word, as if enumerating items.

At 00:18, he continues, "and categorized it as a disease." He widens his hands and gestures outward, emphasizing "categorized." He then places his left hand on the table and uses his right hand to pick up the "LEFTIST TEARS" tumbler, holding it for a moment before placing it back down at 00:20.

He says, "And in many of these cases, the categorization makes no sense." He looks directly at the camera, then gestures with his right hand as if making a point.

At 00:23, he adds, "But that doesn't mean that the emotion or the experience or the behavior isn't real or doesn't exist." He gestures with both hands, palms open, spreading them wide as he speaks.

At 00:28, he clarifies, "So for example, one of the one of the many clinical mental diseases, the psychiatrists have come up with, uh, to explain misbehaving children is something called oppositional defiant disorder." As he says "many," he briefly puts his hands together, then holds them slightly apart, moving his index finger and thumb as if pinching something.

At 00:39, he defines, "which is a disorder characterized by a child acting, acting oppositional and defiant." He continues gesturing with his hands, emphasizing "disorder" and "acting."

At 00:46, he states, "Now, it's definitely true that some children, many children, even, all children, to some extent, do behave in oppositional and defiant ways." He gestures with both hands, palms open, then clasps them together at 00:48, then spreads them wide again at 00:50. He emphasizes the words "some children" and "many children."

At 00:54, he says, "That is very real. No one is no one is denying the existence of opposition and defiance." He gestures with both hands, palms open and spread wide.

At 00:59, he adds, "And sometimes that kind of behavior can be severe and have extremely bad consequences." He clasps his hands together.

At 01:05, he asserts, "But it's not a disease." He looks directly at the camera with a serious expression, emphasizing "not a disease."

At 01:07, he provides examples, "Diabetes is a disease. Liver cancer is a disease. Parkinson's is a disease. These are illnesses that can be detected structurally in the human body." He continues to hold his hands clasped, maintaining a serious expression.

At 01:20, he clarifies further, "You can find them without even talking to the patient at all, okay? So this is not, you know, serve doing a survey and they tell you things, and then you go looking for certain chemical things in the brain." He gestures with his right hand as if pointing, then touches his forehead, then points again.

At 01:31, he continues, "No, they could not even talk to you and run tests and find the disease without you saying a word to them." He gestures with both hands, palms open, then closes them into fists, then opens them wide again, placing his left hand on the table at 01:36.

At 01:38, he contrasts, "But a child's defiant behavior is not a structural illness of the human body. Guess what? They could run a test on every single child in the world and they will not find anything that they could call, oh, there it is. There's oppositional defiant disorder. They've got it." He clasps his hands, then spreads them, then points at the tumbler on the table, then gestures with both hands again, spreading them out wide.

At 01:56, he states, "Cannot happen." He looks directly at the camera, hands still spread open.

At 01:57, he asks, "Why is that? Because it's not a disease." He clenches his fists, then opens his hands, palms open, emphasizing "not a disease."

At 02:00, he continues, "They've they've made it up. They've invented this disease." He places his hands flat on the table, palms up.

At 02:03, he asks, "That's why they don't do blood tests to diagnose it. It's an unwanted behavior. Unwanted behavior is not a disease." He clasps his hands, then spreads them and brings them together again, emphasizing each point.

At 02:13, he transitions, "Now, one of the newer inventions of the psychiatric industry is something called prolonged grief disorder." He clasps his hands, then brings his index fingers up.

At 02:18, he explains, "This is a disease, quote-unquote, that can be diagnosed if a person is very sad after losing a loved one and those feelings of sadness persist for more than a year." He spreads his hands wide, then gestures with his right hand as if giving a timeline.

At 02:29, he asserts, "Now, I would say with great confidence that this is a fake disease." He clenches his right fist, then gestures with his right hand, palms open, then spreads both hands wide, emphasizing "fake disease."

At 02:35, he asks, "Is grief fake? Is Matt Walsh denying that grief exists?" He spreads his hands, palms open, then brings his hands back together, then spreads them again.

At 02:41, he clarifies, "Am I claiming that if you lost your spouse, that you're not really grieving them? No." He spreads his hands, then places them on the table, palms down.

At 02:44, he continues, "What I'm saying is that you don't have a disease." He gestures with his hands, palms open, emphasizing "disease."

At 02:46, he asks, "Is grief very serious? Is it something that that deeply impacts a person's life? Can it lead even to self-destructive behavior and suicide in extreme cases?" He clasps his hands, then brings them apart, palms open, then back together.

At 02:53, he answers, "Yes, on all counts, but it's not a disease." He spreads his hands wide, then brings them together, emphasizing "not a disease."

At 02:57, he criticizes, "The psychiatric industry has once again taken a normal human experience and medicalized it, turned it into a thing that you can prescribe drugs for." He clenches his right fist, then gestures with his right hand, emphasizing "normal human experience" and "medicalized it."

At 03:07, he states, "And in order to do so, they have come up with completely arbitrary guidelines and parameters." He gestures with both hands, palms open, emphasizing "arbitrary guidelines."

At 03:10, he asks, "Who says that grief is only supposed to last 12 months? Who decided that? Who who the hell puts psychiatrists in charge of deciding how long you should be sad after your wife dies?" He gestures with both hands, palms open, then points forward with his right index finger, then gestures again with both hands, palms open.

At 03:26, he continues, "Who the hell is this person to sit there with their little notepad and say, well, it's been 13 months, too long, you have a disease. What?" He mimes writing on a notepad, then gestures with both hands, palms open, emphasizing "disease" and "what."

At 03:31, he says, "And most people just accept that." He shrugs his shoulders.

At 03:33, he adds, "Not only do they accept it, but they it's like they don't understand why some of us criticize it." He spreads his hands wide, then brings them together, rubbing his thumbs together.

At 03:39, he states, "It it it blows my mind. The whole idea is absurd." He takes off his glasses and rubs his eyes, then puts his glasses back on at 03:41 and clasps his hands.

At 03:43, he continues, "I mean, it's outrageous. It's insulting." He holds his hands up, palms open, then clenches them and brings them together again.

At 03:47, he adds, "But this is what these people have done." He gestures with his right hand.

At 03:49, he expands, "They've done it with, again, the entire human condition. Every difficult human emotion or experience has been arbitrarily and entirely subjectively re-categorized as a clinical problem." He gestures with both hands, then pinches something between his index finger and thumb, then gestures again, emphasizing "entire human condition."

At 04:02, he challenges, "And if you think I'm making that up, you can go, you can find the DSM. You can find it online. You can find you could go through it and I guarantee you," He gestures with his right hand, pointing, then brings his hands together, emphasizing "guarantee you."

At 04:09, he explains, "what literally every negative emotion has multiple diseases for it." He gestures with both hands, forming circles with his index fingers and thumbs.

At 04:16, he challenges, "Try me if you don't believe me." He spreads his hands, palms open.

At 04:19, he concludes, "And this is what has happened with depression in my view. Depression is real. It is serious." He clasps his hands, then clenches his fists and then opens his hands, palms open, emphasizing "depression is real" and "serious."

At 04:28, he states, "I do not believe that it is a disease, however, okay?" He places his hands on the table, palms down, emphasizing "not a disease."

At 04:30, he explains, "Depression is despair, which itself is again essentially a much more persistent, deeper, more more encompassing sadness. I prefer the word despair." He gestures with his right hand, then places his right hand on his chest.

At 04:39, he states, "So despair, I submit, is not a disease because it is a completely natural, even fundamental aspect of the human experience." He gestures with his right hand, then places his right hand on his chest.

At 04:47, he elaborates, "One one of the parameters that is supposed to separate clinical despair from non-clinical is that we are told clinical despair, depression, often has no discernable cause or reason." He gestures with his right hand, then spreads his hands wide, emphasizing "no discernable cause."

At 05:01, he continues, "And I heard this from a lot of the people that, you know, when I asked that question and people were freaking out and were angry. Of the people who are not freaking out and we're trying to give a reasonable answer, which I appreciate, many of them said this." He gestures with both hands, rubbing his thumbs together.

At 05:08, he summarizes, "They said something like this that that that, you know, well, when you're sad, there's like a reason, something happens. But when you're depressed, it there's no reason, you just you just you you you it's just there. It's like this cloud that follows you." He gestures with both hands, then spreads them wide, then motions to his head, then spreads them wide again.

At 05:21, he continues, "Um, if somebody's very sad because they lost their job or a family member, uh, died or something, that's not clinical depression, I'm told." He gestures with his right hand.

At 05:28, he adds, "Though though as we've seen, the psychiatrists still have a clinical disease and a bottle of pills even for that, but it's not depression." He points to the tumbler, then spreads his hands wide.

At 05:36, he clarifies, "Depression has no immediate external cause." He spreads his hands wide.

At 05:39, he explains, "So if everything seems to be fine in your life and yet you feel this deep persistent despair, you are clinically depressed." He gestures with his hands, palms open, then spreads them wide.

At 05:45, he concludes, "And you should probably be on drugs, that's the idea. But here's my point." He gestures with both hands, palms open, then takes off his glasses and rubs his eyes.

At 05:50, he re-emphasizes, "And this is like, this is after 50 minutes of rambling. This is my point. This is what I am trying to say on this subject." He gestures with both hands, then clenches his fists, then spreads them wide.

At 05:59, he states, "There there is no such thing as despair, depression, felt for no reason." He brings his hands together, palms open, then clenches them, then spreads them wide, then brings them together again, emphasizing "no such thing."

At 06:08, he insists, "That is a misnomer. So if somebody says, I feel depressed for no reason, no, they don't." He spreads his hands wide, then brings them together, then spreads them wide again, emphasizing "no, they don't."

At 06:14, he states, "There is a reason, and I would argue that simply being a human, having a mind, living as a conscious being on this planet full of death and suffering, the very fact of being sentient, of being aware of yourself and your place in the world is reason enough to feel despair." He clasps his hands, then spreads them wide, then gestures upward, then gestures to himself, then spreads his hands wide again.

At 06:51, he clarifies, "That doesn't mean people should feel that way, or that we should abandon people to those feelings and not try to help them. It doesn't mean that you shouldn't seek help. It doesn't mean that you should be content with your despair. It doesn't mean any of that." He gestures with both hands, palms open, then clenches them, then spreads them wide, then brings them together again, emphasizing each point.

At 07:05, he continues, "My only point is that the despair is rooted in something deep, the deepest thing of all. It is a product of being human." He gestures with both hands, palms open, then clenches them, then spreads them wide, emphasizing "product of being human."

At 07:12, he clarifies, "Which which which which means that, though it is real, and though it is painful, and though it is very serious and very hard, it is not the same sort of thing as malaria or typhoid fever. It is not a disease." He gestures with both hands, then spreads them wide, emphasizing "not a disease."

At 07:25, he adds, "Yes, getting malaria and typhoid fever are also things that can happen to people, but they are not a product of the human condition itself." He gestures with both hands, then clenches them, then spreads them wide.

At 07:33, he concludes, "They are an aberration, that's what makes them a disease. They don't they don't come from your very humanity, like despair does." He gestures with both hands, then clenches them, then spreads them wide, emphasizing "humanity" and "despair."

At 07:41, he elaborates, "Now, philosophers and theologians and thinkers through the ages have had a lot to say about the problem of despair, and how we should cope with it as human beings. Only in very recent time did we decide that we should put it into the same category as physical illness and treat it the exact same way." He gestures with both hands, then spreads them wide, then brings them together, then spreads them wide again.

At 08:00, he continues, "And I would argue that this strategy, along with being rooted in a fundamental misunderstanding of depression and of the human condition itself, has been an abject failure. We have been treating depression as a disease for decades, and you tell me." He gestures with his right hand, then clenches his fist, then spreads his hands wide, then gestures to the camera, emphasizing "abject failure."

At 08:16, he asks, "Generally speaking, has it worked? Have people become less depressed? Have people become more able to function? Has suicide rates rate gone down? No. No and no." He gestures with both hands, then spreads them wide, then brings them together, then gestures to the camera, emphasizing "no" three times.

At 08:29, he states, "In fact, as we disease-ify and medicalize all of these difficult human emotions, what we find is that year over year, the problems get worse. Suicide becomes more common. People become less able to cope with the challenges of life." He clasps his hands, then spreads them wide, then clenches them, then spreads them wide again, emphasizing "problems get worse."

At 08:44, he concludes, "The strategy isn't working, and it isn't working because it's based on a lie, and it's a lie that makes people feel powerless." He gestures with both hands, then clenches them, then spreads them wide, emphasizing "lie" and "powerless."

At 08:56, he continues, "If your feelings are a disease, then you can't do anything about them. You can't conquer them on your own. You are helpless against them. That's been the message for decades." He gestures with both hands, then clenches them, then spreads them wide, emphasizing "helpless" and "decades."

At 09:05, he adds, "And for decades we have paid an unimaginable price because of it." He clasps his hands, emphasizing "unimaginable price."

At 09:10, he states, "So I am suggesting that it may be time to stop, just for a moment, analyze some of these basic presuppositions of the modern psychiatric field." He gestures with his right hand, then pinches something between his index finger and thumb, then gestures with both hands, emphasizing "modern psychiatric field."

At 09:34, he advises, "Realize how how totally at odds they are with the insights of of of virtually everyone who lived on earth up until the existence of modern psychiatry, um, realize that this is a field that has been extremely disastrously wrong about a lot of things." He gestures with both hands, then pinches something between his index finger and thumb, then spreads his hands wide, emphasizing "disastrously wrong."

At 09:44, he states, "And I believe that this is one of those things. It's perhaps the main thing it is wrong about, and that's all I'm trying to say. And I will keep saying it, no matter how angry people are about it." He gestures with both hands, then clenches them, then spreads them wide, then brings them together again, emphasizing "main thing."

At 09:54, he concludes, "I believe you're directing your anger in the wrong place. I'm not the one who has been tasked with solving a problem that I have only made progressively worse for the last 60 years. That's the psychiatric industry." He gestures with both hands, then clenches them, then spreads them wide, emphasizing "wrong place" and "psychiatric industry." The video ends.

The video features a man with a long, grey beard and rectangular, tinted sunglasses, speaking directly into the camera. He appears to be inside a vehicle, possibly a car, with a seatbelt visible over his light-colored shirt or jacket, and a car seat in the background on the left. His facial expressions are serious and focused as he delivers his message.

Throughout the video (0:00-1:35), several text overlays are present:
*   A user interface for a video player is at the bottom, showing "Just Own It. ✔ Following" with the handle "@JustOwnItIs..." and the date "3/27/23".
*   Below this, more text reads: "Dr. Zev Zelenko gives a quick biology lesson, with national security implications... more".
*   Further down, a profile picture of a man (presumably Frank Zelenko) is next to the text "Frank (Ephraim) Zelenko" and "@frank_zelenko 2023-04-18".
*   Below that, a quote from Frank Zelenko reads: "... theres a class of virus called SINGLE STRAND RNA-...includes all the covid strains, influenza virus, rsv, MARBURG VIRUS, ebola, hanta....SAME TREAT... more".

At **0:00**, the man begins speaking, saying, "So there's a class of viruses called single-stranded RNA viruses."
From **0:09**, he lists examples: "And some of those viruses include all the Covid strains, Covid-19, influenza virus, RSV virus, Marburg virus, Ebola virus, Hantavirus, and there are a few others."
At **0:26**, he continues, "The point being is that they all are single-stranded RNA viruses that use the same common pathway in making copies of their genetic material."
He then specifies at **0:38**, "That's when they're called RNA-dependent RNA polymerase."
From **0:41**, he adds, "That's what I've been talking about for the last two years in the context of COVID-19."
At **0:47**, he broadens his statement: "But if we extrapolate that to think more broadly, then it's the same mechanism of treatment using zinc ionophores and zinc."
He reiterates the scope of this treatment from **1:00**: "Could inhibit all the strains of COVID-19, influenza virus, RSV virus, Ebola virus, Marburg virus, and Hantavirus."
From **1:12**, he emphasizes, "This has tremendous amount of national security implications. And I I don't think anyone realizes this."
He concludes at **1:23** by stating, "And so I'm pleading with our scientists and uh, the honest people in our government to investigate this because as the..." The audio cuts off at **1:35**.

The video then transitions to a full-screen display of a Twitter/X post at **1:35**, showing the same video frame but with a play button overlay. The tweet text is now fully visible:
"Frank (Ephraim) Zelenko
@frank_zelenko
“.... theres a class of virus called SINGLE STRAND RNA-...includes all the covid strains, influenza virus, rsv, MARBURG VIRUS, ebola, hanta....SAME TREATMENT using ZINC IONOPHORES(HCQ, IVN, QUERCITIN, EGCG) and ZINC....”
4:18 PM 2023-04-18 from Earth 283K Views
📊 View Tweet activity"
The embedded video's own UI is still visible at the bottom, showing "Just Own It. ✔ Following" and "@JustOwnItIs... 3/27/23". The audio has stopped.
