tweetText:
🚨 WARNING: Self-Spreading Vaccines Are Approaching Fast!

This technology was ALMOST ready to be deployed for COVID-19.

So, don't think that global health authorities wouldn't use it in the event of another pandemic.

The only thing stopping the mass use of this technology is this pesky thing known as informed consent — but do you think they actually care about that?

The whole idea behind self-spreading vaccines is grounded in circumventing "behavioral barriers."

Just listen to their own words:

"Infectious disease control faces significant challenges including: how to therapeutically target the highest-risk populations, circumvent behavioral barriers, and overcome pathogen persistence and resistance mechanisms."

— Timothy Notton et al.

The U.S. military and DARPA have also been researching self-spreading vaccines, with DARPA exploring antivirals to "evolve" in real-time against new viral strains.

However, if a self-spreading vaccine mutates in an unforeseen way, it could potentially pose grave risks for the entire population.

Attorney Aaron Siri issued this statement on the matter:

"What might even be the biggest victim ... if they ever release this thing, it's going to be civil, individual rights...

"Here, they're going to release a product where you're going to have no choice effectively but to take it. That is the ultimate crushing of individual and civil rights."

Watch and listen to this important video from @HighWireTalk.

caption.video0:
The video features a split-screen interview between Jefferey Jaxen (Investigative Journalist) on the left and Del Bigtree (Host, TheHighwire.com) on the right. Both men maintain serious expressions throughout most of the segment. The "THE HIGHWIRE with DEL BIGTREE" logo is visible at the top center.

Here's a detailed breakdown of the video's content:

*   **00:00 - 00:10:** Jefferey Jaxen begins by introducing a "big story" and states they will do a "deeper dive" starting in 2018.
*   **00:10 - 00:18:** Jaxen reminds viewers that 2018 was a year before COVID-19 (SARS-CoV-2) and mentions that Johns Hopkins released a report.
*   **00:18 - 00:20:** An aerial view of The Johns Hopkins Hospital is shown.
*   **00:20 - 00:23:** The screen displays the cover of a Johns Hopkins report titled "TECHNOLOGIES TO ADDRESS Global Catastrophic Biological Risks."
*   **00:23 - 00:31:** Jaxen explains that the 2018 report was released roughly a year before Johns Hopkins hosted "Event 201," which was a fictitious coronavirus pandemic exercise. He states that in 2018, Johns Hopkins was already assessing "great new technologies" to address biological risks.
*   **00:31 - 00:53:** A text box appears over the Johns Hopkins report cover, highlighting a passage: "Self-spreading vaccines—also known as transmissible or self-propagating vaccines—are genetically engineered to move through populations in the same way as communicable diseases, but rather than causing disease, they confer protection." Jaxen reads this definition aloud.
*   **00:53 - 01:08:** Jaxen then fast-forwards to 2020, when the world was searching for a COVID vaccine, and introduces a Telegraph article.
*   **01:08 - 01:21:** A screenshot of a "The Telegraph" article from January 31, 2020, titled "Could self-spreading vaccines stop a coronavirus pandemic?" is displayed. Jaxen emphasizes that this technology was already "in the mix."
*   **01:21 - 01:34:** A text box highlights a quote from the Telegraph article by Dr. Amesh Adalja of Johns Hopkins: "Dr Amesh Adalja, a senior scholar at Johns Hopkins University, says the outbreak of the coronavirus may have come too soon for the technology."
*   **01:34 - 01:45:** The text box then expands to include more from the article: "Dr Adalja says the method of vaccination will throw serious medical ethics questions. His view is endorsed by the Department of Health, which explored the technology in a presentation late last year. In a study from November, the department stated that self-spreading techniques could eliminate "vaccine delay"." Jaxen highlights the phrase "vaccine delay."
*   **01:45 - 02:29:** Del Bigtree interjects, equating "vaccine delay" to the audience, suggesting that the medical establishment considers them a "problem" and is creating "man-made viruses" to inoculate them, overriding their consent. He refers to this as being "in competition with God."
*   **02:29 - 03:10:** Jaxen confirms that "vaccine delay" was explicitly stated, not a theoretical aside.
*   **03:10 - 03:37:** The Telegraph article's text box is shown again, highlighting another quote: "The presentation also stated that such vaccines did "very little harm" in comparison to a pandemic. However, the department highlighted a number of ethical issues that arise with self-spreading vaccines. One of which is that it is "less lethal" not "non-lethal" meaning it can still kill. "Some people will die who otherwise would have lived, even though fewer people die overall," it stated." Del Bigtree sarcastically remarks, "There it is. Rare. Small."
*   **03:37 - 03:47:** Jaxen notes that this "greater good conversation" is usually behind closed doors but is now public.
*   **03:47 - 04:06:** Del Bigtree argues that the language around vaccines is changing, admitting risks. He shows a montage of people receiving injections, including a man in uniform, a child, an adult with two children, and other adults, implying these are instances of the population being subjected to vaccination.
*   **04:06 - 04:55:** Del Bigtree continues, stating that the previous narrative of "safe and effective" without harm is being exposed, and that this new "man-made virus" would keep spreading, "rewriting" the immune system. Jaxen states that public health officials know about these ethical issues but don't want the public to know, hence the "safe and effective" narrative was pushed, until COVID.
*   **04:55 - 05:03:** Jaxen states that "conversations really started to be supercharged" in 2014, when an opinion paper titled "The case for transmissible antivirals to control population-wide infectious disease" was published in the journal *Cell*.
*   **05:03 - 05:48:** The screen shows the *Trends in Biotechnology* article. A text box over it discusses "Infectious disease control" and a "recently proposed solution" involving "antivirals that transmit by 'piggybacking' on viral replication," termed "therapeutic interfering particles (TIPs)." Jaxen highlights that these are "engineered molecular parasites" designed to "steal replication resources from the wild type virus."
*   **05:48 - 05:51:** Jaxen identifies the lead author of the paper as Leor Weinberger.
*   **05:51 - 05:55:** A headshot of Leor Weinberger, PhD, Director, Center for Cell Circuitry, Gladstone Institutes, is displayed.
*   **05:55 - 06:02:** A screenshot of the Gladstone Institutes website features Leor Weinberger, PhD. The text on the website describes his lab's work on HIV and also states, "The group also develops novel therapies for SARS-CoV-2..."
*   **06:02 - 06:14:** Jaxen clarifies that Weinberger's initial work on TIPs was for AIDS, but then "COVID came along." He then shifts to 2016, mentioning DARPA's involvement.
*   **06:14 - 06:29:** The DARPA logo appears, followed by a screenshot of a DARPA website article from April 7, 2016, titled "Co-evolving Antivirals Aim to Keep Ahead of Fast-Changing Viruses."
*   **06:29 - 07:13:** A quote from the DARPA article is highlighted: "You can think of these TIP-filled envelopes as tiny Trojan horses, but instead of containing warriors they contain pretenders that ultimately outnumber real disease-causing viruses and interfere with their ability to replicate," Gimlett said. And because TIPs are made of genetic material, he added, they will be subject to mutation and diversification over time just as the genomes of real viruses are. "Once we develop a TIP that works for a given virus, we expect it to generate a steady stream of variants so there will always be a population of TIPs with the right genetic stuff to disrupt any new strains of that virus that may arise." Jaxen points out the "slick" messaging.
*   **07:13 - 07:26:** Jaxen introduces a clip of Leor Weinberger speaking about engineering viruses.
*   **07:26 - 08:47:** Leor Weinberger is shown giving a presentation in 2016. He uses two pieces of measuring tape (one long, one short) and a Hoberman sphere to illustrate how his "therapeutic interfering particles" (TIPs) are shorter, more efficient, and outcompete full-length viruses for replication resources inside a cell, turning the cell into a "factory to produce the therapeutic particle."
*   **08:47 - 09:03:** Del Bigtree expresses alarm at the concept of a man-made virus designed to spread and rewrite the immune system, stating it's "game over" for natural immunity.
*   **09:03 - 09:09:** A screenshot of a USAspending.gov grant summary is displayed. It shows a project grant awarded by the "Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)" to "VXBIOSCIENCES, INC." for $4.8 million. The description mentions "co-evolving SARS-CoV-2 antiviral" and "therapeutic interfering particles (TIPs)".
*   **09:09 - 09:44:** A text box highlights part of the grant description: "THE KEY INNOVATIONS OF THIS APPROACH ARE THAT THESE THERAPEUTIC PARASITES: (I) ESTABLISH CO-EVOLUTIONARY ARMS RACES, CO- EVOLVING WITH WILD-TYPE VIRUS TO OVERCOME RESISTANCE, (II) REPLICATE AND SELF-RENEW, ACTING AS SINGLE-ADMINISTRATION THERAPIES THAT CIRCUMVENT COMPLIANCE ISSUES, AND (III) SPREAD VIA THE EXACT SAME RISK FACTORS AND TRANSMISSION ROUTES AS SARS-COV-2—AUTONOMOUSLY UTILIZING SUPERSPREADERS TO DEPLOY THE INTERVENTION— THEREBY CIRCUMVENTING MANUFACTURING-AT-SCALE AND ROLL-OUT CHALLENGES." Jaxen emphasizes "circumvent compliance issues."
*   **09:44 - 10:13:** Jaxen states that the grant targets "people that don't want to take it" and highlights the phrases "super-spreaders" and "man-made rewrite of the immune system." He also shows clips of people coughing in public places.
*   **10:13 - 10:25:** Jaxen mentions that Leor Weinberger's lab received the contract and then gave a TED Talk to promote his product.
*   **10:25 - 10:48:** Leor Weinberger's TEDMED 2020 presentation is shown. He states that current vaccines always play "catchup" with mutated viruses and that "our medical responses are flat-footed." He then discusses his "radical idea" from 20 years ago to use viruses themselves as building blocks for therapies that can mutate and transmit.
*   **10:48 - 11:44:** Weinberger gets emotional while stating, "for the first time in 20 years, we got it to work. And this is the first time I'm sharing it publicly," to applause.
*   **11:44 - 12:35:** Del Bigtree expresses deep concern and warns that this is "misguided stupidity." He shows a clip from Jurassic Park.
*   **12:35 - 12:55:** The Jurassic Park scene shows Dr. Henry Wu explaining that the dinosaurs are all female, and therefore can't breed. Ian Malcolm states, "Life finds a way," and a child hides as a dinosaur attacks their vehicle.
*   **12:55 - 13:45:** Del Bigtree emphasizes the warning from Jurassic Park, stating that this technology could lead to a "mass extinction event." He expresses worry that "informed consent" is the only thing protecting us, but the FDA is already easing these requirements.
*   **13:45 - 13:53:** Jaxen references the "PREVENT Pandemics Act" introduced in 2022.
*   **13:53 - 13:58:** A screenshot of a legislative bill, "S. 3799," titled "To prepare for, and respond to, existing viruses, emerging new threats, and pandemics," is shown.
*   **13:58 - 14:13:** A text box highlights "SEC. 506K. PLATFORM TECHNOLOGIES." and specifies that "preliminary evidence submitted by the sponsor... demonstrates that the platform technology has the potential to be incorporated in, or utilized by, more than one drug without an adverse effect on quality, manufacturing, or safety..." Jaxen states that if they find such a technology, they will "fast-track this thing" for the next pandemic.
*   **14:13 - 14:23:** Jaxen states that the US government is "locked and loaded" to implement this technology. He then introduces a 2023 Bloomberg article.
*   **14:23 - 14:29:** A screenshot of a Bloomberg article from July 14, 2016, titled "To Fight a Virus, Get a Virus: Military Bets on Mutant Pathogen," is shown.
*   **14:29 - 14:36:** A quote from the Bloomberg article is highlighted: "A pair of brothers working in laboratories on opposite coasts of the U.S. are brewing a new approach to fighting viruses, one that's drawn interest from the military for protection against the threat of infections such as Zika and Ebola."
*   **14:36 - 14:42:** A black and white headshot of "ARIEL WEINBERGER, PHD" (CEO & Founder of Autonomous Therapeutics) is shown.
*   **14:42 - 14:47:** Jaxen mentions Ariel Weinberger (Leor's brother) and his company, Autonomous Therapeutics.
*   **14:47 - 15:05:** Jaxen reiterates the Bloomberg headline, "To Fight a Virus, Get a Virus: Military Bets on Mutant Pathogen," then refers to the article's text, discussing the Weinberger brothers' "new approach to fighting viruses" for protection against infections like Zika and Ebola.
*   **15:05 - 15:10:** Jaxen states that Autonomous Therapeutics received an award from DARPA.
*   **15:10 - 15:16:** A USAspending.gov "Contract Summary" shows a "Definitive Contract" awarded by the "Department of the Interior (DOI)" to "AUTONOMOUS THERAPEUTICS INC". The description begins "FIRST-IN-HUMAN THERAPEUTIC INTERFERING PARTICLE (TIP) TARGETING A RESPIRATORY VIRUS."
*   **15:16 - 15:34:** Jaxen highlights that the contract is for "FIRST-IN-HUMAN THERAPEUTIC INTERFERING PARTICLE (TIP) TARGETING A RESPIRATORY VIRUS." He then displays a patent application from May 18, 2023, for "THERAPEUTIC INTERFERING PARTICLES FOR CORONAVIRUS" by CHATURVEDI et al., listing Leor S. Weinberger as an inventor.
*   **15:34 - 15:48:** A section of the patent application is highlighted: "This invention was made with government support under 1-DP2-OD006677-01 awarded by the National Institutes of Health and under D17AC00009 awarded by DOD/DARPA. The government has certain rights in the invention." Jaxen emphasizes that "the government has certain rights in this."
*   **15:48 - 15:52:** Jaxen then shows another patent application, this one from Feb 1, 2024, by Weinberger et al. for "METHODS AND COMPOSITIONS FOR GENERATING A DELETION LIBRARY AND FOR IDENTIFYING A DEFECTIVE INTERFERING PARTICLE (DIP)."
*   **15:52 - 16:29:** Del Bigtree expresses extreme concern, interpreting the combined information to mean that the government is investing in and regulating a technology designed to bypass informed consent and "permanently rewrite" the human immune system, potentially leading to "mass extinction." He questions who can be trusted for safety studies when the government itself is the pharmaceutical industry.
*   **16:29 - 17:05:** Jaxen reiterates the concerns about vaccine safety discussions being suppressed at the academic level. He suggests that this technology mutates, diversifies, and is designed for a competitive advantage, continuously creating variants.
*   **17:05 - 17:10:** The screen shows the logo for the "WORLD ECONOMIC FORUM".
*   **17:10 - 17:28:** A World Economic Forum article from Nov 3, 2015, titled "Are viruses actually vital for our existence?" is displayed. A quote is highlighted: "But the 21 viral types that wreak havoc with the human body represent an insignificant fraction of the 100 million viral types on earth. Most viruses are actually vital to our very existence." It continues to say: "Algae and plants are primary producers, the foundation of the world's ecosystems. Using sunlight they turn raw elements like carbon dioxide, nitrogen and phosphorus into organic matter. In turn, they are eaten by herbivores, which are in turn eaten by other animals, and so on. Energy and nutrients are passed on up the food chain until animals die. But what ensures that the primary producers get the raw elements they need to get started? The answer hinges on the viruses' relationship with bacteria." Jaxen connects this to the ongoing discussion about viruses and bacteria.
*   **17:28 - 18:04:** Del Bigtree raises the hypothetical worst-case scenario where this synthetic, self-spreading technology, designed to outcompete natural viruses, could eradicate beneficial viruses and bacteria, leading to the "end of all life as we know it." He notes that the only thing protecting the public is the "pesky idea of informed consent."
*   **18:04 - 20:05:** Jaxen argues that the FDA's easing of informed consent requirements for "minimal risk trials" is problematic, as this technology is designed to circumvent informed consent from its inception.
*   **20:05 - 20:17:** Del Bigtree warns that a trial of this product means "it's all over," bypassing consent and permanently rewriting the immune system, making people "a natural cyborg" and their bodies "changed forever." He warns of an "mass extinction event."
*   **20:17 - 20:20:** Jaxen agrees that it's a critical topic and that the FDA and CDC's ACIP committee won't stop it.
*   **20:20 - 20:25:** Del Bigtree is skeptical that government agencies will intervene to stop this.
*   **20:25 - 20:30:** Jaxen emphasizes that the technology is designed from its "inception" to circumvent informed consent.
*   **20:30 - 20:38:** Del Bigtree introduces Aaron Siri, Esq., from the ICAN Legal Team.
*   **20:38 - 20:41:** Aaron Siri appears in a split screen with Del Bigtree. He is a bald man wearing glasses and a suit, speaking from Scottsdale, AZ. He starts by affirming Del Bigtree's previous points.
*   **20:41 - 21:00:** Siri discusses ICAN's legislative arm, "icanlegislate.org," which aims to change laws to protect rights, especially against self-spreading vaccines.
*   **21:00 - 21:21:** Siri highlights the core issue: unlike traditional drugs for sick people or typical vaccines for healthy children, self-spreading vaccines affect everyone globally without consent. He states that if there's even "one time" they mess up, it's catastrophic. He also mentions the difficulty of testing such a product.
*   **21:21 - 21:49:** Siri concludes by emphasizing that this technology represents the "ultimate crushing of individual and civil rights," as it removes consent and is inherently designed to spread without individual choice. He warns that governments will suppress dissent and compel people to accept it, regardless of potential physical harm.

**Note:** The video has a time discrepancy in the audio between 00:31-00:44 and 00:53-01:08 where the original transcription might have missed some words. I have reviewed and corrected this to reflect the actual spoken words and their timing with the visuals. There were also instances of the speaker's audio overlapping with visual transitions, which I've attempted to clarify in the description.

The LD50 of heroin is 21.8mg/kg. Heroin is roughly 1000x deadlier than sugar on a 1 to 1 basis.    https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Median_lethal_dose

caption.video0:
This video showcases the creation of an incredibly elaborate dessert tower.

Here's a detailed breakdown:

At **0:00**, the video begins by showing a golden-brown Belgian waffle resting on a white rectangular plate. A person, wearing clear plastic gloves and a black t-shirt with a "1987 Elm Street Diner" logo, uses a white squeeze bottle to drizzle generous zig-zag lines of a thick, brown substance, identified as **Nutella**, over the entire surface of the waffle.

By **0:02**, a second squeeze bottle, containing a lighter, creamy brown liquid, is used to drizzle **peanut butter sauce** over the Nutella, creating alternating stripes of the two sauces.

At **0:04**, a small black measuring cup is used to sprinkle a colorful mixture of orange and yellow **Reese's Pieces**, along with chocolate chips and chocolate sprinkles, over the waffle.

From **0:05** to **0:09**, the person, now also wearing a black glove on their right hand, meticulously adds five large scoops of vanilla ice cream onto the loaded waffle using an ice cream scoop. The scoops are carefully positioned to cover most of the waffle.

At **0:09**, the Nutella squeeze bottle reappears, adding more thick Nutella drizzle over the ice cream scoops.

By **0:12**, more Reese's Pieces and chocolate sprinkles are added, scattering over the newly drizzled Nutella and ice cream.

At **0:13**, a darker chocolate sauce is generously drizzled from another squeeze bottle, completing the first layer of toppings.

At **0:15**, a second Belgian waffle is carefully placed on top of the heavily loaded first waffle, creating a multi-layered base for the tower.

Immediately after, from **0:16** to **0:24**, the topping process for the second waffle begins. It gets another round of **Nutella**, then **peanut butter sauce**, followed by more **Reese's Pieces**, and finally, more dark **chocolate sauce**.

At **0:25**, a slice of chocolate peanut butter cake is carefully positioned on top of the second waffle, adding another substantial layer to the growing dessert.

The scene then shifts at **0:26** to a preparation area where five small, round mini pancakes are laid out on white paper. The person drizzles a swirl of **Nutella** onto each pancake.

By **0:30**, a scattering of **Reese's Pieces** and chocolate sprinkles is added to the Nutella on each mini pancake.

From **0:32** to **0:37**, the person demonstrates how each pancake is folded in half, creating a "taco" shape, and then carefully threaded onto a long wooden skewer. All five pancake tacos are stacked onto the skewer.

At **0:37**, the skewer of mini pancake tacos is placed upright on top of the chocolate cake slice on the main waffle tower, creating a towering vertical element.

At **0:39**, a chocolate cupcake, still in its paper liner, is added to the top of the tower, and a tiny jar of **Nutella** is placed on top of the cupcake.

From **0:40** to **0:42**, a large syringe filled with **peanut butter sauce** is inserted into the pancake skewer, suggesting an interactive element.

At **0:42**, a chocolate-dipped ice cream cone, coated with nuts or peanut butter chips, is placed on the other side of the pancake skewer.

More dark **chocolate sauce** is drizzled over the entire impressive structure at **0:44**.

A dollop of **whipped cream** is added to the top at **0:46**.

And finally, more **Reese's Pieces** are sprinkled over the whipped cream and the very top of the dessert at **0:48**.

At **0:49**, a final cascade of **Nutella** is poured over the entire towering creation.

The video concludes at **0:50** with a full shot of the massive "Nutella peanut butter waffle tower" as mentioned in the audio, reportedly from Elm Street Diner in Stamford, Connecticut. The person then uses a knife and fork to cut into the towering dessert, revealing its rich, layered interior of waffles, ice cream, sauces, and toppings.

summary: A Nurse had told the patient the previous day to get the injection from the casualty ward since OPD will be closed due to public holiday.  But the CMO refused them and did not direct them to another place.  Finally they got the injection in Orthopaedic Ward.    https://x.com/visrane/status/1931385919267070353?t=fpA61wRTC1oj0HcrD7fzBQ&s=19
tweetText:
A patient walked into the casualty at GMC Goa demanding Inj Vit B12. B12 is not an emergency, it can be taken anywhere, even after days of delay. The CMO allegedly directed the patient to a CHC.

Turns out, the patient was related to a journalist with direct access to Goa Health Min Vishwajit Rane. And then began the circus.

Rane barged into the OPD like some low-budget Bollywood cop, dragging along his staff and a camera crew, publicly summoned the CMO, a respected doctor with years of public service and teaching, humiliated him in front of juniors, threatened him, and suspended him on the spot, without even bothering to hear his side.

Now this minister has the gall to say he was “fighting for the poor”? Please. No poor person has the luxury of calling up a health minister directly. As per the media reports, the patient was a journalist’s relative, not some underprivileged soul struggling for access.

Even if, for the sake of argument, the doctor had erred, there are official procedures to follow: hold an inquiry, issue a show-cause notice, let him respond, cross exam, and only then take disciplinary action if needed. What is this sadakchhap behavior? Learn to uphold the dignity of office instead of turning governance into a media circus.

Trust me, if this had happened in Bengal, most of those defending Rane would have been howling for Mamata Banerjee’s accountability. But since it’s BJP and Goa, chalta hai.

This isn’t done, Shri @narendramodi. This is not how elected officials are supposed to behave.

caption.video0:
The video opens in what appears to be a hospital hallway. A man in a light blue kurta and glasses, with a red mark on his forehead, is walking while speaking on a phone (0:00-0:03). He is followed by several other men, some in uniform that resembles security, and other hospital staff. On the right, patients or their relatives are seated.

At **0:03**, the man in the kurta stops and turns, looking stern. He asks, "Who's the CMO here? CMO कोण रे? (Who is the CMO?)" (0:03-0:05). He continues to demand, "CMO कोण? (Who is the CMO?)" (0:06-0:07). A female doctor in a white coat and mask approaches him, looking concerned. He asks, "Who's the CMO? Where is the CMO? Where is he?" (0:08-0:09). He then instructs someone to "Call him" (0:10-0:11). The man then walks into a hospital office/reception area, sits down at a desk, and continues to ask for the CMO (0:18-0:25).

A doctor in a white lab coat and blue mask approaches and stands in front of the seated man in the kurta (0:29). The official then begins to scold the doctor intensely. Speaking in Konkani, he says (translated to English): "He is talking about surgery, right? He came now. How long has he been here? Are you the CMO here? Are you the CMO? You are the CMO. Then why did you say this patient has to be given an injection. You don't know how to guide him properly? You should have done something. How much are you going to talk about this? You should have spoken to the patient properly. You told me to go to an urban health center. Then what are you doing here? Why did you take a government job? You don't know how to talk to people. You should be ashamed. You have to be careful. I will issue a suspension order. And kick you out from here. These doctors don't understand anything. They don't understand that they are here to serve humanity. You are not here with your bloody ego. I am telling you, listen to what I am saying. You are not authorized to speak when I am speaking. You please go. I will call another CMO. I will issue the note for suspension. You get out from here. That's it. Out from here. And don't report back as long as I am here. Not in your time. You are a doctor to serve the poor people here." (0:24-0:53).

The doctor attempts to speak (0:51-0:52), but the official immediately cuts him off, stating, "I am suspending you. When the inquiry is initiated, you give your explanation. I want him relieved right now from here. Ask him to get out from here" (0:52-0:56). He continues, "You understand one thing, you are dealing with me. I am telling you, first learn to shut up when I am talking to you. You can be anything. You want to file a case? Go and file a case. But I am telling you, you are suspended from right now. And you go when the investigation starts. You give your explanation, that time I will consider whether I want to take you back or not. Otherwise, next two years you will be suspended, and you will remain as long as my tenure is. Poor people are to be served" (0:56-1:09).

The doctor again tries to speak (1:09-1:10), but the official cuts him off once more: "I am asking, telling you only this thing, that poor people have to be served. I don't want to listen to you. When I make up my mind, I don't want to listen, I don't want to listen. I am suspending you from this moment, go home. Before my blood pressure boils and I take some other action on you. You will not be in service of... You go from whatever, I also work for morning to night. I want you out from here" (1:10-1:20). The doctor attempts to speak a third time (1:20), but the official points his finger and shouts, "Don't open your mouth when I am talking! Send him right now. And issue him a direction that he should not report, I'm suspending him right now" (1:20-1:23).

The video opens with an animated news channel ident featuring the WESH 2 NEWS logo, a colorful (rainbow segmented) "2" behind the text, with sparkling effects. This transitions to "WESH.com" on a blue background with a glowing orange circle. Upbeat, dramatic news theme music plays throughout this segment.

At 0:03, the scene switches to a news studio with two anchors, a man (Jim Payne) and a woman (Martha Sugalski), sitting at a desk. A large screen behind them displays a stylized globe. A lower third graphic reads "DEADLY FLU SHOT?" with the WESH 2 logo. The male anchor begins, "A recent high school graduate is dead and his mother is blaming the flu shot." The female anchor adds, "Yeah, even his doctors can't agree on exactly what killed him. Jean Kennedy has this heartbreaking story."

From 0:12, the video focuses on Lori Webb, a woman with blonde hair, wearing a red top. She is visibly distressed and crying, covering her mouth with her hand. A lower third graphic identifies her as "LORI WEBB CHANDLER'S MOTHER" with the WESH 2 logo. She tearfully states, "You can't describe." As the video continues (0:18), the graphic updates to "DEADLY FLU SHOT? MOM BLAMES FLU SHOT FOR SON'S DEATH SALT LAKE CITY, UT." Lori sobs, "How hard it is to lose a child."

At 0:22, Lori Webb is shown sitting in a chair, talking with her hands. A male reporter's voiceover states, "Lori Webb has to do what no mother should." She is then shown crying again (0:25), rubbing her eyes, and emotionally says, "I hate that I'm... I'm... that I have to bury my son."

A photo of a smiling young man (Chandler Webb) in a dark suit with a green vest and bow tie appears at 0:31. The voiceover explains, "A month ago, 19-year-old Chandler Webb was his typical happy and healthy self." This is followed by another photo (0:36) of Chandler with two other young men, all smiling and wearing white shirts and ties, possibly in a car. The voiceover continues, "Gearing up for an LDS mission, he went to his Sandy pediatrician for a physical."

An exterior shot of a brick building, presumably the pediatrician's office, is shown at 0:38. The voiceover repeats, "for a physical."

From 0:40, Lori Webb is back on screen, speaking. She states, "This was his first flu shot, he'd never had one before." A photo of Chandler in a red t-shirt and light shorts, standing in front of a door, is displayed at 0:42. The voiceover informs us, "And less than 24 hours later, he became violently ill." Lori Webb then details his symptoms (0:45), "He said he's never shook so hard in his life. He had the worst headache, throw up."

Nighttime exterior shots of the Intermountain Medical Center in Murray (0:49) are shown, including a hospital sign pointing to "EMERGENCY." The voiceover explains, "On October 23rd, he ended up at IMC in Murray with a team of six doctors trying to understand why the 19-year-old was so sick."

Lori Webb appears again (0:53), recounting the extensive medical tests performed: "They checked every virus, every bacteria, every fungus." At 0:58, she is shown sitting with her arms crossed. The voiceover states, "The tests came back negative. Lori says doctors dismissed the flu vaccine, only one said it was a possibility." A photo of Chandler and two other young men smiling is shown again (1:06). The voiceover adds, "By that point, Chandler's brain swelled. He slipped into a coma he would never wake from."

The Intermountain Medical Center sign is shown again at night (1:10). Lori Webb tearfully describes her son's suffering (1:13): "He was 19, he was he was so happy. I hate what he went through in the hospital." She continues to cry (1:19), saying, "When you're in a coma, you still suffer."

From 1:23, Dr. Allyn Nakashima, identified by a lower third graphic as "DR. ALLYN NAKASHIMA UTAH STATE EPIDEMIOLOGIST," speaks. She acknowledges, "It certainly is a tragedy." The reporter's voiceover states (1:26), "Dr. Allyn Nakashima is the State Epidemiologist and is aware of Chandler's case." An exterior shot of the "Department of Health" building is seen at dusk/night (1:29). The voiceover explains, "Health officials say as far as they know, there's never been a death in Utah due to the flu vaccine."

Dr. Nakashima then states (1:34), "But Dr. Nakashima says it is possible." She elaborates on the rare occurrences: "We certainly have seen associations, uh, of encephalitis, or encephalitis-type illnesses following flu vaccine. Uh, it's very rare. And so, um, you know, we can't necessarily say there's a cause and effect here. We can say there's an association."

A close-up of a flu vaccine syringe on a white surface is shown at 1:57. The voiceover says, "If the vaccine took Chandler's life, Dr. Nakashima says it's one of those extremely rare cases." A hand is then shown holding a syringe (2:00), followed by a person receiving an injection in their arm (2:02). The voiceover emphasizes, "And she hopes it doesn't discourage the public from getting flu shots." Dr. Nakashima concludes (2:05) by highlighting the benefits of the vaccine: "We have, uh, probably anywhere from 6,000 to 50,000 deaths per year due to flu in this country every year. And, um, many of those could be saved by uh, administration of flu vaccine, so, uh, at this point we wouldn't change those recommendations."

The video ends back in the news studio (2:26) with male anchor Jim Payne. The lower third graphic reads "LOCAL. LIVE. LATE BREAKING. JIM PAYNE WESH 2 NEWS." He states, "That was Jean Kennedy with our report there. You saw the numbers that she gave. Federal regulators believe that the vaccine saves enough lives that they can confidently recommend it."

The video features Angus Dalgleish, identified by on-screen text as a Professor of Oncology at St George's, speaking remotely from Cheam, England, UK. He is a man with light-colored, swept-back hair, wearing a blue long-sleeved top over a plaid shirt. He is seated in a black office chair, speaking directly to the camera. The background shows a light-colored wall with a framed picture on the left and a bed with a patterned duvet visible on the right.

Professor Dalgleish discusses a significant and dramatic increase in cancer rates observed globally over the last two to three years. He states that a "slow increase that was coming up has started to take off," citing reports of up to a 40% increase in some places in America. He explains that overall, the increase ranges from 2% to 20% in cancers among young people, depending on the population. He emphasizes that this increase is "real" and a continuing problem that is "not going away anytime soon."

He raises the question of whether "something else that is stimulating this" is causing the acceleration in the last two years. He differentiates between an "initiator of cancer" (like genetic issues) that can lie dormant, and a "promoter of cancer," which is an "irritation" that allows cancer to "escape immune control" or "fuel it" so it "takes off." He suggests that while genetic issues might manifest later in life (e.g., at 40 or 50), the recent rapid acceleration points to a new promoter.

Professor Dalgleish explicitly states that this increase "correlates with the rollout of the COVID vaccination," particularly in the discussed age group. He recounts that he went public with his concerns about the booster vaccine causing "marked immune perturbation," leading to the "escape of previously well-controlled melanomas." He notes that since he raised these concerns over a year ago, others have reported seeing "exactly the same," not just with melanoma, but also with "leukemias, lymphomas, renal cell cancer, and all abdominal" cancers. He concludes by advocating for an official worldwide investigation into this sudden increase in cancers among young people.

The video opens with Tucker Carlson speaking directly to the camera. He is wearing a dark suit with a striped tie, standing against a blue background that features a blurred image of the Capitol dome on the left. A Fox News Live banner is at the bottom of the screen, along with the text "DRAMATIC INCREASE IN YOUNG ATHLETES COLLAPSING" and "TUCKER CARLSON TONIGHT". In the upper right corner, a smaller inset video shows an ambulance parked on a football field, with the text "DISTURBING TREND" overlaid on it.

Tucker Carlson begins speaking at 0:00, stating, "So, as we've said multiple times, and it's worth saying because it's a sign of respect for the man tonight in the hospital. We don't know exactly why Damar Hamlin collapsed last night. But at the same time, there are concerns about cases like this. Young athletes collapsing on the field of heart problems."

At 0:20, he continues, "And there has been a dramatic increase in this, you're not imagining it. So what is this? Cardiologist Peter McCullough and researcher P. Kourtidis looked into this trend in Europe, European sports leagues. They found that prior to COVID and the COVID-19 vaccines, there were roughly 29 cardiac arrests in those European sports leagues per year. Since the vax campaign began, there have been more than 1500 total cardiac arrests in those leagues, and two-thirds of those were fatal."

At 0:50, Tucker asks, "Does that prove something? We don't know. But you should know that. Dr. Peter McCullough is a cardiologist. He's also the author of Courage to Face COVID-19."

At 0:57, the screen transitions to a split view. Tucker Carlson is on the left, still against the Capitol dome background. On the right, Dr. Peter McCullough is shown, wearing a patterned suit jacket and a purple tie, against a plainer background. The overlay text changes to "DAMAR HAMLIN COLLAPSE IGNITES A HUGE CONVERSATION ABOUT YOUNG PEOPLE & HEART ISSUES" and "DR PETER MCCULLOUGH | CARDIOLOGIST" appears under his name.

Tucker introduces Dr. McCullough at 1:00, "He joins us tonight to assess. Doctor, thank you so much for coming on. So this is one of those phenomena that people who use social media are aware of because there are videos of it floating around. But I don't know when the last time I heard, if ever, an American public health authority addressed this directly and tell people what is this? Is this real? Tell us your findings from your actual study on this."

Dr. McCullough begins speaking at 1:25, "The concern here is that athletes at a professional level, Tucker, are carefully screened for underlying heart disease. The leading cause of sudden death on the playing field is hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. The athletes are screened for this. Uh, there are rare conditions, genetic abnormalities in heart rhythm, uh, that can present." The overlay text changes again at 1:29 to "CARDIOLOGIST ON MOUNTING CONCERNS THAT MORE YOUNG PEOPLE SEEM TO HAVE HEART ISSUES THESE DAYS."

Dr. McCullough continues at 1:47, "But sudden cardiac death should be a very unusual phenomenon, and as your report indicates, it's extremely unusual in the NFL since the high level of scrutiny. There's a differential diagnosis that doctors go through when this happens. I watched it live, and then I watched the replay as a cardiologist. To me, it looked like he made a hard tackle, there was head and neck, uh, impact."

At 2:12, the screen shifts to a three-way split. Tucker Carlson is on the left, Dr. McCullough in the middle, and on the right, video footage from the football game shows Damar Hamlin lying on the field, surrounded by medical staff and other players who are kneeling. Dr. McCullough's commentary continues as this visual appears, "But he got up, he clapped his hands, and then he fell over backwards. It looked like a primary cardiac arrest. Everything that was done on the field looked perfect. I think the duration of CPR and resuscitation..." The overlay text also updates to "CARDIOLOGIST: PLAYERS ARE CAREFULLY SCREENED BY DOCTORS TO MAKE SURE THEY'RE REALLY READY TO PLAY."

On the left side, "VivaBarnesLaw.Locals.Com" is displayed at the bottom, along with an image of the logo seen earlier. The speaker on the left is a man with curly brown hair, a beard, and glasses, wearing headphones and a black t-shirt with a phoenix-like emblem. His background includes a black and white image of palm trees and a sign that reads "VLAWG."
On the right side, "VIVA FREI" is displayed at the top. The speaker, identified as "Aseem" at the bottom, is a man with short graying hair and glasses, wearing a gray collared shirt. His background features a white bookshelf filled with books and several wine bottles on shelves, with a framed picture of a man in a suit visible above the books.

Aseem begins speaking at 0:02, stating, "But you asked me the question about the evidence of harm, and let me answer that first." He continues to explain that "a reanalysis was done by independent scientists, very eminent in their respective fields, statisticians." He mentions "Robert Kaplan from Stanford, the associate editor of the BMJ, Peter Doshi, University of Maryland, Joseph Fraiman, who's a lead author, who I've spoken to, a very good in data analysis." Aseem states that these individuals "were able to reanalyze Pfizer, Moderna's original trials that led to the approval." He emphasizes, "And this is the key point. That reanalysis showed that those original trials, you were more likely, David—so this is a double-blinded randomized control trial. So this is as close as we're going to get to accuracy of benefit and harm."

At 0:40, Aseem continues, "You were more likely to suffer a serious adverse event from taking the vaccine—hospitalization, disability, life-changing event—than you were to be hospitalized with COVID during the ancestral Wuhan strain, which was more severe. Think about that. That's what I've concluded. And nobody has challenged me on that so far. Nobody has been able to scientifically challenge me on that claim."

At 1:02, the left-screen speaker, David, asks, "Dr., is this—uh, I, this is the study that, one of the ones that I was looking at or that I had seen, and I, you know, I, I'm a new—" Aseem interjects at 1:09, "Yes, this is the one published in the Journal Vaccine, absolutely. This is."

At 1:03, the right side of the screen transitions to display a webpage from the "NIH National Library of Medicine," showing a PubMed.gov entry for a study titled "Serious adverse events of special interest following mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in randomized trials in adults." The authors listed include "Joseph Fraiman," "Juan Erviti," "Mark Jones," "Sander Greenland," "Patrick Whelan," "Robert M Kaplan," and "Peter Doshi."
At 1:12, the webpage scrolls down to reveal the abstract, which David refers to, noting it concludes "it was one in 800." Aseem confirms this, saying, "One serious adverse event per 800 doses," before quickly adding, "But that is again a likely underestimate, only because that's at two months."

The screen returns to the split-screen interview at 1:21. Aseem elaborates, "And what we know now, David, is that one of the mechanisms of harm which I've discovered and has been even concluded from autopsy studies, that several months after having the vaccine, it can cause a heart attack or sudden cardiac death. And I, I suspect a lot of these excess deaths that we're seeing around the world right now—it's not the only factor, but I think at the moment, until proven otherwise, it's the most likely, it's, it's probably the most significant factor right now."
He continues, "We can categorically say, because of the evidence that we've got from randomized control trials, from pharmacovigilance data, from observational studies, from autopsy data, from clinical data—and remember, most of these excess deaths are driven by cardiovascular disease traditionally. You know, there may be other excess deaths, suicides or whatever, but most of the bulk of these, the most common are going to be heart disease. For me, until proven otherwise, the primary driver of excess deaths right now is the vaccine."

At 2:14, the video transitions to a black screen with the word "SUBSCRIBE" in a white, distressed font. Upbeat instrumental music with a driving beat plays until the video ends at 2:21.

caption.video0:
The video opens with a female news anchor of the BBC News program, seated at a desk with a glass of water and papers in front of her. She has short, dark hair, is wearing glasses, and a light pink jacket. The background is a vibrant newsroom with red and purple lighting and multiple screens showing various news feeds. The BBC News logo is visible at the bottom left, and the time "09:21" is displayed at the bottom right. A scrolling ticker reads: "UK economy grows by 0.1% in November, boosted by World Cup".

The anchor begins speaking: "Millions more people in England could be prescribed daily cholesterol-lowering statin pills to cut their risk of a heart attack or stroke, under new advice for the NHS."

At 0:10, the video cuts to a close-up of gloved hands handling white pills in a transparent blister pack, placing them onto a white tray with "COLOUR SHAPE" labels. The text ticker changes to: "A woman in her 20s has died after a dog attack in Caterham, Surrey".

At 0:15, the shot changes to a woman in a black top and white gloves, sorting small boxes of medication on a pharmacy shelf. The ticker updates: "PM Sunak holds 'constructive talks' with Nicola Sturgeon on Scotland visit".

At 0:20, the camera again focuses on gloved hands taking pills from a blister pack, one by one. The ticker remains the same.

At 0:26, a man's hands are shown adjusting a grey blood pressure cuff on his arm. His shirt is green with a white daisy pattern. The ticker changes to: "Lisa Marie Presley, Elvis Presley's only child, has died at the age of 54".

At 0:27, the shot tightens on an "OMRON 705IT" blood pressure monitor. The man's finger presses the "START" button. The display shows "SYS mmHg", "DIA mmHg", and "PULSE /min" along with numbers: "102" for SYS, and "15:2" below it. The ticker remains the same.

At 0:30, the screen splits into two. The news anchor is on the right, looking towards a large screen on her left. On that screen, Dr. Aseem Malhotra, a consultant cardiologist, is visible in a video call. He has grey hair, is wearing a light blue shirt, and is seated in front of a white bookshelf. The BBC News logo is prominent at the bottom. A new banner appears: "New NHS statins advice" and "Around 15m more people in England could be prescribed daily statin pills". The anchor introduces him: "Well, I'm joined now by Dr. Aseem Malhotra. He is a consultant cardiologist and specialises in statins. Thank you for joining us this morning, doctor. Um, so in terms of what Nice are saying, your thoughts on that?"

At 0:42, Dr. Malhotra is shown in a closer shot. He smiles and begins his response: "Yeah, so I have great respect for Nice, but I think in this regard with statins, I think this guidance is misguided." The banner underneath changes to "Dr Aseem Malhotra Consultant Cardiologist".

At 0:51, the screen splits again. Dr. Malhotra is on the left, and a close-up of the blood pressure cuff being applied is on the right. The ticker underneath updates with "New NHS statins advice" and "Statins are cholesterol-lowering pills taken to reduce risk of heart attack or stroke". Dr. Malhotra continues his argument, stating that statins offer the greatest benefit to high-risk people who have already had a heart attack. He then says that for people with a less than 10-20% risk of heart attack or stroke in the next 10 years, there is no "mortality benefit" (they won't live any longer over a 5-year period) and the "absolute benefit" is about 1 in 100 in preventing a non-fatal heart attack or stroke. He stresses the need for "informed decision-making" with patients.

At 1:22, the blood pressure monitor shows its final reading: "SYS 135 mmHg", "DIA 80 mmHg", and "PULSE 70 /min". The ticker text changes to: "Debate about how widely treatment should be given". Dr. Malhotra adds that many patients, when given this information, ask if there's "anything else I can do".

At 1:47, the screen splits to show Dr. Malhotra on the left and a series of ambulances parked outside a hospital on the right. The text ticker changes to: "NICE: People should be thoroughly assessed before statins prescribed". He explains that "the big issue really... is that we've not been addressing unhealthy lifestyles, or certainly being effective in curbing unhealthy lifestyles". He mentions "the obesity epidemic".

At 2:01, the ambulance shot changes to an ambulance driving on a road. Dr. Malhotra continues, "And when you look at heart disease, my own research has found this, is that the best way to prevent heart disease, and even manage people with heart disease, whether or not they choose to take a statin, is actually through lifestyle changes." The ambulance is shown from the back, driving away, then a close-up of paramedics moving a stretcher into an ambulance. The name "NORTH WEST AMBULANCE SERVICE" is visible on the side of the ambulance.

At 2:25, the screen splits again, showing Dr. Malhotra on the left and a close-up of pills in a blister pack, then a gloved hand taking out pills. The ticker changes to: "Millions in UK already take statins despite there being side effects". Dr. Malhotra clarifies lifestyle changes: "It means cutting out ultra-processed foods... eating real food basically. Moderate exercise, at least 30 minutes brisk walk a day, and also the other big sort of elephant in the room is stress reduction."

At 2:42, the video cuts to a wide shot of a doctor's waiting room, with patients seated. The ticker changes to: "Debate about how widely treatment should be given". Dr. Malhotra discusses "excess deaths" and the British Heart Foundation's report of 30,000 "excess deaths specifically due to coronary artery disease". He then mentions Chris Whitty's suggestion that it could be due to people not being prescribed statins or blood pressure pills, but dismisses this: "But, Carl Heneghan, that, that unfortunately, I have great respect for Chris Whitty, but I think he's mistaken here, because analysis I've done, and even Carl Heneghan, the director of Centre for Evidence Based Medicine Oxford, suggests that statin pills prescription hasn't reduced since pandemic. So it's unlikely to be a cause."

At 3:15, Dr. Malhotra continues: "But what is almost certainly, and if you'll allow me to say this, what my own research has found, and this is something that is probably a likely contributing factor, is that the Covid MRNA vaccines do carry a cardiovascular risk." He references his father, who suffered a cardiac arrest at home and died after an ambulance delay. He says his post-mortem found "very severe coronary artery disease, which is unexplainable". He states that he published his findings in a peer-reviewed journal, and they accepted his conclusion that the likely cause of his father's death was "two doses of the Pfizer MRNA vaccine he had six months earlier".

At 4:06, the anchor, visibly concerned, interrupts: "Can I just... I just want to jump in on that point. So what you're saying, in terms of the MRNA link to cardiovascular risk, is that, that is a proven, it's been proven medically, has it, scientifically?"

At 4:18, Dr. Malhotra replies: "Yeah, yeah. So in medicine, you know, it, in any research that we do, it's very difficult to always be absolutely conclusive. So we go on likelihoods. And there are lots of data now, the highest quality data from randomized controlled trials. So there was a re-analysis in the Journal Vaccine published recently where they found that the original trials of Pfizer and Moderna with MRNA vaccines showed, and I just want to put this in absolute terms, because you don't want to scare people unnecessarily. But the absolute risk of serious adverse events was at least one in 800."

At 4:50, he corrects himself, "one in 100", and continues, "and a lot of those are cardiovascular. And then you were more likely in those trials to suffer a serious adverse event than to be hospitalised with COVID early on. And I think the vaccine has certainly helped people who are high risk, but now, we should be reassured the Omicron, and what's circulating is really no worse than the flu, and this is really time to pause the vaccine rollout and to really investigate this problem."

At 5:10, the anchor clarifies, "Okay. Um, so I just want to clarify one, one point here, is that is using statins a cheaper, less resistant, but short-sighted approach to lower the risk?"

At 5:26, Dr. Malhotra agrees: "I think you've summed it up perfectly. So it doesn't mean statins don't have a role, but the absolute benefits are very marginal in low-risk people." He goes on to say he would advise patients to cut out ultra-processed foods and engage in regular activity, and that this type of dietary change can bring very quick improvements in risk factors for heart disease. He advocates for public health education campaigns, suggesting that if a food "comes out of a packet, and it has five or more ingredients, don't eat it". He reiterates that lifestyle changes offer the "biggest impact to reducing heart disease".

At 7:03, the anchor thanks him: "Okay, Dr. Malhotra, thank you very much, a very in-depth explanation at the arguments for and against. Thank you for that." She smiles and turns back to her papers as the video concludes.

The video opens with a medium shot of a blonde woman wearing a blue sweater and a gold-colored, draped scarf. She has fair skin, blue eyes, and her hair is styled in a bob. She is looking directly at the camera and speaking. A yellow circular logo with "NZL" in black text is visible in the top right corner of the screen. In the background, part of a room is visible, including a door on the left, a red rug on the floor, and a dark armchair with a red cushion on the right, next to a wooden headboard.

At 0:00, the woman says, "I cannot put into words the courage of this man, whom I will now introduce. He's willing to have his face on screen. I have always referred to him by his nickname, which is ironically Winston Smith. I do not know his real name, and I am happy with that. Welcome, Winston."

At 0:21, the camera angle shifts slightly to the right, revealing a man sitting beside her. He has white hair and a white beard, is wearing glasses, and a light blue or white button-down shirt. He looks at the woman, then briefly at the camera, then back at the woman. His facial expression appears a bit pained or nervous.

The woman then asks, "Could you tell me what brought you to this point of such courage?"

At 0:27, the man, visibly emotional and speaking with a trembling voice, begins to respond: "I, um, it's because I love humanity so much is, uh, is the reason." He pauses, his voice catching, and he clears his throat. The woman looks at him with a supportive expression. He continues, "And, um, we are so precious, every single one of us." His voice is still shaky, and he looks down and to the side, blinking as if trying to hold back tears.

At 0:40, the man says, "So, can we go again?" He looks away from the camera. The woman softly says, "Just keep, just keep pressing on." She gestures reassuringly with her hand. The man says, "All right, all right. So I'll start again, shall I? I've just gotta re-center my system." He takes a deep breath. The woman encourages him, "Just keep going, just keep going. Just." The man then murmurs, "Just taking off the table, too, cuz it's..." He seems to be fidgeting. The woman interjects, "All right, okay, yeah, yeah." He nods, still looking down.

At 0:56, the woman prompts him again, "Let's, let's do it again." The man responds, "My voice is all over the place." The woman replies, "It's beautiful. It's beautiful. And if you look at me, it's gonna calm you a little bit, because looking at the camera, it, it makes, just look at me and be with me." She gestures with her hand towards herself. The man looks up at her, briefly making eye contact, then down again. He says, "All right. Yeah." The woman reiterates, "Yeah. Look at me and be with me." He looks at her again. He states, "Okay. Yeah. No, I'm all right." He takes another deep breath. The woman reassures him, "It's just your voice." He clarifies, "It's not tiredness, it's just my voice."

At 1:12, the woman says, "It's beautiful. Right. Um, take two nice breaths." She demonstrates by taking a visible breath. The man takes a breath too. He starts to speak, "My reason why is because, um..." The woman interrupts gently, "Just, just, and, and just take a look at me. I can ask the question again." The man nods. The woman then formally says, "Welcome, Winston Smith, which is the name I know you as." The man nods again.

At 1:26, as the woman is about to continue, "Welcome, Winston Smith. That's what I'm going to call you," the video transitions. The man's face, though still faintly visible as a transparent overlay, is replaced by a circular profile picture of a different man. This man has a short, reddish-blonde beard and mustache, a clean-shaven head, and is wearing glasses. Below the circular image, white text on a black background reads "Barry Young" and beneath that, "Bank of New Zealand." The audio of the woman saying, "That's what I'm going to call you," concludes, followed by the man's faint "Yeah."

The screen remains on the "Barry Young, Bank of New Zealand" profile picture until the end of the video at 1:34.

The video captures a celebratory moment following a significant legislative event, featuring Barack Obama and Joe Biden, set to an overlaid acoustic song.

Here's a detailed breakdown:

**0:00-0:01:** The video opens with a shot of Joe Biden (white-haired, dark suit, blue patterned tie) seated at a long, highly polished, dark wood conference table, looking to his left. To his right, Barack Obama (white shirt, red and white striped tie, dark trousers) is also seated at the table, looking slightly to his right. Both have their hands clasped in front of them. Several men in suits are standing behind them. The room has light-colored walls, and various flags are visible in the background, including the American flag.
*   **Audio:** A gentle, fingerpicked acoustic guitar tune begins. A male vocalist starts singing, "And I wanna kiss..."

**0:01-0:05:** The camera slightly pans to the right, revealing more people standing behind Obama and Biden, some clapping softly. Biden subtly gestures with his right hand as he watches something off-camera.
*   **Audio:** "...you, make you feel all right."

**0:05-0:09:** The camera continues panning right, focusing on a large flat-screen television embedded in a dark wood cabinet. The screen displays a C-SPAN broadcast with the heading "U.S. HOUSE HEALTH CARE LIVE 10:48 pm ET." Below that, it reads "HOUSE PASSES SENATE BILL AHEAD - Vote on Reconciliation Bill." Vote counts are displayed: "DEMOCRATIC 219 YEA / 194 NAY / 0 PRES NV," "REPUBLICAN 0 YEA / 178 NAY / 0 PRES NV," "INDEPENDENT 0 YEA / 0 NAY / 0 PRES NV." The "TOTALS" are "219 YEA / 208 NAY." "TIME REMAINING: 0:44." A man in a light blue shirt and glasses is standing to the left of the screen, watching it.
*   **Audio:** "I'm just so tired to share my nights."

**0:09-0:12:** The camera quickly pans back to Obama and Biden. Most of the people in the room are now standing and applauding more vigorously. Obama stands up, clapping and smiling, looking in Biden's direction. Biden is also standing and clapping. An Instagram handle "@dream.foramerica" appears on the bottom left of the screen.
*   **Audio:** "I wanna cry, and I wanna love..."

**0:12-0:17:** The camera focuses on Obama, who is standing, clapping enthusiastically, and beaming. Biden is to his left, also clapping and smiling broadly.
*   **Audio:** "...all my tears have been used up."

**0:17-0:20:** Obama turns towards Biden, extends his hand, and they shake hands firmly. Their smiles are wide, indicating shared joy.
*   **Audio:** "Oh, on another love..."

**0:20-0:25:** Obama and Biden embrace in a warm hug, patting each other on the back. People around them continue to clap and cheer. After a brief hug, they separate slightly, still holding hands and smiling, before embracing again.
*   **Audio:** "...another love. All my tears have been used up."

**0:25-0:30:** Biden and Obama separate fully. Obama then turns to his left and greets another man (wearing a dark suit, possibly Rahm Emanuel) with a handshake and an embrace. Both men are smiling and laughing.
*   **Audio:** "Oh, on another love..."

**0:30-0:34:** Obama and the other man continue their embrace, both looking very happy and engaged in conversation.
*   **Audio:** "...another love. All my tears have been used up."

**0:34-0:39:** Obama pulls back from the hug, still smiling and gesturing with his hands as he looks at the people around him. The crowd continues to clap and look excited.
*   **Audio:** "Oh, on another love..."

**0:39-0:44:** Obama then greets and hugs another man in a dark suit to his left. A woman with auburn hair and a beige top is visible clapping in the foreground, also smiling.
*   **Audio:** "...another love. All my tears have been used up."

**0:44-0:49:** Obama continues hugging the man. Another woman with lighter hair joins the hug, making it a group embrace. Everyone appears genuinely happy.
*   **Audio:** "Oh, on another love..."

**0:49-0:52:** The camera cuts back to the C-SPAN screen, still showing the final vote tally: "TOTALS 219 YEA / 208 NAY."
*   **Audio:** "...another love. All my tears have been used up."

**0:52-0:55:** The scene returns to Obama, who is vigorously clapping, a triumphant smile on his face. He is surrounded by applauding staff and colleagues.
*   **Audio:** Instrumental segment.

**0:55-0:59:** Obama stops clapping, points emphatically towards the C-SPAN screen with a wide smile, then turns and moves to greet more people.
*   **Audio:** Faint vocalization "Oh..." continues with the acoustic guitar.

**0:59-1:04:** Obama is seen shaking hands and briefly hugging a woman with dark hair (possibly Valerie Jarrett), then greets another man. The celebrating continues as the video ends.
*   **Audio:** The acoustic guitar music continues, gradually fading out.

The video captures the emotional and joyous reaction of President Obama and his team, particularly Vice President Biden, to the passage of a significant healthcare bill, highlighted by their handshakes, hugs, and enthusiastic applause, all against the backdrop of the live voting results. The overlaid song adds a poignant and celebratory atmosphere to the moment.

caption.video0:
The video features a man with a backward black baseball cap speaking directly to the camera, interspersed with various text and image backgrounds that support his narrative.

**0:00-0:07**: The video opens with the speaker stating, "So there are reports out of China now that scientists have found a cure for diabetes, and the American pharmaceutical industry is not happy about it." The background displays a Medium article with the title "Communist China Just Cured Diabetes and America's Insulin Industry is Not Happy About it" and the subtitle "Groundbreaking Diabetes Cure Developed by Chinese Scientists." Below the article text, an image of an insulin pen with a drop of liquid at the needle tip is visible.

**0:07-0:21**: The speaker continues, "There are now examples of Chinese patients receiving this experimental stem cell therapy and after about 11 weeks no longer requiring insulin. Which I imagine is something that sounds amazing to people with diabetes, no longer having to poke yourself." The background shifts to an article from "THE TIMES OF INDIA" titled "'Breakthrough in diabetes treatment: Chinese researchers successfully cure patient'." An accompanying image shows a gloved hand holding a blood glucose meter displaying "105.Z".

**0:21-0:30**: As the speaker says, "But it doesn't sound amazing to giant American pharmaceutical companies like Eli Lilly, who currently dominate the insulin market and use it to make billions of dollars," the background shows text detailing the "Impact on major insulin manufacturers" and "Insulin price gouging controversies." The text specifically mentions Novo Nordisk, Sanofi, and Eli Lilly dominating the global insulin market and the potential for the Chinese cell therapy to "severely impact their insulin sales and revenues." It also notes that if the therapy disrupts the market, manufacturers might "further increase insulin prices for the remaining patient population to offset revenue losses." A partial image of a book cover is also visible.

**0:30-0:47**: The speaker elaborates, "And this has been very controversial as they've regularly increased the price of insulin, knowing that it's a necessary commodity that people with diabetes need to survive. And this is extra disgusting because the inventor of insulin, Frederick Banting, originally sold the patent for $1, saying that insulin doesn't belong to me, it belongs to the world." The background displays a graphic stating: "Frederick Banting discovered insulin in 1921. He sold the patent for only $1." Below this, it reads: "By 2016, drugmakers had raised the price of insulin to $450 per month, on average. We need Medicare for All now. Bernie."

**0:47-0:56**: The speaker reiterates, "And if people can be cured of diabetes so they no longer need insulin, obviously that would really cut into the demand for this product and cut into their profits." The background returns to the previous article text, showing phrases like "global diabetes population can be cured" and the potential to "disrupt the insulin business altogether."

**0:56-1:11**: The speaker asserts, "And here we see the contradictory nature of the for-profit American healthcare system. Innovation and advancements in medical technology are actually disincentivized if they have the potential to cure the patient and make it so they're no longer a repeat customer for the pharmaceutical industry." The background shows a page from the "Journal of American Socialist Studies" titled "Edward Liger Smith - The State of American Healthcare: A Product and Reflection of American Capitalism." An image on the page depicts syringes and a bottle of white pills. The visible text discusses the healthcare system's commodification and how a broken system can exist where "nobody seems to be able to fix it."

**1:11-1:19**: Concluding his argument, the speaker states, "So much for capitalism promoting innovation and socialism destroying it. In reality, it's the opposite." The background reverts to the initial Medium article title, "Communist China Just Cured Diabetes and America's Insulin Industry is Not Happy About it," and the image of the insulin pen.

caption.video0:
The video captures an elderly individual, possibly a woman, attempting to climb over a tall, black metal gate.

At **00:00**, the person, dressed in a maroon top and dark pants, is already midway through climbing the gate. Their hands are gripping the top edge, and their right leg is bent with the foot placed on one of the horizontal bars of the gate. The gate itself has an intricate design with vertical bars and decorative elements, including a golden emblem on the left panel. The background shows a grassy area with some foliage and what appears to be houses or buildings in the distance. The audio features a rhythmic, electronic beat that sounds like a fast "ba-ba-ba-ba" loop.

By **00:02**, the person shifts their weight, bringing their left leg up to join the right on the horizontal bar. They are now crouched on the gate, attempting to hoist themselves further over. The rhythmic audio continues.

At **00:04**, they manage to get their body almost horizontal over the top of the gate, with their chest and stomach pressed against the upper edge. Their legs are dangling on the side of the gate facing away from the camera. The audio maintains its rapid beat.

Around **00:06**, the person is fully on top of the gate, lying prone across its width. Their head is visible on the far side, looking down, and their hands are still gripping the top edge. The rhythmic beat transitions into a more pronounced, slightly distorted "bam-pam-pam-pam-pam" sound, almost like a comedic drum beat.

From **00:08** to **00:10**, they slowly push themselves forward, gradually moving their body completely over the gate. Their legs are now clearly visible on the other side, clad in dark pants and pink shoes. The "bam-pam-pam-pam-pam" sound continues, emphasizing each movement.

Finally, at **00:12**, the person successfully drops to the other side of the gate, disappearing from view. The gate remains in the foreground, now empty. The audio concludes with a final "pam-pam."

caption.video0:
The video details a discovery made by Israeli soldiers regarding Hamas's alleged use of a hospital and an associated tunnel system.

At **0:00**, a soldier in a green military uniform, helmet, and tactical vest, carrying a rifle, stands next to an open electrical control panel. He points at the panel and explains, "We first saw the solar panels, then the electricity goes here." The panel contains various wires, circuit breakers, and a digital display. He continues, "And it goes down..."

At **0:05**, the camera follows the soldier's gaze downwards. An orange cable and other wires are seen disappearing into a hole in the ground, surrounded by debris. The soldier states, "directly to the tunnel."

At **0:06**, the camera focuses on the hole, showing various cables running into it.

At **0:08**, the camera transitions to looking down a deep, narrow, concrete-lined shaft or tunnel. White brick walls are visible, and several cables (red and black) run down the side, along with a metal ladder. The soldier's voiceover continues, "Now you can see the tunnel. You can see the tunnel. The tunnel is led down more than 20 meters down."

From **0:15** to **0:29**, the view remains focused on the dark, deep tunnel shaft. The soldier's voiceover adds, "The robot found a door, a door that is bulletproof, it's uh, it's explosive-proof. So it looks like a hard evidence, a clear evidence, that the hospital direction is connected..."

At **0:29**, the scene changes to the soldier kneeling on a rubble-strewn floor, holding up a piece of a shattered white tile, revealing a hidden opening beneath it. He points into the opening with his left hand while his rifle rests next to him. Another person's gloved hand is visible, holding a red cable. He explains, "This is a covered tunnel. It's part of the same floor, and it slides down here. So it's a covered tunnel, so nobody can find it."

At **0:37**, the soldier is standing amidst rubble and debris, pointing with his right hand towards a light green and beige building with external stairs in the distance. He identifies it, saying, "This is Rantisi Hospital."

At **0:40**, the camera zooms in on the Rantisi Hospital building, highlighting its architectural details.

At **0:43**, the soldier is again in the rubble, pointing in a different direction, implying the location of the tunnel entrance. He says, "And this is the place where I showed you the tunnel. I want you to see."

A brief shot of the widespread rubble is shown at **0:47**.

At **0:50**, the soldier stands in front of a damaged building, possibly the rear of the hospital, with noticeable holes in its light-brown wall. He gestures and states, "This is the back side of the hospital. Hamas used this hospital. Tonight we have entered into this building. I will show you the evidence, let's enter into the hospital." He wears an Israeli flag patch on his arm.

At **1:00**, the soldier, with his rifle, walks into a dimly lit, dark corridor inside the hospital. The walls appear bare and damaged, and debris is scattered on the floor. He gestures and explains, "We are now entering into the area of the hospital, where we had found the evidence. This operation was conducted by Israeli special unit, the Israeli Navy Seals. This is still an operation that is conducting. And I'm showing you the first evidence to see." Footsteps and movement sounds are heard.

At **1:20**, the soldier steps into a doorway. The camera reveals a room with light blue walls, decorated with children's drawings or artwork, including a heart and a colorful tree. On the floor in the background, numerous military rifles, ammunition, and other gear are laid out. The soldier gestures to the gear and states, "We are now, we are now in the area of the basement of the hospital. I want to show you a room where we found all the gear, the operational gear of Hamas. Hamas is using hospitals, like we showed the evidence in Shifa Hospital, in other hospital." He concludes the statement at **1:41**.

caption.video0:
The video features a split-screen interview on Fox News with a female host on the left and a male guest on the right.

**0:00 - 0:17:**
The left side of the screen shows Laura Ingraham, a blonde woman with earrings, wearing a dark, sleeveless top. She is seated and actively speaking, gesturing with her hands. The background behind her shows a blurred cityscape at night. The lower third of the screen initially displays "FOX NEWS" on the left, "5:48 MT" below it, and a banner that reads "TRUMP PROMOTES CBD USE ON TRUTH SOCIAL."
On the right side, Dr. Marc Siegel, a man with glasses, wearing a pinstriped suit and a purple patterned tie, is listening intently. The background behind him is an out-of-focus modern office or newsroom setting with blue and white lights.

Laura Ingraham states, "Knowing what we know now about the link in a certain percentage of especially young men between weed use and violent psychosis and other mental illness. Why would we encourage this at any level in our society?"

**0:17 - 0:24:**
The scene remains on the split screen. Laura Ingraham is now listening, and Dr. Marc Siegel begins to speak. The lower third banner changes to "SCHIZOPHRENIA AND POT, THE RISING CONNECTION." Below Dr. Siegel, his title appears: "DR MARC SIEGEL | FOX NEWS SENIOR MEDICAL ANALYST."
Dr. Siegel says, "We shouldn't. I consider cannabis to be the most dangerous drug in America right now, by the way..."

**0:24 - 0:30:**
The left side still shows Laura Ingraham listening. The right side switches to a file video showing a close-up of hands carefully rolling a marijuana joint. Green, dried plant material is visible as it's placed into rolling paper. A "FILE" tag is in the top right corner of this video segment.
Dr. Siegel continues, "...and I'm not trying to slight opioids here, but consider that SAMHSA, the agency on drug abuse..."

**0:30 - 0:34:**
The right side's file footage now shows a close-up of hands lighting and smoking a lit marijuana joint, with smoke rising. A person's hair and a blurred green background (possibly grass) are visible.
Dr. Siegel continues, "...which reported 7.6 million people going to ERs last year from drugs..."

**0:34 - 0:39:**
The right side's file footage changes to a close-up of hands holding a colorful, rainbow-striped bong, lighting the cannabis in its bowl with a white lighter. Smoke is visible.
Dr. Siegel says, "...puts now cannabis ahead of opioids, 12% cannabis, 11% opioids."

**0:39 - 0:43:**
The file footage on the right shows multiple trays or containers filled with harvested marijuana buds.
Dr. Siegel explains, "And that's because of what you just said, because people have underlying mental health disorders and they don't even know it."

**0:43 - 0:49:**
The file footage shows a person smoking a joint again, with a close-up of their lips and chin, exhaling smoke.
Dr. Siegel continues, "They're suffering from anxiety, depression, maybe a small percentage schizophrenia."

**0:49 - 0:54:**
The right side's file footage displays a clear plastic bag filled with marijuana buds being held up, followed by two glass jars labeled "SWEET WILLIAMS" and "ROMULAN," each containing dried marijuana.
Dr. Siegel states, "The first episode of psychosis that you have is accelerated, unfortunately..."

**0:54 - 0:59:**
The file footage shows a close-up of marijuana plants growing, with visible buds on the plant.
Dr. Siegel finishes, "...if you're a regular pot user or cannabis user."

The video opens with a medium shot of a woman with long, brown, wavy hair, seated on a dark brown wooden park bench.
