tweetText:
Prof @Kevin_Fong  giving the most devastating and moving testimony to the Covid Inquiry of visiting hospital intensive care units at the height of the second wave in late Dec 2020.  

The unimaginable scale of death, the trauma, the loss of hope. 
Please watch this 2min clip. https://t.co/bt1MLkj9je

caption.video0:
The video, taken from what appears to be a formal hearing or presentation, features a man in a dark suit and tie speaking at a desk. There are two microphones in front of him, and a glass carafe of water is visible. The background is a plain blue wall.

As the video progresses, the man occasionally touches his nose or wipes his eye, appearing emotional. His voice is heard describing the overwhelming experience of intensive care teams during COVID-19, particularly the scale of death they witnessed.

Here's a detailed timeline of his statements:

- **00:00-00:03**: "It's one of those things that is really very difficult."
- **00:03-00:10**: "...to capture and figures the scale of death experienced by the intensive care teams during COVID was unlike anything they had ever seen before."
- **00:10-00:21**: "They're no strangers to death. Uh, they are the Intensive Care Unit, they look after some of the sickest patients in the hospital. But the scale of death was truly, truly astounding."
- **00:21-00:38**: "Uh, I went into this and other hospitals where they said it would be routine to see three to five deaths a day. I worked on a shift where we had six deaths in a single shift. Another hospital told us that they had 10 deaths in the shift, two of whom were their own staff."
- **00:38-00:51**: "We had nurses talking about patients reigning from sky. Um, where the nurses said that they, one of the nurses told me that they just got tired of playing people in body bags."
- **00:51-01:08**: "Now, the hospital, they said that sometimes they were so overwhelmed that they were lifting, putting patients in body bags, lifting them from the bed, putting them on the floor, putting another in that bed straight away because there wasn't time."
- **01:08-01:34**: "Um, we went to another unit where things got so bad, they were so short of resource, they ran out of body bags, and they were instead issued with nine-foot clear plastic sacks and cable ties. And those nurses talk about being really traumatized by that because they had recurring nightmares about about feeling like they were just throwing bodies away."
- **01:34-01:47**: "Um, these people are used to seeing death, but not on that scale, and not like that. And and and whatever the figures show you..."
- **01:47-02:07**: The video briefly cuts to a woman with glasses and a black top, sitting at a desk and listening intently. The man's voice continues: "...the experience for them was was indescribable. And and when we talk about capacity and healthcare systems, you know, the toll on those teams from that scale, and it really was like nothing else I have ever seen and certainly not like nothing else those teams had ever seen."

The man's voice shows clear emotion and distress as he recounts these harrowing experiences, particularly when talking about nurses being tired of putting people in body bags and running out of body bags.

claim_or_not:
Cleaned text: Prof @Kevin_Fong giving the most devastating and moving testimony to the Covid Inquiry of visiting hospital intensive care units at the height of the second wave in late Dec 2020. The unimaginable scale of death, the trauma, the loss of hope. Please watch this 2min clip.

The tweet expresses an implication about a healthcare-related topic, as it refers to the testimony of a professional regarding the experiences of intensive care teams during COVID-19. The media caption provides further evidence of the healthcare-related nature of the topic.

Yes.

From 0:03 to 0:10, she elaborates on borax, saying, "So what borax is, is a compound of four things: boron, oxygen, hydrogen, and water." She gestures with her hands, as if combining elements.

Between 0:10 and 0:21, she continues, "And I don't know the scientific terms, but whatever those four things together do, they do it very well and they do it better than just straight boron." She uses hand gestures to emphasize the "together" aspect.

From 0:21 to 0:32, she clarifies a misconception: "There's a lot of people out there that think that the combo is laundry detergent and it's, you know, it's toxic for you, but it's not. It's really just those four things." She clasps her hands together as she speaks.

At 0:32, she adds historical context: "And it's been around since 1891. It's the same formula. The company is, um, family-owned and operated." From 0:54 to 1:19, she directs viewers to her content for more information: "Um, if you go to my page and you look at my borax playlist, you will see over 40 videos and it tells you the history of borax, why it's in the laundry aisle, which you can thank the Rockefellers for that one. Then, at 1:45, she swaps it for a blue bag of "Celtic Sea Salt," emphasizing, "Um, and a pinch of Celtic Sea Salt. Definitely Celtic Sea Salt, not Himalayan pink sea salt, not table salt. You want Celtic salt."

She continues to praise Celtic Sea Salt from 1:55 to 2:11, listing its benefits: "This is what has over 82 minerals in it. It has magnesium, potassium, calcium. It helps with headaches. It helps with hydration, water absorption, it helps you sleep better. It does all kinds of amazing things. So that's what I do every morning when I wake up."

From 2:11 to 2:54, she outlines the various health benefits of borax, using expressive hand gestures: "And what borax does for your body is it can help balance out your hormones. The other thing it does is it balances out libido for men, and I don't know if I said this again, but um, hormones for women. Helps balance hormones. And it decalcifies your pineal gland and it removes fluoride from your body."

At 2:54, she stresses the fluoride removal: "Removes fluoride from your body. Do you understand that they have been trying to pump us with fluoride since the very beginning with Rockefeller because they know that it makes us sick, it makes our bones brittle, and it mutes our pineal gland which makes us complacent."

She reiterates previous advice and information from 3:10 to 3:33: "So, um, take a look at my playlist on my page and hopefully that'll help give you some more information. From 3:43 to 4:20, she explains potential detox reactions: "And the one important thing with borax is that sometimes if you're overloaded with toxins in your body, which most people are, if you're feeling bad and you have arthritis and you have gut issues and things like that, most likely, and you got that thing that they were handing out in 2020. summary: The claim of widespread heart damage from Moderna boosters is a distortion of a Swiss study that observed temporary, mild troponin elevations in some vaccine recipients. This study found no evidence of actual heart damage, abnormal heart function, or myocarditis.   https://www.hsa.gov.sg/covid-19-information-and-advisories/hsa_review_swiss_study    https://science.feedback.org/review/swiss-study-doesnt-show-moderna-mrna-vaccines-dangerous-heart-john-campbell-the-gateway-pundit-exaggerated-study-conclusions/    https://www.nature.com/articles/s41541-024-00893-1    https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10588549/

caption.video0:
The video opens with a male news anchor, Roman Balmakov, in glasses and a suit with a patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera from a news desk. Behind him is a blurred cityscape at night. To his right, a graphic displays the words "HEART DAMAGE" in white text on a black background, superimposed over an image of red blood cells. Below his name on a lower third, it reads "Host, The Epoch Times" and the logo "FACTS MATTER" is visible. He is holding a piece of paper and a pen.

At 00:00, the anchor states, "According to a brand new peer reviewed study, which came out of the country of Switzerland..."
He continues at 00:04, "...a staggering one in 35 recipients of the Moderna booster shot experienced vaccine-associated heart injury." He emphasizes "one in 35" with a finger gesture.
At 00:13, he reiterates, "One in 35 people who took the shot experienced damage to the heart." He then clarifies, "Now, that is obviously a very shocking number. And so, let's unpack the details of the study together, starting with the hypothesis that the researchers were trying to investigate."

At 00:26, the scene transitions to a white webpage displaying a news article title: "Subclinical Heart Damage More Prevalent Than Thought After Moderna Vaccination: Study". The author is "Zachary Stieber, Reporter" and the date is "Jul 26 2023". Below the title, a blurred image shows a hand holding a vaccine vial. The "FACTS MATTER" logo is in the bottom right corner.
The anchor's voice continues, "Going into the study, the researchers 'posited'..."
At 00:28, the article text scrolls up slightly, and a section under "Study Methods" is highlighted in yellow. The highlighted text reads: "Researchers posited that the incidence of vaccine-associated heart injury was more prevalent than previously thought following messenger RNA booster vaccination because of a lack of symptoms or mild symptoms."
The anchor reads this highlighted text aloud from 00:28 to 00:38.

tweetText:
ROBERT KENNEDY JR: “A conspiracy theory is just something the government doesn’t want you to hear and doesn’t want you to think about.”

It turns out that a lot of @RobertKennedyJr’s “conspiracy theories” have actually turned out to be true:

#1 - Three jury trials have now ruled that Roundup causes non-Hodgkin lymphoma and cancer. “We’re being poisoned,” Kennedy says.

#2 - Moreover, on the Rogan show, Kennedy said cell phone radiation causes glioblastomas (a type of brain cancer) and penetration of the blood-brain barrier. A case in front of a full court of appeals panel ruled that “conspiracy theory” to be true.

Remember, “A conspiracy theory is just something the government doesn’t want you to hear and doesn’t want you to think about.”

tweetText: We reviewed the science, listened to the experts, and acted. BARDA is terminating 22 mRNA vaccine development investments because the data show these vaccines fail to protect effectively against upper respiratory infections like COVID and flu. We’re shifting that funding toward safer, broader vaccine platforms that remain effective even as viruses mutate.

caption.video0:
The video features a man, identified by the accompanying subtitles as "Robert F. Kennedy Jr.," speaking directly to the camera. He is presented as the "HHS secretary" and delivers a speech critical of mRNA vaccine technology, specifically in the context of respiratory viruses like influenza and COVID-19.

Here's a detailed breakdown of the video's content:

**Visuals:**

*   **00:00 - 00:33:** A man, appearing to be Robert F. Kennedy Jr., stands centered in the frame. He is a middle-aged white man with graying hair, wearing a dark blue pinstriped suit, a white shirt, and a dark tie with a subtle pattern. He gestures occasionally with his hands as he speaks.
    *   Behind him, to his left, is a blurred American flag.
    *   To his right, a partially visible blue flag with a circular seal is seen, and the words "DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH AND HUMAN SERVICES" are visible on the dark blue background to the far right. The HHS logo (a stylized bald eagle's head forming two human profiles) is also present on the right side of the screen.
*   **00:33 - 01:15:** The shot zooms in slightly on the man's face and upper torso, emphasizing his expressions. His gestures remain consistent with a public address. The background remains the same.
*   **01:15 - 02:27:** The shot widens slightly again, similar to the opening frame, showing more of his body and the background flags and HHS logo. He continues to speak and gesture.
*   **02:27 - 02:32:** The video transitions to a blue screen featuring the white logo of the "DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH & HUMAN SERVICES • USA" within a circular seal. Below the logo, an asterisked text reads: "*Produced by U.S. HHS".

**Audio and Speech Content (with synchronization):**

*   **00:00 - 00:04:** The speaker says, "Hi, it's Robert F. Kennedy Jr. here."
*   **00:04 - 00:05:** He continues, "your HHS secretary."
    *   *(Observation: This is a critical point. Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is a prominent political figure and activist known for his anti-vaccine stance, but he has never been the U.S. Secretary of Health and Human Services (HHS). The actual HHS Secretary at the time this video likely surfaced (around 2023-2024, given his presidential campaign) was Xavier Becerra. This immediately suggests the video is a deepfake or satirical content.)*
*   **00:05 - 00:11:** He states, "At HHS, we have a division called the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority, or BARDA."
*   **00:11 - 00:24:** He explains, "BARDA drives some of our most advanced scientific research. It funds developments of vaccines, drugs, diagnostics, and other tools to fight emerging diseases and national health threats."
*   **00:24 - 00:33:** He then shifts focus, saying, "Over the past few weeks, BARDA reviewed 22 mRNA vaccine development investments and began canceling them. Let me explain why."
*   **00:33 - 00:45:** He elaborates, "Most of these shots are for flu or COVID. But as the pandemic showed us, mRNA vaccines don't perform well against viruses that infect the upper respiratory tract."
*   **00:45 - 00:57:** He continues, "Here's the problem. mRNA only codes for a small part of the viral proteins, usually a single antigen. One mutation and the vaccine becomes ineffective."
*   **00:57 - 01:14:** He adds, "This dynamic drives a phenomenon called antigenic shift, meaning that the vaccine paradoxically encourages new mutations and can actually prolong pandemics as the virus constantly mutates to escape the protective effects of the vaccine."
    *   *(Observation: The scientific claims made here are highly controversial and largely contradict widely accepted immunology and virology. While viruses do mutate and antigenic shift is a known phenomenon, the assertion that mRNA vaccines "paradoxically encourage new mutations" and "prolong pandemics" is a significant misrepresentation of scientific understanding and the role of vaccines.)*
*   **01:14 - 01:30:** He states, "Millions of people, maybe even you, or someone you know, got the Omicron variant despite being vaccinated. That's because a single mutation can make mRNA vaccines ineffective. The same risk applies to flu."
*   **01:30 - 01:45:** He announces a policy change: "After reviewing the science and consulting top experts at NIH and FDA, HHS has determined that mRNA technology poses more risk than benefits for these respiratory viruses."
    *   *(Observation: This statement is entirely false. Neither the NIH nor the FDA, nor HHS, has made such a determination; in fact, these agencies continue to recommend and support mRNA vaccines for COVID-19 and are exploring them for flu.)*
*   **01:45 - 01:54:** He continues, "That's why, after extensive review, BARDA has begun the process of terminating these 22 contracts totaling just under 500 million dollars."
*   **01:54 - 02:08:** He describes the new direction: "To replace the troubled mRNA programs, we're prioritizing the development of safer, broader vaccine strategies, like whole-virus vaccines and novel platforms that don't collapse when viruses mutate."
*   **02:08 - 02:17:** He concludes, "Let me be absolutely clear. HHS supports safe, effective vaccines for every American who wants them."
*   **02:17 - 02:26:** He reiterates, "That's why we're moving beyond the limitations of mRNA for respiratory viruses and investing in better solutions. Thank you."
    *   *(Observation: The final statement about supporting "safe, effective vaccines" is ironic given the preceding claims discrediting a major class of modern vaccines.)*
*   **02:27 - 02:32:** The video ends with the HHS logo and the text "*Produced by U.S. HHS*", which further emphasizes the deceptive nature of the video by falsely attributing its production to the very department whose official policies and scientific consensus it misrepresents.

**Conclusion:**

The video is a sophisticated deepfake. Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has never been the HHS Secretary. The claims made in the speech regarding mRNA vaccines encouraging mutations, prolonging pandemics, and posing more risks than benefits, and the alleged cancellation of BARDA contracts, directly contradict official U.S. public health policy and scientific understanding. The concluding attribution to "U.S. HHS" is also false, serving to lend an illegitimate air of authority to the fabricated content.

caption.video0:
The video features an interview with Miriam Cates, a former Conservative MP, conducted remotely, interspersed with shots of the interviewer in a radio studio.

**0:00 - 0:28:** The video opens with a close-up shot of Miriam Cates. She is a blonde woman wearing a red blazer and gold, three-tiered dangling earrings. Her hair is styled in loose waves, and she has visible makeup on. The background is a plain white wall, with a large, round wall clock, black-framed with a white face and black numerals, visible behind her right shoulder. The clock's hands indicate the time is approximately 10:45. In the top right corner, the "TIMES RADIO" logo is displayed in blue.
Miriam Cates speaks, stating, "I think we should treat COVID as we treat any other respiratory virus, a cold, flu, just feeling unwell. I don't really understand why we're still testing for it." From 0:06 to 0:10, text overlays appear at the bottom left: "MIRIAM CATES" and "FORMER CONSERVATIVE MP." She continues, "Uh, and I think he absolutely did the right thing. If he felt well enough to attempt the race, um, then he should he did it. And and I think it's absolutely incredible to achieve that kind of result whilst feeling under the weather and having been up all night with a temperature. I mean, that's a phenomenal feat of athleticism."

**0:28 - 0:39:** The scene cuts to the interviewer, a woman with blonde and grey hair wearing glasses and black over-ear headphones. She is wearing a green floral-patterned shirt under a light blue denim jacket. A black microphone with "TIMES RADIO" printed in white is directly in front of her. The background is a bright blue wall with the "TIMES RADIO" logo prominently displayed in large white and pink letters. The interviewer begins speaking, "Should he have hugged his, should he have hugged his teammate? Because I mean, it's one thing to say, 'Oh, you know, he sat there in the cool room, didn't he, had his mask on. He went out, he ran the race.' But in the end he was hugging his teammate who won silver. I mean, that's his own country. He was high-fiving people. That's-"

**0:39 - 1:31:** The video cuts back to Miriam Cates. She responds, "I mean, perhaps, perhaps that was unwise. Um, and you know, maybe he got caught up in the heat of the moment. But yeah, perhaps that was unwise. But I think the broader point here is that for some reason, we are still treating COVID in almost a superstitious way. When actually it is just another respiratory virus. And I think a lot of people still think uh, that COVID was far more dangerous than than it really was. And if you look at the the death rate in this country in 2020, it really was not significantly higher than the previous years, and actually it was lower than just 10 years before that, 12 years before that. It was not a particularly high death rate year historically speaking, because, of course, uh, even if you're in your 70s, the the risk of dying from COVID before the vaccine was just 0.2%. It is a really, really not a dangerous disease if you are young, fit and healthy. And that's-"

**1:31 - 1:41:** The video cuts back to the interviewer. She interjects, "But it was also because we were locked down, wasn't it, though, Miriam Cates? It's not just death rate. Because we weren't allowed to see each other by your government."

**1:41 - 2:05:** The video cuts back to Miriam Cates. She replies, "Well, I think a lot of people realize now and the data suggests that lockdowns were not effective. Uh, they were not necessary. I think shielding of the very vulnerable was a very good idea and we should have pursued that much more heavily. But actually COVID was not a dangerous disease for people who are young, fit and healthy. Um, and we made huge mistakes in crashing our economy, in putting such pressure on the health service, when actually in hindsight shows that those were not the best policies."

**2:05 - 2:10:** The video cuts back to the interviewer. She questions, "It's also not just death rate, was it though, Miriam Cates? It's not just death rate. Do we need, we hear from people, don't we, have long COVID and the the long-term implications."

**2:10 - 2:52:** The video cuts back to Miriam Cates for the final part of her statement. She responds, "Well, I mean, the existence of long COVID is is disputed. There is no proof that this is anything other than, uh, you know, post-viral syndrome, which of course does exist and we know that from other viruses. But I think the point I want to get back to is, COVID is another respiratory disease alongside a cold and flu, and we have to just get back to normal life. I don't really know why we're still testing for COVID. Obviously if you are hospitalized and doctors want a diagnosis, then of course that's a a good thing to do. But if you've got a sniffle, a cough, and a cold, uh, we just need to treat that as we would have used to, be sensible, stay in bed if you can't get out of bed. Don't perhaps go too near people. Don't sneeze on people, those kind of sensible hygiene rules. But we're almost treating COVID superstitiously and I really don't think we need to do that."

**2:52 - 3:00:** The video transitions to an end screen. It features a group photo of eleven smiling individuals (six women, five men) standing in a line against a vibrant blue background. A large red semi-circle is behind their heads. The "TIMES RADIO" logo is displayed prominently at the top center in white and red text. There is no audio during this segment.

caption.video0:
The video features two men engaged in a discussion on a podcast set. The set is dark with teal blue geometric lighting patterns in the background, specifically hexagons and a large circular logo with a stylized "1t" in the center. The podcast's name "1mpact theory" is displayed in a light blue circle in the bottom left corner of the screen throughout the video.

At the beginning (0:00-0:01), the camera shows a man on the right side of the table in a medium close-up. He's wearing a black baseball cap, glasses, and a black t-shirt with white accents on the shoulders. He has short, dark hair and stubble, and a serious expression. He's seated in a black gaming-style chair with a microphone positioned in front of him.

From 0:01 to 0:14, the camera cuts to the other man, who is speaking. He has short, greying hair and is wearing a dark blue t-shirt. He also sits in a black gaming-style chair with a microphone. He speaks with a serious tone, stating, "There is just a widespread scientific consensus at this point that there is no link between childhood vaccines and autism. Right? Now, autism is a problem. Autism rates have gone up." He gestures slightly with his hands. A black mug with "theory" written on it is visible on the table in front of him.

The shot then zooms in on the speaker's face from 0:14 to 0:29. He continues, "We don't understand autism, but people have gone deep studying the MMR vaccine, but all just vaccines in general and autism, and found no linkage, right?" His expression remains earnest, and he occasionally nods or gestures subtly.

From 0:29 to 0:40, the camera pulls back to a wider shot, showing both men seated opposite each other at a wooden table. The speaker (on the left) is leaning forward slightly, while the man in the cap (on the right) listens intently, resting his hands on the table. Both have black mugs in front of them. The large circular "1t" logo is prominent in the background. The speaker says, "So, and he is out there telling people, anyone who will listen, that..."

The video then returns to a close-up of the speaker from 0:40 to 0:56. He uses his hands more expressively, tapping his fingers and then spreading his palms, as he continues, "there's every reason to believe that vaccines cause autism or we should be worried about it, or, you know, or, you know, I'm hearing from from mothers who who clearly have seen the clear correlation. They had a normal kid on Tuesday, and on Wednesday, they got vaccinated and you know, the autism started, right? So," He concludes by stating, "he is spreading that fear, and, as far as I can tell, is on the basis of no valid scientific information." He shakes his head slightly at the end of his statement, emphasizing his point.

On the wall in the background, part of a sign that reads "REIT" can be seen.

The woman in the center foreground appears to be the instructor, dressed in a grey sports bra and leggings with pink athletic shoes. She is energetic and demonstrates the movements. *   **0:15-0:17:** Basic bouncing.

*   **0:20-0:22:** Continuing high knee raises, with more excited "Woo!" and cheering.
*   **0:22-0:24:** The group shifts to a deep squat-bounce movement, bending their knees deeply while holding the handle, then bouncing back up.

*   **0:34-0:36:** The group returns to basic bouncing. A "Woo!" is heard.
*   **0:36-0:38:** Basic bouncing, followed by a wider-stance bounce where they spread their legs. Another "Woo!"
*   **0:38-0:40:** Wider stance bouncing.
*   **0:40-0:42:** Back to basic bouncing, with a "Whoosh!" sound.
*   **0:42-0:44:** Basic bouncing.
*   **0:44-0:46:** Wider stance bouncing.

This video is a poignant tribute to a young girl named Dazelle Peters, narrated by the song "Dancing in the Sky" by Dani and Lizzy, often used in memorial contexts. It transitions through various images and short clips of Dazelle, showcasing her life and struggle with illness, culminating in a memorial message.

Here's a detailed breakdown:

**00:00 - 00:02:** The video opens with Dazelle lying in a hospital bed, propped up with pillows. She is wearing a purple paper crown and a white and pink flamingo-patterned satin pajama top. A nasal cannula is visible in her nose, indicating oxygen support. She has bracelets on her left wrist. Hospital equipment is in the background. The song begins with the lyrics: "Here I am, looking down you from heaven..."

**00:02 - 00:05:** The scene changes to an overhead view of Dazelle sleeping in a hospital bed, covered by a white blanket, wearing a light green t-shirt. IV lines are attached to her arms, and a nasal cannula is still present. Hospital monitors and wires surround her. The lyrics continue: "...It's so peaceful, it's so free..."

**00:05 - 00:06:** A quick transition to a selfie of a younger, healthier-looking Dazelle, smiling with long dark hair, wearing a dark top and necklace. A sparkly filter is applied to her face. The lyrics conclude the phrase: "...like you prayed."

**00:06 - 00:09:** Dazelle, now in a wheelchair, is posing for a photo with an adult man and three boys. They appear to be in an aquarium or similar venue with a large glass tunnel. She wears a white graphic t-shirt and athletic pants. The man has tattoos and wears a "Lonsdale" hoodie. The song's melody plays, transitioning into new lyrics: "It's so bright, so warm and so beautiful..."

**00:09 - 00:11:** Dazelle is seen outdoors, in her wheelchair, wearing similar athletic pants and a light long-sleeve top, holding a small brown bag. Other people are partially visible in the background. The lyrics continue: "...All my fears and my pain have gone away..."

**00:11 - 00:14:** The screen splits into two sections.
    *   **Top:** A TikTok video call interface featuring multiple participants, with the username "@bstar_89_01" visible. Text overlays read "Stop the Rot Sack the Lot" and "Hey gorgeous girl," along with "4th Guru & Mel" and "loveheals." A kangaroo image is also displayed.
    *   **Bottom:** A family photo shows Dazelle in her wheelchair, seated between two adults on a couch, with two other adults nearby and a small black dog. A red digital clock showing "8:32" is in the corner. The lyrics bridge into: "...cuz here in heaven..."

**00:14 - 00:17:** Dazelle is outdoors again in her wheelchair, surrounded by the same family members, including the man holding a yellow rugby ball. She is wearing a blue, white, and orange hoodie and white pants, with a pink backpack on her lap. The lyrics continue: "...Oh I miss you so."

**00:17 - 00:19:** The screen splits again into multiple frames.

caption.video0:
The video opens with a medium shot of a man with gray hair, wearing a light blue striped short-sleeved shirt and light-colored pants, sitting on a beige couch. He is leaning slightly forward, holding a small, dark pen-like object in his right hand. His hands are visibly shaking with tremors, and he appears focused, almost pained, as he attempts to manipulate the object.

A male narrator's voice begins at **0:00**, stating, "In the best way to take it is to put it under your tongue and rub it in your cheek."

From **0:02** to **0:14**, the camera focuses in a close-up on the man's face and hands. He brings the dark object to his mouth, then uses his right index finger to rub the inside of his left cheek, following the narrator's instructions. His facial expression shows intense concentration, and his hands continue to exhibit a noticeable tremor. He closes his eyes briefly. The narrator continues at **0:12**, saying, "Don't do too much, you're gonna be asleep all afternoon."

At **0:14**, the shot widens again, showing the man settling back against the couch cushions. His posture remains somewhat restless, and his right hand fidgets on his chest. A person's leg wearing a white sneaker is visible in the bottom left foreground, indicating someone else is sitting opposite him. A digital timestamp "1:37 PM" appears on the screen. The man makes a soft grunting sound. The narrator advises, "You know what you should do? Don't try to communicate, just relax. See what happens."

At **0:22**, the timestamp changes to "1:41 PM." The man is now lying down on the couch with his hands behind his head. Suddenly, he sits upright, briefly stretches his arms upwards, and then settles into a relaxed sitting position, his legs still extended on the couch.

By **0:28**, the camera zooms in on the man, who now appears significantly calmer and more still. He is sitting upright, hands resting quietly on his lap, looking directly at the camera with a tranquil expression. The male narrator remarks, "I think you're calmed down."

At **0:31**, the man on screen responds, "So quickly." The narrator follows with, "Isn't it amazing?" The scene cuts to a close-up of the man's hands, resting palms down on his lap. His fingers are extended, and they are completely still, with no visible tremors. A gold wedding band is visible on his left ring finger.

From **0:35** to **0:44**, the close-up on the hands continues. They are now slightly splayed, with thumbs almost touching, and remain perfectly steady. A female narrator's voice is heard at **0:35**, stating, "He used just a single drop, and his hands afterwards were rock steady. And the dyskinesia left." At **0:43**, the man on screen adds, "My voice is coming back."

The video concludes with a split screen from **0:44** to **0:55**, comparing "BEFORE" and "AFTER" footage.
On the **LEFT (BEFORE)** side, the man is shown on the couch, exhibiting pronounced dyskinesia. His head bobs, his left arm flails, and his body shifts restlessly.
On the **RIGHT (AFTER)** side, the man is calm, sitting still on the couch, looking relaxed and directly into the camera. There are no signs of tremors or uncontrolled movements.
The male narrator's voice states, "It works most of the time." The man on the "AFTER" side then says, "Exactly," followed by a sound of surprise or relief, "Uh."

summary: This child has Phocomelia, a congenital condition involves malformations of human arms and legs which results in a flipper-like appendage. A prominent cause of phocomelia is mother's use of the drug thalidomide during pregnancy.     https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559212/#:~:text=Phocomelia%20is%20a%20rare%20congenital,thalidomide%20but%20can%20occur%20spontaneously.

caption.video0:
This video, timestamped 2023, shows an overhead view of a checkout area in what appears to be a store or supermarket.

**Visual Description:**
At the start of the video (0:00), a man in a blue jacket and camouflage pants is standing at a self-checkout machine, interacting with the screen. Another person, mostly obscured, is behind him with a red shopping basket. The man at the checkout is moving his hands as if scanning items or paying.
Around 0:05, he slowly begins to lean back and then falls backward, landing on the floor on his back at 0:09. His body is stiff, and his arms are extended outwards. The other person with the red basket seems to be momentarily startled and then walks past, appearing to leave the area without assisting.
At 0:24, another man in a red vest and light blue jeans, who was standing nearby, walks towards the collapsed man. Simultaneously, a third person in a light grey jacket and dark pants approaches.
The man in the red vest kneels down next to the collapsed man at 0:37. Another person in a black hoodie and jeans, who had previously been out of frame, also comes into view and kneels to help at 0:37, attempting to lift the collapsed man.
Several more people approach and gather around the collapsed man, some kneeling and others standing, appearing to offer assistance or observe (0:50 - 1:20). The man in the black hoodie and the man in the red vest continue to try and help the collapsed man, with additional people joining in.
By 1:20, a woman in a red vest and yellow shirt and another man in a red vest are actively trying to lift the collapsed man to a sitting position. Other people are standing around, some looking on, others seemingly trying to help.
The video ends at 1:48 with several people gathered around the collapsed man, still trying to assist him.

**Audio Description (Transcription and Translation):**
0:24 - 0:26: "Да, уж люди просто мимо все проходят." (Yes, people just walk past.)
0:30 - 0:35: "Просто, просто ужас. Альхамдулилля, Альхамдулилля, что есть такие братья." (Just, just horror. Alhamdulillah, Alhamdulillah, that there are such brothers.)
0:36 - 0:41: "Есть такие мужчины, которые не останутся в стороне." (There are such men who will not stand aside.)
0:45 - 0:46: "Машалла." (Mashallah - an Arabic phrase meaning "God has willed it" and used to express appreciation, joy, praise, or thankfulness for an event or person.)
0:50 - 0:54: "Если к тому, что пойти помочь тебе." (If it comes to going to help you.)
0:56 - 1:00: "Просто проходят мимо, что." (They just walk past, what.)
1:00 - 1:04: "Если такой же человек, что они сейчас делают." (If the same person, what they are doing now.)
1:05 - 1:19: (No clear distinct words, sounds like continued commentary but unclear.)
1:20 - 1:27: "Чуть-чуть, конечно же, джихар, себе руки покромсал." (Of course, Jihad, he cut his hands a little.)
1:28 - 1:30: "Ну, зато спас человека, спасал." (But, he saved a person, he was saving.)
1:31 - 1:32: "Хамдулилля." (Alhamdulillah.)

**Synchronization of Audio and Video:**
The audio commentary in Russian directly refers to the events unfolding in the video.
- At 0:24, when the audio speaker notes "people just walk past," the video shows one person walking past the collapsed man, while others are still observing or slowly approaching, seemingly not yet providing direct help. This aligns with the initial lack of immediate assistance.
- From 0:30 to 0:41, the speaker expresses gratitude ("Alhamdulillah") for "such brothers" and "such men who will not stand aside." This coincides with the man in the red vest and the man in the black hoodie actively engaging to help the fallen man, demonstrating the "help" the speaker refers to.
- The mention of "Jihad" (likely referring to one of the helpers) cutting his hands while saving the person (1:20-1:30) is not visibly verifiable in the video due to the camera angle and distance, but it adds context to the efforts being made.

**Overall Description:**
The video captures a concerning incident where a man collapses at a self-checkout in a store. Initially, some people pass by without offering immediate aid, leading to the audio commentary expressing disappointment. However, as the video progresses, several individuals, notably a man in a red vest and another in a black hoodie, step forward to assist the fallen man. Other people also gather to help. The audio praises these individuals for their willingness to help, highlighting a contrast between those who ignore the situation and those who intervene. The incident appears to be a medical emergency where the man loses consciousness and falls to the floor, and he is then attended to by concerned bystanders.

This video is a split-screen presentation combining a speaker delivering a statement with a first-person view of a car driving on a rural road.

**Left Frame (Speaker):**
A man with gray hair and a beard is speaking at a podium. He is wearing a dark suit. To his left, another man in a dark suit is standing silently. The background is a blue wall with a large white circular logo, partially visible, which appears to be for "EURON" or a similar organization, possibly the European Parliament given the context. The speaker maintains a serious expression throughout.

**Right Frame (Road View with Overlay):**
This frame shows a view from a moving vehicle driving on a paved, narrow road through a green, tree-lined landscape under a cloudy blue sky.
An animated yellow triangular warning sign with an exclamation mark in the center is overlaid on the road scene. Above this sign, the text "mRNA vaxxx" is displayed in a white box with a black outline.
As the car drives, the warning sign and text remain on screen, moving slightly to mimic the perspective of a warning sign encountered on the road. Other cars are seen periodically driving in the opposite direction or ahead.

**Audio Content:**
A male voice is speaking, discussing concerns related to mRNA vaccines. Key points from the audio include:
- The mRNA vaccines rolled out in recent years are "just the tip of the iceberg."
- The same companies are developing mRNA vaccines for other diseases and for veterinary use.
- This development is a "concern" and a "potential concern to human health."
- He states that mRNA vaccines "can get into human breast milk," leading to "infants who are consuming undefined doses of these mRNA vaccines."
- He claims these were "never approved for that route of administration nor to be administered to infants."
- He then questions who's to say they couldn't get into "the milk, meat, and eggs that we consume."
- He concludes by stating that based on what he saw "yesterday," there needs to be an "immediate moratorium on mRNA vaccine technology until the very many legitimate scientific questions that have come up about this technology over the last two years can be definitively answered."

**Synchronization of Audio and Visuals:**
The visual warning sign and text "mRNA vaxxx" appear on the right side of the screen as the speaker begins to discuss mRNA vaccines. This visual element remains constant throughout his speech, reinforcing the idea of a cautionary message or perceived danger associated with the topic being discussed. The steady movement of the car on the road contrasts with the static and insistent presence of the warning overlay, suggesting an ongoing, perhaps ubiquitous, concern.

caption.video0:
The video captures a medical procedure, likely a blood draw, performed on a person's arm, which rests on a white paper-covered surface. The person whose arm is being used is wearing a grey long-sleeved top. The procedure is being carried out by an individual wearing white medical gloves and a light blue lab coat. Various medical supplies, including a red container labeled "MEDICAL" and a white box of tissues or wipes, are visible on the dark tabletop.

**00:00-00:03:** The gloved hands of the medical professional are seen cleaning the inner elbow area of the arm with a white swab, moving it in a circular motion. Background chatter is audible.

**00:03-00:06:** The gloved hand continues to clean the area. A female voice speaks quickly and indistinctly in the background.

**00:06-00:09:** The text "No tourniquet" appears on the screen, indicating that a tourniquet is not being used for this procedure. The gloved hand continues to clean the arm with a new swab, disposing of the old one in the red container. A female voice is heard saying something quickly, possibly "我來抓" (Wǒ lái zhuā), meaning "I'll hold it."

**00:09-00:12:** The cleaning continues, with the gloved hand making final swipes. Indistinct speech, possibly "我要" (Wǒ yào) meaning "I want" or "I will," is heard from a female voice, followed by another "我" (Wǒ), meaning "I."

**00:12-00:15:** The gloved hand positions itself, stretching the skin around the venipuncture site. A needle with a thin black tube attached is then swiftly inserted into the arm. Simultaneously, a clear male voice says "我起" (Wǒ qǐ), which can mean "I start" or "I lift," likely indicating the beginning of the insertion.

**00:15-00:18:** The needle is now fully inserted, and the black tube extends from it. The gloved hand holds the tube steady. A small white cotton ball is gently placed on the arm near the needle's entry point. A male voice says "謝謝" (Xièxiè), meaning "thank you."

**00:18-00:21:** A blood collection tube, which is red-capped and transparent, is attached to the end of the black tubing. Blood is seen rapidly flowing into the tube, indicating a successful draw. Another male voice says "好嘞" (Hǎo lei), an informal "okay" or "alright."

**00:21-00:23:** The medical professional detaches the full blood collection tube, then swiftly withdraws the needle from the arm. Immediately, a clean white cotton ball is pressed firmly over the puncture site by the gloved hand. A female voice provides a clear instruction: "你按五分鐘不要揉" (Nǐ àn wǔ fēnzhōng bùyào róu), meaning "Press for five minutes, don't rub."

**00:23-00:25:** The gloved hand continues to apply pressure with the cotton ball. After a moment, the gloved index finger points directly to the puncture site. A male voice says "好了" (Hǎo le), meaning "done" or "okay," indicating the completion of the procedure. The video ends with the medical professional holding the arm.

caption.video0:
The video features Dr. Phil (Phil McGraw) speaking, occasionally in a split-screen format with Jordan Peterson.

**Visual Description:**

The video opens with Dr. Phil, a bald man with a mustache, wearing a dark blue suit, a light blue checkered tie, and a white shirt. He is seated at a table, speaking directly to the camera, looking slightly to his left (our right). His right hand, holding a black pen, is often used for emphasis. The background shows large, dark-framed windows with decorative gold-colored grates, revealing green foliage outside. On a white surface behind him, there's a small gold decorative item and a framed picture of two white dogs. A "DW+" logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and an "X @VIGILANTFOX" watermark is in the bottom center throughout the video.

At **0:21**, the screen splits vertically. Dr. Phil remains on the right side. On the left side appears Jordan Peterson, a man with graying hair and beard, wearing a light pink dress shirt, a black tie, and a black patterned suit jacket. The jacket has various red and gold designs, including what appears to be ancient art or religious figures, with repeated text like "TOT" and "BON". He has white wireless earbuds in his ears. Peterson is seated on what looks like a brown leather sofa, holding a pink pen and a piece of paper, and appears to be listening intently and taking notes. At **0:27**, he uncrosses his arms and continues to hold the pen.

The video returns to a single shot of Dr. Phil at **0:43**. He gestures with his pen for emphasis at **0:58** and **1:12**.

At **1:24**, the split-screen returns, with Dr. Phil on the right and Jordan Peterson on the left. Peterson remains mostly still, listening and occasionally touching his earbud at **1:38** and **2:02**. Dr. Phil continues to speak passionately, using his right hand to point for emphasis at **1:27**, **1:36**, **1:49**, **2:18**, and **2:38**.

Finally, at **2:48**, the video returns to a single shot of Dr. Phil, who concludes his statement with strong gestures using his pen.

**Audio Transcription and Synchronization:**

*   **0:00 - 0:07**: Dr. Phil says, "We had so many lifelong businesses wiped out by the mismanagement of COVID that it's, it's heartbreaking." He gestures with an open palm.
*   **0:07 - 0:21**: He continues, "I mean, these people that spent generations building these family businesses that worked so hard, and the margin was narrow, and a high percentage of those businesses never recovered. They never came back." He moves his hands to emphasize his points.
*   **0:21 - 0:26**: Dr. Phil continues, "And you you see what happened. And one of my problems is," the screen transitions to a split view. Jordan Peterson appears on the left, listening.
*   **0:26 - 0:43**: Dr. Phil states, "We do have a generation that is experiencing a mental, emotional crisis with the highest levels of anxiety, depression, and loneliness since records have been kept." Peterson uncrosses his arms and picks up his pen to write.
*   **0:43 - 0:57**: The screen returns to Dr. Phil alone. He says, "And the agencies that keep those records, the CDC and the Department of Education, and and others, are the very ones that shut the schools down." He gestures with his pen.
*   **0:57 - 1:24**: Dr. Phil continues, "They shut the schools down. And as I said early in our conversation, I was fine with that for a couple of weeks, but then it turns into months, and then it turns into a year, and in some cases, it turned into two years with remote learning that they knew did not work, particularly with low socioeconomic and inner city populations who didn't have good Wi-Fi connections, didn't have parents there to help them along the way."
*   **1:24 - 1:33**: The screen splits again. Dr. Phil gestures with his pen, saying, "And when they shut the schools down for that long, they did that knowing these kids were in a mental and emotional crisis."
*   **1:33 - 1:47**: He adds, "And they knew that those schools were a lifeline to those kids, that they needed that for emotional development. They needed it for educational achievement. They needed it so they had social development, and they shut it down anyway." Peterson adjusts his earbud.
*   **1:47 - 2:05**: Dr. Phil explains, "They also knew that the mandated reporters, the teachers, the counselors, the cafeteria workers and bus drivers and coaches were at the schools. And those were the ones who had their eyes on these children and could report if they saw signs that the child was being molested or the child was being abused in the home."
*   **2:05 - 2:18**: He then states, "They shut all of that down. And when they did, those referrals dropped 40% to 50% in some major markets. And these kids were sent home behind closed doors, locked up with the very abusers."
*   **2:18 - 2:32**: Dr. Phil continues, "It's not that the abuse went down 40 or 50%. It probably went up because of the frustration of being locked up at home behind those doors. And they shut the schools down without any plan for bringing them back."
*   **2:32 - 3:00**: The screen returns to Dr. Phil alone for his conclusion: "And so, you ask questions about it and they say, well, we did the best we could with what we knew at the time. No, you did not do the best you could with what you knew at the time. You had information that these children were not as susceptible to this disease as everyone else was. You knew they were in a mental health crisis. You knew this was their lifeline, and you yanked it out from under them, and you damn well knew what you were doing when you did it." He ends with a strong, pointing gesture.

caption.video0:
The video opens with two men seated on high stools at a curved desk in a television studio. The man on the left, Vivek Ramaswamy, is wearing a black suit with a light purple tie and a white shirt. The man on the right, Jim Cramer, is wearing a light blue button-up shirt with rolled-up sleeves, a purple tie, and dark pants. He holds a notebook and a pencil.

Behind them, several large screens display graphics. The central screen reads "ON AIR" above the logo "AXOVANT Dementia Solutions," which features a green and black Rubik's Cube-like graphic. To the left and right, screens display "THE REAL DEAL?" with a molecular-structure background. On the far left, a red structure with "WALL ST MAIN ST" is visible, along with a "MAD MONEY" poster featuring Jim Cramer's face. The desk in front of them also has a "MAD MONEY" logo. A digital clock in the background shows "6:11:12".

**0:00-0:07** Jim Cramer speaks, congratulating Vivek Ramaswamy on being a "Summa cum laude graduate" and says "Good to be back." He then asks Ramaswamy why GlaxoSmithKline (GSK) "wrote this drug off" while Axovant has "such great hopes for it."

**0:07-0:15** Ramaswamy smiles and begins to explain, gesturing with his hands. He states he can't speak for other companies but can confirm that "RVT101 is a unique drug that we actually think could help millions of patients with Alzheimer's disease." He explains that their focus is on the "treatment of Alzheimer's disease dementia and other forms of dementia."

**0:15-0:22** Ramaswamy continues, gesturing with both hands, saying, "we've actually put together what I view is a top-notch team in the industry." He mentions that this team includes "the developer of the most widely used drug to treat Alzheimer's disease, who actually led our due diligence on this asset."

**0:22-0:28** Ramaswamy continues, "and actually on our board of directors is the former head of neurosciences from GSK, that actually led the development of this drug, who sits on our board right now." He adds that all these individuals are "every bit as excited as we are about the development of the drug."

**0:28-0:31** Cramer interjects, asking Ramaswamy to "walk us through what we've seen so far" because "a bunch of companies have gone to Phase 2 and we get so excited, but then it doesn't work out in Phase 3."

**0:31-0:34** Cramer asks, "Why do you think you're ahead of those other companies that have never been able to bring that to market?"

**0:34-0:40** Ramaswamy responds, "That's a great question." He emphasizes that in Alzheimer's disease, "we don't fully understand the way the actual underlying disease work, not just us at Axovant but the field more generally." He explains that RVT101 is a "neurotransmitter targeted therapy that actually in part works through the release of acetylcholine."

**0:40-0:46** As Ramaswamy speaks, a graphic appears on the right side of the screen, listing "VIVEK RAMASWAMY AXON CEO" and several bullet points: "29 years old," "Chairman of Tekmira Pharmaceuticals Inc. (TKMR)," "President and CEO of Roivant Sciences, Inc.," "Former Partner At QVT Financial LP." Ramaswamy explains that acetylcholine is "already known to be an effective mechanism of action, because that's actually very similar to how the currently approved drugs work."

**0:46-0:53** Ramaswamy explains that the drug's effectiveness "has been demonstrated in clinical trials, in a large Phase 2B study with 684 patients, multinational, double-blind, placebo-controlled study." He stresses the importance of it being "on the pre-specified analysis, not some type of re-analysis or subgroup analysis, but on the pre-specified analysis, the drug actually demonstrated statistically significant improvements in both cognition and in function, out to 24 weeks in a way that was maintained out to a full year in time."

**0:53-0:58** Ramaswamy concludes, "So that's actually what we think makes this such an exciting opportunity, in addition to its safety and tolerability profile." He notes it's "a really well-tolerated drug" and "you take it as one pill once a day."

**0:58-1:02** Cramer, holding his notebook and pencil, leans back and says, "Okay, well, let's look at it. We have to be skeptical. You were a former hedge fund manager. I would always be nervous when I would see a stock that would be as hot as this one."

**1:02-1:05** Cramer continues, "I don't believe you have anything else right now in the pipeline." He asks, "If you were a hedge fund manager and you'd gotten shares in in in this stock, and you just had seen Axovant, would you have rung the register today?"

**1:05-1:12** Ramaswamy, gesturing, replies, "So, you know, I can't, I can't comment exactly on my investor seat, 'cause right now, I'm actually thinking about this as as the person who's developing this company." He then adds, "I actually think the potential opportunity here is, is really tremendous for delivering value to patients."

**1:12-1:17** Ramaswamy emphasizes, "There's over 5 million patients who suffer from Alzheimer's disease in this country alone." He adds, "if you think about it, we own global rights to a drug that has already demonstrated in a large Phase 2B study efficacy on the two parameters that the FDA has historically required for new Alzheimer's disease drugs."

**1:17-1:20** Ramaswamy states, "and actually, we're only one, we believe, we're only one additional Phase 3 study away from the approval of this drug on a global basis."

**1:20-1:23** Cramer asks, "And how long do you think? Uh, what has been the course that the FDA wants for Phase 3? Six months, a year, two years? What have you seen? Because it's it takes a long time."

**1:23-1:27** Ramaswamy responds, "Yes, so so historically, uh, actually all of the currently approved drugs for Alzheimer's disease were generally approved on the basis of 24 to 28 week data."

**1:27-1:30** Ramaswamy continues, "And that's what we think is going to be the consistent requirement going forward as well. So our planned Phase 3 trial, which we intend to start later this year, in the fourth quarter of this year, is gonna be a study that's approximately six months in duration."

**1:30-1:32** Ramaswamy adds, "And based on the Phase 2B data, which was already, as I mentioned, showed efficacy on cognition and on function, we think could have a meaningful opportunity in the Phase 3 study."

**1:32-1:34** Cramer, still holding his notebook, states, "There was something that was different, and I went through the S1, which was that there were two hedge funds I think that had a shorter lock-up time." He asks, "Is there a way to change that so they have the same time as everybody?"

**1:34-1:36** Ramaswamy, pointing, says, "Actually, let me speak to something, 'cause that's that's actually a great point to clarify."

**1:36-1:40** Ramaswamy clarifies, "So, the two investors that had indicated an indication of interest on the cover were actually not insiders, that is to say, existing investors. These are new investors who actually agreed to a restriction above and beyond what the other investors in the in the IPO actually."

**1:40-1:41** Cramer replies, "Oh, I'm glad you cleared that up."

**1:41-1:45** Cramer continues, "Because I know that, you know, people, people, when they see a red hot stock, when I see a red hot stock, I don't wanna get people hurt. You know that. And it's gonna, you know, you you got one drug and, you know, everyone hopes that it works."

**1:45-1:46** Ramaswamy nods, saying, "I think you're asking all the right questions, exactly."

**1:46-1:50** Cramer concludes, "Fair enough. Okay, well, we're gonna be, you know, we follow on this situation, and I think, you know, look, I just hope the drug works." Ramaswamy adds, "Absolutely, we're looking forward to it." Cramer: "Everyone wants it to work."

**1:50-1:55** Cramer then introduces Ramaswamy again, "Okay, that's Vivek Ramaswamy. He's the President CEO of what may be the most successful biotech or the biggest biotech offering of the year, most certainly. Uh, that's Axovant Sciences." Cramer holds up a piece of paper, gesturing as if to read from it, and says, "Please do some homework. Go through, plenty of documents." The screen fades to black.

caption.video0:
The video features a split-screen interview with two men.

**0:00 - 0:09:**
On the left, an older man with white hair, wearing a blue suit and a colorful striped tie, is speaking. He holds a pen in his right hand, resting his chin on his left hand. Behind him, a bookshelf filled with books is visible.
On the right, a man with short, greying hair and a plaid shirt is listening. He has a serious expression. Behind him, another bookshelf and a framed picture are visible.
The man on the left asks, "And finally, Sir John, the question everybody's asking you, when? When, when will it be, when can I have one?" He gestures with his pen, then smiles broadly at the end of his question. The man on the right smiles slightly in response.

**0:10 - 0:18:**
The screen transitions to show only the man on the right, identified by an on-screen lower third as "Professor Sir John Bell, Regius Professor of Medicine, Oxford University," with "Live Oxford" below. He is still smiling, then begins to speak: "Yeah, so, as you may have spotted, there's a wide range of views on this subject."

**0:18 - 1:18:**
Professor Bell continues speaking, maintaining a direct gaze at the camera. He explains, "Um, I, I'm, I'm hopeful we're going to start to get a readout early in the autumn as to whether this thing works or not. A lot of this depends on the intensity of infection. So, in order to get a readout, you have to have a certain number of incident cases in the control vaccine population." He gestures with his hands to emphasize points. His expression remains serious and focused as he elaborates. "And that then tells you, you can look at the real vaccinated population and see whether they've been protected. So, I'm, I'm hoping that's going to happen pretty smartly this autumn. But then don't forget, there are, these vaccines are unlikely to completely sterilize a population. They're very likely to have an effect which works in a percentage, say 60 or 70%." He pauses briefly, then continues, "We'll have to look quite carefully, and the regulators will have to look quite carefully to make sure that it's done what we needed to do before it gets approved. So there will be a delay between the outcome of the trial and a decision whether it can be approved as a vaccine."

**1:18 - 1:37:**
The screen returns to the split view. The man on the left (the interviewer) is seen bringing his hand to his mouth, seemingly about to interject, but Professor Bell continues speaking. The interviewer looks apologetic. Professor Bell continues, "...And of course, anything that happens..." The interviewer quickly says, "Professor Sir John Bell," interrupting him slightly. Professor Bell nods, then says, "Yeah." The interviewer, having placed his hands together in a sort of apology, then says, "I'm sorry, I didn't mean to cut you right off like that." Professor Bell continues his thought from before the interruption, "Can I just say that anything that happens to undermine the legitimacy of regulators to make independent decisions is in my view profoundly unhelpful." He finishes speaking, and the interviewer says, "Professor Sir John Bell, thank you very much indeed for joining us." The video fades to black.

caption.video0:
The video presents a narrative about children recovering from autism, contrasting this with the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's (CDC) stance on the condition.

The video opens at **0:00-0:01** with a close-up of a young child lying on a blue and beige patterned carpet, wearing a light green t-shirt and light shorts. The child is holding a baby bottle to his mouth with his left hand while reaching for the yellow wheel of a gray and yellow toy dump truck with his right hand. The truck is overturned on the carpet. The audio features a male voice speaking about "the recovery of these kids."

At **0:01-0:09**, the scene transitions to a news desk with two anchors, a man and a woman, seated. The Kcal 9 logo is visible in the bottom right corner. The female anchor mentions "That time of year again, time to start thinking about those flu shots." The male anchor adds, "Also, there's a possible treatment for autism that some say is curing children."

From **0:09-0:15**, the screen splits. On the left, a woman in a green top and jeans is sitting on a white couch, reading a book to a young boy in a blue shirt and khaki shorts, who is leaning against her. On the right, the same boy, now wearing a dark blue polo shirt and khaki shorts, is running across a grassy lawn towards the camera. The female anchor's voice continues, "Baxter Burley looks and acts like a normal six-year-old because he is."

At **0:15-0:19**, the split screen continues. On the left, the boy is sitting on a white couch, looking thoughtful with his hand to his head. On the right, a close-up shot of a document shows a checkbox marked "Autistic (120)." The female anchor states, "That's why it's hard to imagine that at age three, he had full-blown autism."

From **0:19-0:25**, a woman in a white lab coat, identified by a name tag as a doctor, is shown sitting in an office, speaking directly to the camera. Bookshelves with various items are in the background. She says, "The same regional center that gave him the diagnosis, the same group of people, reversed his diagnosis."

At **0:25-0:33**, the screen splits again. On the left, a table listing "Cadmium," "Lead," "Mercury," "Nickel," "Platinum," and "Thallium" with corresponding numerical values is displayed. "Mercury" is listed as "13." On the right, a person is applying a substance to a child's arm. The table then changes, showing "Mercury" as "0.5." Text at the bottom reads "Not all children with autism respond to treatment." The female anchor explains, "Something else that reversed, the amount of mercury in his system. It went from a high level of 13, to what's considered almost negligible, 0.5."

From **0:33-0:40**, the woman from the news segment is now identified by a chyron as "JULIA BERLE" (mother of Baxter Burley). She is wearing a green jacket and smiling, talking to an unseen interviewer. When asked, "No longer considered autistic?", she replies, "No longer considered autistic. That was a big day. We actually call it the day he lost his diagnosis, we call his second birthday."

The scene transitions at **0:41-0:49** to a white station wagon driving on a sunny street, seen from the perspective of a car behind it. Traffic lights are visible, with one turning from red to green. The car has a bumper sticker that reads "1 in 91 is TOO MANY." The audio is of somber piano music.

At **0:49-0:53**, a different woman, with dark hair and a light green and white striped shirt, is shown sitting in an office, speaking. A framed document and a window are in the background. She says, "We see children that come through that are severely autistic and, you know, within a year they're mainstreamed in school and out of the spectrum."

The white station wagon is shown again from **0:53-0:56**, still with the "1 in 91 is TOO MANY" bumper sticker, as it drives through a sunny suburban area. The woman's voice continues over the footage.

From **0:56-0:58**, the woman in the office continues, "Some children turn around in a number of months, other children take years."

At **0:58-1:03**, the video returns to the first child from the beginning, still lying on the carpet with his bottle, reaching for the toy truck. He gently taps the truck with his finger. The somber piano music continues.

From **1:03-1:11**, a man and a woman are seated side-by-side on a couch, speaking. The man, with curly dark hair, wears a striped shirt and a red undershirt, identified as "Ford Riley Father." summary: Definitely a false & misleading post. This is a gold top SST (Serum Separator Tube)  The tubes have micronized silica particles which help clot the blood before centrifugation, & a gel at the bottom which separates blood cells from serum. The blood is meant to clot quickly.  https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Serum-separating_tube
tweetText:
This blood was taken from a vaccinated patient.

It usually takes 30 minutes for blood to clot in an SST tube, not 3 minutes. https://t.co/hxQUoozH33

caption.video0:
The video opens with a close-up shot of a person's hand, adorned with a vibrant blue medical glove, holding a transparent test tube with a yellow cap. The test tube contains a sample of what appears to be human blood.

As the video progresses, the person slightly tilts the test tube, allowing for a better view of its contents. The blood inside the tube is visibly separated into distinct layers, indicating significant coagulation. The bottom portion of the tube contains a dark, thick, reddish-brown mass, consistent with clotted blood. Above this, there's a lighter, reddish-pink fluid layer, likely plasma or serum that has separated from the cells. At the very bottom of the tube, beneath the main clot, there's a thin, opaque, off-white layer.

Throughout the visual presentation of the test tube, a female voice provides commentary:
- At **0:00**, the voice begins by stating, "So, this patient's blood..."
- By **0:04**, as the shot focuses on the layered blood, the voice continues, "...was drawn three minutes ago."
- Around **0:08**, as the person adjusts their grip on the tube, the voice observes, "Already clotting in the tube."
- From **0:16** to **0:21**, with the test tube still being held and slightly moved, the voice concludes with an assessment of the situation: "Not normal. Takes a half hour usually."

The video and audio effectively synchronize, with the visual evidence of rapid blood clotting being directly addressed and explained by the accompanying commentary, highlighting an unusual and potentially concerning medical observation.

The video features a young woman with fair skin and dark brown hair, tied up in a messy high bun with a pink scrunchie. She is wearing a light-colored, short-sleeved collared shirt with a subtle floral pattern and blue jeans. She is kneeling on dry, grassy ground, with a large stack of cut logs (firewood) behind her, extending into the distance under a bright, sunny sky.

Throughout the video, a slow, melancholic, and somewhat distorted or slowed-down instrumental melody plays continuously. The music has a somber, almost eerie tone.

Here's a synchronized breakdown of the video's content:

*   **0:00-0:02:** The woman is shown in profile, looking upwards with her eyes closed, a gentle smile on her face, basking in the sunlight. Text appears on screen: **"Do you wear sunscreen?"**
*   **0:02-0:05:** The woman's expression shifts. She looks down and slightly to the side, with a subtly sad or pensive look on her face. The text changes to: **"No"**
*   **0:05-0:08:** She now looks directly at the camera, smiling faintly. The text on screen reads: **"There is no PROOF the sun causes cancer"**
*   **0:08-0:10:** Her head is again tilted upwards, eyes closed, smiling, and enjoying the sun. The text changes to: **"We have been brainwashed to blame the sun instead of our diet"**
*   **0:10-0:12:** She maintains the same pose, looking up with closed eyes and a smile. The text changes again: **"The sun is not the enemy"**
*   **0:12-0:15:** The woman once again looks directly at the camera with a small smile. The text on screen appears as: **"Your diet and sunscreen are"** The video abruptly ends here, leaving the sentence incomplete, implying that diet and sunscreen *are* the enemy, or at least a problem, in contrast to the sun.

caption.video0:
The video opens with an illustration of a fancy, old-timey frame surrounding the phrase "ARE YOU GOOD WITH THAT?" in a distressed white font, flanked by two hand-shaped icons. The background is a blurred, lavish red office with dark leather furniture, gold accents, a bookshelf, a golden rhino head, and a golden AK-47 mounted on the wall. A painting of a shirtless man holding a gun is also visible. Upbeat, carnival-like music with a male singer begins.

**(0:00-0:02)** The camera slowly zooms out, revealing more of the red office. A bald man in a red suit stands with his back to the camera, looking at a large wall screen.
**(0:02-0:04)** The screen changes to a "BREAKING NEWS" graphic. The bald man walks into the frame, then back out. The singer says, "They're selling you safety, to make big money."
**(0:04-0:06)** The "BREAKING NEWS" graphic changes to a close-up of a police car's flashing blue lights with a news banner: "NEWS: ANOTHER MASS SHOOTING". A female voice says, "Breaking news this morning, a mass shooting." The bald man uses a remote to turn the screen black.
**(0:07-0:09)** The man, now identified as Don, in the red suit sits in a black leather chair.
**(0:09-0:10)** Don says, "God, I love this country." He leans back, appearing content.
**(0:10-0:14)** Don stands up, adjusts his cuffs, and walks towards the camera.
**(0:14-0:15)** Don exclaims, "Oh shit."
**(0:15-0:16)** The scene shifts to a computer screen with a video call interface. A single participant, "GARY - ROGUE SELLER," in a yellow-framed window, is biting his fingernails nervously. A phone rings and a notification sounds.
**(0:16-0:17)** The screen splits to show another participant, "LESLIE - TRICKY MARKETER," in a purple-framed window, looking at her phone.
**(0:17-0:20)** Gary pours liquid from a flask into a cup. Leslie says, "5 o'clock somewhere, hey Gary?" Gary looks up, startled, then smiles.
**(0:21-0:22)** The screen splits again, introducing "DON - GREEDY GUNMAKER" in a red-framed window. He looks stern. Gary quickly closes his flask and adjusts his bolo tie. Gary says, "Gotta stay limber."
**(0:23-0:25)** Leslie greets, "Morning, sir!" Gary adds, "Morning, boss!" Leslie smiles.
**(0:25-0:27)** Don leans back, then forward, slamming a pink file folder on his desk.
**(0:29-0:30)** Don says, "And I don't want you two slack-wits to screw this up." The screen reverts to the three-way call.
**(0:30-0:32)** Don states, "Big shootings mean big money, so let's make this quick." He gestures. Leslie looks serious, Gary nods.
**(0:33-0:34)** The call focuses on Don.
**(0:34-0:35)** Don asks Leslie about marketing.
**(0:35-0:37)** The call switches to Leslie, who moves to her laptop. She explains, "Unfortunately, the latest shooting has gun reform trending again." She turns to a large screen displaying a bar chart titled "GEN-Z TRENDING TOPICS BY PLATFORM," showing "Gun Reform" as a significant trending topic.
**(0:39-0:40)** Don looks annoyed.
**(0:40-0:41)** Don sighs, "Again with this shit." He looks around.
**(0:42-0:43)** Don asks about "our DC prick Tony."
**(0:43-0:47)** Leslie, now smiling confidently, replies, "He has a two-time with some senators that should seal the deal. But, he of course, sends his thoughts and prayers."
**(0:49-0:50)** Don is focused, clasping his hands and leaning in. He says, "Good."
**(0:51-0:55)** Don makes controlled gestures, stating, "I want them buttered up. And I want them on strings."
**(0:55-1:01)** He emphasizes, "The amount of money that I give these people, I want to see persuasion, not legislation. I don't want them messing with our sales." The call switches to Leslie.
**(1:02-1:04)** Leslie says, "Speaking of, let's talk messaging options." She walks towards the camera, gesturing.
**(1:04-1:12)** She explains, "Our goal should always be to deflect. It's an unsolvable problem. You know, the shooter was unstable, mental health crisis."
**(1:12-1:13)** She continues, "Guns don't kill people..." The screen splits to show all three. Don shakes his head. Gary raises a fist.
**(1:13-1:14)** Gary interjects, "...people do!" Leslie smiles and snaps her fingers. Gary drinks from his cup.
**(1:15-1:19)** Leslie says, "Exactly. Then we evolve the message and own the headlines. We'll turn this tragedy into strategy like we always do." The call focuses on Leslie. Gary shrugs, concerned.
**(1:21-1:22)** The call focuses on Gary, who asks, "Is it always this easy?"
**(1:22-1:23)** The call splits. Don gestures to Gary, saying, "Don't be a snowflake, Gary. I want to see something tangible." Leslie looks at her phone.
**(1:25-1:28)** Don asks, "How are we handling the marketing around this?" Leslie turns to her laptop.
**(1:28-1:30)** Leslie replies, "Yes, let me share my screen. My team is already on it." She turns her back. Her screen now shares a full-screen image: a man aiming a large gun from a doorway, with a child and woman behind him. Text reads "DEFEND WHAT'S YOURS." Don looks satisfied, Gary disturbed.
**(1:31-1:35)** Leslie states, "The only way to protect your family is with guns." The image changes to two women in patriotic bikinis, each holding an assault rifle, with "GRAB A PAIR," "BOGO," and "50% OFF" stickers.
**(1:36-1:37)** Leslie says, "The more, the bigger, the better." The image changes to a shirtless man holding a large gun with "BE THE GUY WITH THE BIGGEST ONE." Gary looks confused.
**(1:38-1:39)** Leslie says, "Manipulate the wannabe alphas." The image shows a soldier and a civilian, both with assault rifles, with "MADE FOR ALL WARZONES."
**(1:40-1:42)** Leslie adds, "It's an arms race with the bad guys." The image changes to a patriotic poster with gun outlines, saying "BUY OR DIE."
**(1:42-1:47)** Leslie concludes, "Next time, it could be you. Etc, etc. We're exploring all angles and nothing is off limits." The call focuses on Gary, who raises his hand, distressed.
**(1:48-1:49)** Gary asks, "Well, if nothing's off limits, what about..." He then pulls out white underwear and holds them up excitedly.
**(1:50-1:55)** Leslie turns to her screen, looking disgusted. Gary exclaims, "Gunderpants!"
**(1:55-1:56)** The call focuses on Don, who stares blankly.
**(1:56-1:57)** Leslie looks even more disgusted and sighs deeply.
**(1:58-2:00)** The screen splits to all three. Don holds his hands up, interrupting Gary, and says, "Leslie, I like the stoking fear options."
**(2:01-2:09)** Don leans in intensely, saying, "Let's crank up the fear. Our guns are the only solution." He emphasizes each word.
**(2:09-2:10)** Leslie is shown writing, saying, "Understood. More fear."
**(2:12-2:13)** The screen splits. Don raises his hand and asks Gary about sales.
**(2:14-2:19)** Gary, nervous and fidgeting, replies, "Oh, from my end, sales are actually pretty great, sir. Uh, there's just one problem, sir. It's the, uh, guns I'm selling." Don gestures impatiently.
**(2:21-2:22)** Don urges, "Spit it out, what is it?"
**(2:23-2:26)** Gary continues, "They just keep being used for crimes, that's all, like all the damn time." The call shows all three. Gary looks increasingly distressed.
**(2:27-2:28)** Gary says, "Cops keep showing up." Don raises two fingers.
**(2:28-2:30)** Gary starts, "I'm a little worried they're gonna—" Don cuts him off, smiling.
**(2:30-2:31)** Don says, "That's perfect." He leans back, still smiling.
**(2:32-2:37)** Don says, "We'll send you more product ASAP. Now, what you need to do is you need to push real hard." He makes a fist. The call shows all three.
**(2:37-2:38)** Don states, "More street violence means more demand." Gary writes furiously.
**(2:39-2:40)** Gary says, "Oh, well, I can definitely keep doing that, sir." He looks up, points his finger, and lists taglines.
**(2:41-2:46)** Gary says, "Uh, while I have you, got some new taglines. Um, 'No ID, no problem!'" He holds his hands up.
**(2:46-2:49)** Gary continues, "Uh, waiting period? How about 'No waiting, period!'" Don raises his hands.
**(2:49-2:51)** Don says, "Okay, all right, all right, just calm down, Princess." The call focuses on Don.
**(2:52-2:55)** Don asks about new gun safeties.
**(2:55-2:59)** The call switches to all three. Don taps his cigar. Leslie says, "Uh, Rick from innovation was working on phasing those out, like you asked." Gary looks down.
**(3:00-3:01)** Don says, "Good."
**(3:01-3:05)** Don gestures, "I want those things to be so easy to shoot that even a toddler could shoot it."
**(3:06-3:08)** The call switches to all three. Don chuckles. Leslie, looking excited, says, "Oh, speaking of the youth market..." She retrieves something. Don takes a puff. Gary leans in.
**(3:09-3:12)** Leslie says, "Merchandising just showed me prototypes of the new plushy AR-15 toys, which are just too good."
**(3:12-3:15)** The call switches to Leslie, proudly holding up a plush AR-15 with googly eyes.
**(3:15-3:16)** Leslie giggles, "The kids are going to love 'em." She places the toy gun on her desk and turns her back to the camera.
**(3:17-3:22)** Leslie says, "Uh, but we do have a problem with some Gen Z kids running around, waving stories about guns are the number one killer of kids in the country." Don's smile fades. Gary looks concerned.
**(3:25-3:26)** Don asks, "Is that true?" Leslie confirms, "Yeah."
**(3:28-3:30)** Don leans back, a smile growing, and exclaims, "Suck it, Big Tobacco!" He takes a puff from his cigar and laughs maniacally.
**(3:31-3:33)** The call shows all three. Leslie looks annoyed. Gary laughs and makes finger guns. Don continues his maniacal laughter. The background music intensifies.
**(3:35-3:42)** The video transitions to a black screen with white text: "THE GUN INDUSTRY IS A #KILLERBUSINESS THAT CLAIMS TO SELL YOU SAFETY." The music continues with lyrics "They're selling you safety, to make big money. It's the gun industry, honey."
**(3:42-3:45)** More white text appears: "IN REALITY, YOUR SAFETY IS FOR SALE." with hand icons. The screen changes to the "ARE YOU GOOD WITH THAT?" text, adding "IF NOT, JOIN US." and a red rectangle. The rectangle fills with "AREYOUGOODWITH THAT.COM." "STUDENTS DEMAND ACTION" appears below. The carnival music continues, then fades out, followed by a dial tone.
