tweetText: Anti-vaxxers going crazy over headlines from the @Telegraph misrepresenting an already bad paper from the @bmj_latest just shows what has always been true. Anti-vaxxers don't read. https://t.co/rfmfhYEhul

caption.video0:
The video features a man, Dr. Wilson (a PhD molecular biologist), sitting in front of a bookshelf and two colorful paintings. He has curly dark hair, a beard, and is wearing glasses and a dark blue t-shirt with "SCIENCE" written in white. A microphone is positioned to his left.

**00:00 - 00:03**: Dr. Wilson sighs heavily, saying, "Oh lord, oh lord. Anti-vaxxers are reading headlines again and thinking that they are right all along."

**00:03 - 00:07**: The video transitions to a montage of overlapping social media posts.
- The first visible post is from @robinmonotti: "BOMBSHELL STUDY: 3 MILLION EXCESS DEATHS IN 47 COUNTRIES 'Researchers from The Netherlands point out COVID-19 vaccines may be to blame'". The text continues, "A bombshell study was recently published in BMJ Public Health by a group of researchers (Mostert et al.) from Vrije Universiteit, Amsterdam. The group's findings were shared in the mainstream media with The Telegraph daring to write: 'Covid vaccines may have helped fuel rise in excess deaths.' In addition, they referred to an important study which revealed that excess deaths were only seen in Germany 'from April 2021 onward.' The German authors went on to state: 'These findings indicate that something must have happened in spring 2021 that led to a sudden and sustained increase in mortality.'"
- Another post, from @VigilantNews, reads "BOMBSHELL BMJ Study: 3 Million Excess Deaths in 47 Countries Linked to COVID Vaccine The dam has officially broken." accompanied by a video of water rushing out of a dam.
- A third post from @toadmeister (Toby Young) says, "A study in the BMJ has linked excess deaths with COVID-19 vaccines and the Daily Telegraph has featured it on its front page. Are people slowly waking up to what was done to them?" accompanied by an image of a gloved hand filling a syringe from a vial.
- A final post shows a graphic from The Telegraph with a title "Covid vaccines may have helped fuel rise in excess deaths" over an image of red syringes.

**00:07 - 00:12**: Dr. Wilson returns to the frame, asking rhetorically, "I mean, who's gonna have the heart to tell them that they're just being gullible and not actually reading anything before making up their mind?"

**00:12 - 00:20**: Dr. Wilson points to himself and says, "Me, I have the heart. Hello, I'm Dr. Wilson, I'm a PhD molecular biologist, and welcome to another debunking video. So yeah, newspapers like The Telegraph and The Daily Mail are running headlines like this..."

**00:20 - 00:24**: The screen shows The Telegraph tweet again: "Covid vaccines may have helped fuel rise in excess deaths" with the red syringes graphic.

**00:24 - 00:40**: Dr. Wilson continues, "...saying that a study published in the BMJ is suggesting that vaccines may be responsible for excess deaths. This has prompted many influencers on social media to proclaim that they were right all along, that vaccines were dangerous, and that they're now vindicated because the mass media headlines say so."

**00:40 - 00:50**: Dr. Wilson looks to his left, seemingly confused, and asks, "Aren't these people supposed to not trust mass media? I guess that role only applies for when the newspapers go against what they already believe. But if they say something they like, they swallow it without question."

**00:50 - 1:04**: Dr. Wilson says, "So yeah, if you can't tell already, I'm just going to hit you with some cold water right away. These headlines are absolutely wrong, and that BMJ study does not even remotely demonstrate that vaccines are anywhere near responsible for excess deaths."

**1:04 - 1:13**: He states, "So, let's just put our thinking caps on, read a little bit past the headlines, go actually to the primary source, the BMJ article, and assess whether or not they actually found evidence that vaccines are causing excess deaths."

**1:13 - 1:17**: He asks, "So what are the authors in this BMJ article doing?"

**1:17 - 1:20**: The screen shows a screenshot of the BMJ Public Health article: "Excess mortality across countries in the Western World since the COVID-19 pandemic: 'Our World in Data' estimates of January 2020 to December 2022". A section of text is highlighted: "For our analysis, weekly all-cause mortality reports from the 'Our World in Data' database were converted to monthly reports. Subsequently, the monthly reports were converted to annual reports."

**1:20 - 1:30**: Dr. Wilson explains, "They're going to a database called Our World in Data. Our World in Data is a website that posts and compiles data from all over the world on all sorts of different topics, including data relevant to the COVID pandemic. One of the things they list are excess deaths."

**1:30 - 1:39**: The screen changes to a slide titled "Excess mortality during COVID-19" with a subsection "What is 'excess mortality'?". The definition states: "Excess mortality is a term used in epidemiology and public health that refers to the number of deaths from all causes during a crisis above and beyond what we would have expected to see under 'normal' conditions."

**1:39 - 1:52**: Dr. Wilson clarifies, "These are deaths that occurred in a country that are above a projected baseline. To put this really simply, you can think of it this way: If you take an average across five years of all the deaths that occur in a given country, then that is your baseline. And any deaths above that average will be considered excess." The slide changes to show "How is excess mortality measured?" with the formula "Excess Deaths = Reported Deaths - Expected Deaths" and notes about using an estimate produced by Karlinsky and Kobak's World Mortality Dataset.

**1:52 - 2:04**: The screen displays a graph titled "Excess mortality: Deaths from all causes compared to projection" with lines for the United States (blue) and United Kingdom (red) from Jan 5, 2020, to Dec 31, 2023. Both countries show significant spikes in excess mortality, particularly in early 2020 and early 2021. Dr. Wilson states, "The COVID pandemic saw huge increases in excess deaths in most countries in the world. No surprise given it was a pandemic with a new virus that was killing up to 2,000 Americans a day at one point."

**2:04 - 2:18**: Dr. Wilson explains, "But what these authors are saying is that these excess deaths have continued into 2021 and 2022. And they're saying that this increase in excess deaths, despite the rollout of vaccines and better treatments for COVID, is worrying." The screen shows the BMJ article's "Results" and "Conclusions" sections. The "Conclusions" are highlighted, stating: "Excess mortality has remained high in the Western World for three consecutive years, despite the implementation of containment measures and COVID-19 vaccines. This raises serious concerns. Government leaders and policymakers need to thoroughly investigate underlying causes of persistent excess mortality."

**2:18 - 2:34**: Dr. Wilson states, "That's pretty much it. That's all this paper is doing. It's not even offering a new analysis, it's just posting data from Our World in Data and commenting on it, which is kind of a lazy study, and I'm surprised that it got published in the BMJ."

**2:34 - 2:48**: He continues, "So it's a direct misrepresentation of the paper to say that it is suggesting that vaccines are causing these excess deaths, when they don't analyze or do anything to address the question of whether or not that's the case."

**2:48 - 3:18**: Dr. Wilson then says, "And just to show you how ridiculous that idea is and how lazy this paper was, if you take data from Our World in Data and look at COVID deaths for the same 47 countries involved in this BMJ study, and overlay those COVID deaths with excess deaths in the same graph that was used in the paper, in the same timeline that was used in the paper, you see that the COVID deaths align pretty perfectly with spikes in excess deaths. So, yeah, COVID is mostly responsible for these excess deaths." The screen displays a graph titled "EXCESS MORTALITY" with a red shaded area for "Excess deaths" and a blue shaded area (on top of the red) for "OWID Total COVID-19 deaths for the same 47 countries". The two shaded areas track very closely together, indicating COVID deaths largely account for excess mortality. A dotted line for "Linear trendline" is also visible.

**3:18 - 3:25**: Dr. Wilson acknowledges the graph, saying, "This figure is really easy to recreate on your own, but credit for making this particular one goes to Twitter user Uncle John, no relation to me." The graph appears again, embedded in a tweet from "@UncleJo46902375" which reads: "Since OWID is the go-to source, I thought I would just drop its COVID-19 deaths for the same 47 countries on top of their excess deaths plot. Vertical scales are the same." The tweet has 12.3K views.

**3:25 - 3:40**: Dr. Wilson remarks, "So, yeah, it's that easy to take these data, do a little bit more work and show that these excess deaths are not really mysterious. But if you wanted to be really thorough and ask, 'But are the vaccines contributing at all to these excess deaths?'"

**3:40 - 3:55**: He explains, "Well, you can do that. And this paper doesn't do that, but plenty of other papers already have. You just have to do a cohort study where one group of people has gotten vaccinated and another group of people has not gotten vaccinated for COVID." The screen shows an excerpt from "Safety outcomes following COVID-19 vaccination and infection in 5.1 million children in England" (Nature Communications), highlighting text that mentions no deaths from myocarditis in under-18s. Then an excerpt from "Safety monitoring of COVID-19 vaccines in Japan" (Lancet Reg Health West Pac), highlighting "To date, there has been no safety-related regulatory action in Japan for COVID-19 vaccine-associated deaths."

**3:55 - 4:10**: Dr. Wilson continues, "And then you simply ask, do all cause deaths increase in the vaccine group relative to the unvaccinated group? And the answer so far, in every single study, which has been multiple from all over the world, from a variety of different institutions and a variety of different universities, has been no." The screen shows an excerpt from "Risk for Myocardial Infarction, Stroke, and Pulmonary Embolism Following COVID-19 Vaccines in Adults Younger Than 75 Years in France" (Annals of Internal Medicine), highlighting "No association between mRNA-based vaccines and the cardiovascular events studied was observed." Then an excerpt from "Effectiveness of COVID-19 Vaccination in Preventing All-Cause Mortality among Adults during the Third Wave of the Epidemic in Hungary: Nationwide Retrospective Cohort Study" (Vaccine), highlighting "The HVE-corrected general mortality for COVID-19 vaccine cohorts demonstrated the real-life effectiveness of vaccines applied in Hungary...". Finally, an excerpt from "A safety study evaluating non-COVID-19 mortality risk following COVID-19 vaccination" (Vaccine), highlighting "crude non-COVID-19 mortality rates among COVID-19 vaccinees were lower than those among comparators."

**4:10 - 4:18**: Dr. Wilson asserts, "COVID vaccines are not increasing the risk of all cause deaths. Therefore, there is no evidence that they are contributing to excess deaths."

**4:18 - 4:34**: He suggests another approach, "We can also look at this other ways. We can take countries that have a higher percentage of their population vaccinated, compare them to countries with a lower percentage of their populations vaccinated, and look at excess deaths over the 2021 to 2022 time period." The screen shows a scatter plot titled "Cumulative 2021-2023 All Cause Excess Mortality and Primary Series Vaccination DURING EUROPEAN WAVES AFTER VACCINATION: Jan 2021-Jun 2021 and Oct 2021 to May 2022". The plot shows a downward trend, meaning higher vaccination percentage correlates with lower cumulative excess all-cause mortality.

**4:34 - 5:12**: Dr. Wilson confirms, "And we see that the more vaccinated the country is, the fewer excess deaths. In fact, if you look at the perfect country for testing the idea of whether vaccines cause excess deaths, New Zealand, during the time they vaccinated most of their population, there were no COVID cases in the country. And during this time, they had negative excess deaths." The screen shows a chart from Our World in Data, "Excess mortality: Cumulative deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years, per million people" for New Zealand, showing a cumulative negative excess mortality during 2020-2022, before rising towards baseline in late 2022 and 2023. He continues, "And when the country did open back up and SARS-CoV-2 entered the population, instead of seeing huge spikes in excess deaths like we did in other countries when they were dealing with SARS-CoV-2 while most their population was unvaccinated, New Zealand's excess deaths simply went from negative to back to baseline. How is this possible if vaccines are contributing to excess deaths?" The screen then shows another Our World in Data chart, "Estimated daily excess deaths during COVID, New Zealand", which shows daily confirmed COVID-19 deaths (black line) and a central excess death estimate (red line) returning to zero or slightly above zero after an initial period of negative excess deaths.

**5:12 - 5:37**: Dr. Wilson acknowledges a valid point from the BMJ paper, "With all that said, the one useful point that this paper does make, which is pretty obvious and we didn't need the paper for, is that we can do better. There are still people dying of COVID every day. And although the total numbers of deaths are way lower than they were in 2020 and 2021, we can still be doing better because most of these people who are dying are people who are vulnerable to COVID and are not receiving prompt treatment."

**5:37 - 6:03**: He adds, "But I am a scientist and not a medical doctor, so if you want to learn more about what we can be doing better to give early treatment to COVID patients, I'll refer you to Dr. Daniel Griffin. He's an MD, PhD, an infectious disease expert, and a science communicator, hosting a weekly clinical update including all the information you want to know about COVID, its treatments, and how we're doing with it. That's actually going to be useful instead of this BMJ article and all of its associated headlines, which are totally bogus." The screen shows a slide titled "COVID- Early Viral Phase" with links to NIH COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines and a list of licensed treatments (Paxlovid, Remdesivir, Molnupiravir, Convalescent Plasma). An image of Daniel Griffin, M.D., Ph.D., CTropMed CTH, is on the right side.

**6:03 - 6:33**: Dr. Wilson emphasizes, "If these influencers who are sharing these headlines actually cared about the question of whether or not COVID vaccines are contributing to excess deaths, they would at least put on a little bit of a thinking cap and look a little bit into the paper to see if there wasn't an analysis of vaccinated versus unvaccinated groups. There was none of that. They didn't look for it, because they probably don't care. Anyway, in summary, is there anything at all in this BMJ article that supports this idea that COVID vaccines are contributing to excess deaths?"

**6:33 - 6:40**: He takes a big, exaggerated breath, then says, "No, sorry, I sucked in too much air. I'm getting old. Or..." He points a finger at the camera, and a sound effect plays, "...is it the vaccines? Checkmate nerds."

**6:40 - 7:09**: Dr. Wilson concludes the video, "Anyway, that's gonna do it for this week's video. All the science that I talked about and more are in the description below, including a link to the website covidresearch.net, which is a Zotero reference manager that some friends and I have been curating, and in it is a folder called 'COVID Vaccine Safety Studies', which provides links to several papers that reject this idea that COVID vaccines cause excess deaths. And as always, thank you so much for watching, I really do appreciate it. And if you enjoyed this, then don't forget to subscribe so you can catch me next week where I'll be debunking some more funky stuff. See you then."

**07:09 - 07:18**: An animated outro appears with the text "DEBUNK THE FUNK" in large, retro-style letters, and a cartoon illustration of Dr. Wilson holding a pointer stick, against a background of colorful wavy stripes. Playful music plays. The cartoon Dr. Wilson blinks.

tweetText:
New American mother is at the grocery store to buy baby formula

Prices are so high “I can’t tell if this is a joke or not”

A single can is $52.99-$74. Having babies is not affordable

Nestle lobbies against paid maternity leave so mothers are forced to buy this. It’s a racket https://t.co/okFLaDKca3

caption.video0:
The video opens with a medium shot of a woman in a supermarket aisle. She is wearing a white t-shirt and a dark headband with a floral pattern. Her hair is pulled back, and she has a shocked or confused expression, with wide eyes and her mouth slightly open. She holds a purple water bottle with a sticker of mountains on it in her left hand, and her right hand is near her face. Behind her, shelves are stocked with various products, including packages of diapers in green and white. Text overlay reads "I DON'T UNDERSTAND."

At 0:02, the camera cuts to a close-up of a supermarket shelf displaying numerous cans and boxes of infant formula. Above the shelf, a sign reads "FEEDING MY BABY." The woman's voice says, "I can't tell if this is a joke or not, but like what is 52.99 for a can..."
The camera pans slowly to the right. A hand with painted nails points to a can of Enfamil Infant Formula with a price tag of "$52.99." Another can of Nutramigen Hypoallergenic formula is priced at "$56.99." The woman's voice continues, "...CAN 56.99..."
The hand then points to a larger can of Nutramigen Hypoallergenic with a price tag of "$73.49." The woman says, "...73.49..."
The camera continues to pan right, showing two cans of Similac 360 Total Care, both priced at "$51.49" and "$51.39" respectively. The woman's voice is heard again, saying, "...51.49, 51.49. I don't understand how it's 73 dollars." The hand points to the $73.49 Nutramigen can again as she expresses her confusion about the high price.

claim_or_not:
Cleaned text: New American mother is at the grocery store to buy baby formula. Prices are so high “I can’t tell if this is a joke or not” A single can is $52.99-$74. Having babies is not affordable. Nestle lobbies against paid maternity leave so mothers are forced to buy this. It’s a racket.

The tweet expresses a claim/assertion/implication about a healthcare-related topic, specifically the high cost of baby formula and its impact on new mothers.

Yes.

tweetText: Travelling in crowded and poorly ventilated public transportation? #WearAMask to help keep you and your community safe from COVID-19, flu and other respiratory illnesses. #StaySafe https://t.co/SPqDjTAhSy

caption.video0:
The video is an animated public health announcement, approximately 22 seconds long, that advocates for mask-wearing in crowded places with poor ventilation.

**0:00 - 0:03: Initial Scene - Crowded Public Transport (First Group)**
The video opens with a scene depicting several cartoon-style characters sitting on what appears to be a public transport bench or similar seating. The background is a blurred light blue, suggesting movement.
*   From left to right: A person with brown skin and dark hair, wearing a brown shirt, is partially visible. Next to them is a woman with greenish-brown hair, wearing a long-sleeved red dress. She has a small child with curly brown hair, wearing a light blue t-shirt and light blue pants, sitting beside her. The child looks up at her with a happy expression. To their right, a woman with light brown curly hair wears a green sleeveless top and dark gray pants, smiling and looking right. Finally, a woman with long brown hair in a light blue dress smiles and gestures as if talking.
*   Initially, most characters are unmasked. However, at **0:00**, the woman in the red dress immediately puts on a light blue surgical mask.
*   **Audio:** Gentle, uplifting background music begins. A male narrator states: "If you are in crowded places with poor ventilation"

**0:03 - 0:06: Second Scene - Crowded Public Transport (Second Group)**
The scene transitions to a different group of people, also seated in a row, reinforcing the idea of public transport.
*   From left to right: A bald man in a suit and tie, already wearing a light blue mask, sits with his eyes downcast. Next to him, a man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a yellow long-sleeved shirt and blue pants, holds a wooden cane and is also wearing a light blue mask. A blonde-haired person with headphones wears a dark gray long-sleeved shirt with a yellow collar and dark gray pants, holding an orange smartphone. They are initially unmasked. Further right, a person with dark skin and dark hair, wearing a light blue hoodie, stands slightly hunched.
*   At **0:03**, the standing person in the blue hoodie briefly brings a light blue mask to their face, then quickly drops their hand, revealing their open mouth as if coughing or sneezing. The blonde-haired person with headphones looks up from their phone with a slightly concerned expression, glancing towards the coughing individual.
*   **Audio:** The narrator continues: "such as public transportation," At **0:03**, a distinct sound effect of a cough or sneeze is heard.

**0:06 - 0:09: Returning to First Group - Masking Up**
The video switches back to the first group of characters.
*   The woman in the red dress and the child are now both wearing light blue masks, and the child is still smiling. The woman with light brown curly hair, who was previously smiling, is now seen holding a tissue to her nose, as if having just sneezed or blown her nose. The woman in the light blue dress is looking at her.
*   At **0:07**, the woman with light brown curly hair (who was holding the tissue) puts on a light blue mask.
*   At **0:08**, the woman in the light blue dress also puts on a light blue mask.
*   **Audio:** Narrator: "wear a mask" At **0:06**, another cough/sneeze sound effect is heard.

**0:09 - 0:12: Returning to Second Group - All Masked**
The scene cuts back to the second group of people.
*   All characters are now wearing light blue surgical masks. The bald man, the man with the cane, the blonde-haired person with headphones (now looking forward), and the standing person in the light blue hoodie (also looking forward) are all masked.
*   **Audio:** Narrator: "because it keeps you and your community safe from COVID-19, flu, and other respiratory illnesses."

**0:12 - 0:14: Returning to First Group - All Masked and Safe**
The scene switches back to the first group one last time.
*   All visible characters are now wearing light blue masks. The person with brown skin is now masked. The woman in red and the child are masked, the child still smiling. The woman with light brown hair is masked and smiling. The woman in the light blue dress is also masked and smiling. The overall impression is one of safety and health through mask-wearing.
*   **Audio:** The narration continues seamlessly from the previous segment: "from COVID-19, flu, and other respiratory illnesses."

**0:14 - 0:17: Text Screen**
The video transitions to a solid dark blue screen.
*   At **0:15**, white text appears in the center: "Live your life"
*   At **0:16**, more white text appears below it: "and stay safe and healthy"
*   **Audio:** Narrator: "Live your life and stay safe and healthy."

**0:17 - 0:22: Logo Screen**
The text disappears, and the World Health Organization (WHO) logo appears centrally on the dark blue background.
*   The logo features a white emblem of a globe with a snake wrapped around a staff, encircled by olive branches.
*   To the right of the logo, in white text, it reads:
    "World Health
    Organization
    Western Pacific Region"
*   **Audio:** The background music gently fades out at **0:17** and then ends.

caption.video0:
The video is titled "Why I thought it was Autism Back before and after his regression". It features a baby boy named Jack, highlighting his development before and after a period of regression, which the parents initially thought might be autism. The video is set to a melancholic song with a male vocalist.

**Segment 1: Before Regression (0:00 - 1:29)**

*   **0:00 - 0:05**: The video opens with a cheerful baby boy sitting in a white and green activity center (bouncer/jumper). He is wearing a grey top and blue overalls with a Mickey Mouse print. He is actively bouncing and laughing, looking directly at the camera with wide, happy eyes. Toys on the activity center are visible.
*   **0:05 - 0:11**: The text "had great eye contact", "responded to his name", and "interacted" appears on screen. Jack continues to bounce, smile, and look at the camera, occasionally reaching for the toys. His parents' voices can be heard encouraging him, saying "Your turn," "Jump," and "Jack."
*   **0:11 - 0:16**: Jack continues to interact, looking towards the camera and smiling, and then briefly to his left. His parents' voices are audible, saying "Yes," and "Boing, boing."
*   **0:16 - 0:21**: The camera zooms in on Jack's face. He is still smiling and occasionally looking away, but quickly re-engages with the camera. He bounces energetically.
*   **0:21 - 0:26**: Jack looks around, but then smiles brightly at the camera. He seems very responsive.
*   **0:26 - 0:30**: Jack looks to his left and then back at the camera, smiling. His parents' voices are heard.
*   **0:30 - 0:34**: Jack bounces in the activity center. The text "until around 10 months" appears. He is smiling and looking at the camera.
*   **0:34 - 0:39**: Jack is bouncing but sometimes looks more focused on the toys on the activity center, though he still occasionally looks at the camera and smiles.
*   **0:39 - 0:45**: He continues to bounce and engage with the toys and the camera, displaying happiness.
*   **0:45 - 0:50**: Jack looks around and then smiles broadly at the camera. His parents can be heard saying "Boing, boing" and "You."
*   **0:50 - 0:55**: Jack bounces actively, looking at the camera and smiling.
*   **0:55 - 1:00**: Jack looks around, then back at the camera, smiling and bouncing.
*   **1:00 - 1:06**: Jack is bouncing energetically, making eye contact and smiling.
*   **1:06 - 1:11**: The camera is close on Jack's face. He is looking down at the toys, not at the camera. The text "then it all stopped...." appears. He seems less engaged.
*   **1:11 - 1:15**: Jack is seen playing with the toys, but his gaze is not directed at the camera.
*   **1:15 - 1:20**: The camera pulls back. Jack is in the activity center, but he is no longer bouncing or smiling. He is still, looking down at the toys. His parents encourage him, "Ready to jump?" "Jump, Jack." He does not respond.
*   **1:20 - 1:24**: Jack looks towards the camera briefly with a neutral expression, then looks down at the toys again. His parents say "Jack."
*   **1:24 - 1:29**: Jack is still in the activity center. He does not smile or make eye contact, focused on the toys. He does not respond to his name.

**Segment 2: After Regression (1:29 - 2:27)**

*   **1:29 - 1:35**: The scene changes to Jack in a stroller outdoors, wearing a yellow jacket with a furry hood. He is looking down, away from the camera. The text "avoiding eye contact", "stopped responding to his name", and "stopped interaction" appears. A parent's voice calls "Jack," but he does not look up.
*   **1:35 - 1:40**: The parent calls "Jack" again, but he remains looking down. The sun is bright, causing a lens flare.
*   **1:40 - 1:45**: The parent asks "What are you doing?" and calls "Jack" again. He does not respond and keeps his gaze averted.
*   **1:45 - 1:50**: The parent repeats "Jack," still no response.
*   **1:50 - 1:55**: The scene changes to Jack sitting in a grey and white baby chair indoors, wearing Mickey Mouse pajamas. He is holding a colorful toy. His gaze is unfocused and he does not look at the camera or respond when the parent calls "Jack."
*   **1:55 - 2:00**: The parent calls "Jack" again and asks "Jack, what are you doing?" He makes a small vocalization but remains unfocused.
*   **2:00 - 2:07**: The parent asks "What are you doing?" and calls "Jack." He makes another small vocalization, but still no eye contact or interaction.
*   **2:07 - 2:12**: The scene changes to Jack on a swing at a playground, wearing his yellow jacket and white pants. His eyes are wide open, but his gaze is distant and unfocused. He does not smile or show any excitement. A woman's hand is seen gently pushing the swing.
*   **2:12 - 2:17**: Jack remains with a distant gaze, his expression unchanging, as the swing moves.
*   **2:17 - 2:23**: The woman continues to push the swing. Jack maintains his blank stare, showing no interaction or engagement.
*   **2:23 - 2:27**: The video transitions to the TikTok logo.

The music plays continuously throughout the video, a soft, acoustic-style song. The singer's voice is gentle.

caption.video0:
The video captures a vaccination event, where a healthcare worker experiences a sudden medical incident after receiving a vaccine.

Here's a detailed description:

**0:00 - 0:02**: The scene opens with a medium shot of a woman, Tiffany Dover, seated. She is wearing blue scrubs and a light blue medical mask. Her left arm is exposed, and a medical professional, wearing a white lab coat and blue gloves, is administering an injection into her upper left arm. The background features a white banner with the "CHI Memorial" logo repeated. As the injection is being given, Tiffany Dover begins to speak, introducing herself.

**0:02 - 0:06**: The medical professional completes the injection and removes the needle, then applies a small bandage to Dover's arm. Dover continues speaking, explaining her role as a manager in the CCU (Coronary Care Unit) and the importance of her team, who work in the COVID unit, receiving the vaccine first. A "TOP STORY: FIRST VACCINATIONS BEGIN - CHI MEMORIAL" chyron appears at the bottom of the screen.

**0:06 - 0:17**: Dover finishes speaking, turns to face the camera more directly, and looks slightly emotional. She says, "...my team will be getting first chances to get the vaccine. And I know that um, it's really..." Her voice trails off, and she pauses, appearing to struggle for words.

**0:17 - 0:25**: Dover brings her right hand up to her forehead, rubbing it, her eyes closed or looking down. She apologizes, stating, "I'm sorry, I'm feeling really dizzy." Her head remains bowed, and her expression indicates increasing discomfort.

**0:25 - 0:34**: She continues to hold her hand to her forehead, her head dropping further. She appears to be on the verge of collapsing. A male voice asks, "You okay?" She softly responds, "Oh, I'm sorry." As she finishes speaking, her body begins to slump to the left, and her head drops. A man in a black shirt and mask, and another person in a white lab coat, quickly move to support her.

**0:34 - 0:39**: Dover is actively slumping forward in her chair, and the two individuals are seen trying to hold her up, preventing her from falling. The man in the black shirt holds her shoulders, while the person in the lab coat supports her head. Other indistinct voices can be heard in the background, expressing concern.

**0:39 - 0:42**: The camera briefly cuts away, showing the wall with the "CHI Memorial" logo and two small tables with red biohazard containers, implying other vaccination stations. Muffled sounds continue in the background.

**0:42 - 0:54**: The camera cuts back to Dover. She is now on the floor, being attended to by three medical professionals (two in white lab coats, one in a black shirt). They are leaning over her, providing assistance, though her exact condition is obscured by their bodies. The video ends with them still attending to her on the floor.

summary: Major depressive disorder (MDD), also known as clinical depression, is REAL.    https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/depression/symptoms-causes/syc-20356007    https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/9290-depression    https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/health/conditions-and-diseases/major-depression    https://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/topics/depression    https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/depression    https://www.health.harvard.edu/a_to_z/major-depression-a-to-z    https://www.psychiatry.org/patients-families/depression/what-is-depression    https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5875313/    https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamapsychiatry/fullarticle/2671413    It is a mental disorder characterized by at least two weeks of pervasive low mood, low self-esteem, and loss of interest or pleasure in normally enjoyable activities.    https://www.mredscircleoftrust.com/storage/app/media/DSM%205%20TR.pdf#page=309    https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder    https://www.uptodate.com/contents/image?imageKey=PSYCH/89994

caption.video0:
The video features a man with dark hair, dressed in a black suit and white shirt, sitting at a desk with a microphone. He is speaking directly into the microphone.

**0:00 - 0:03**: The man continues speaking into the microphone, looking directly at the camera.
- **Audio**: "There was this one take that you had early on that I really loved and it was..."

**0:03 - 0:05**: The man smiles and points his finger.
- **Audio**: "...Depression isn't real."

**0:05 - 0:09**: The man continues speaking, making hand gestures.
- **Audio**: "And over time, you've kind of stepped back on that a little bit, because people have gotten so crazy."

**0:09 - 0:10**: The man speaking is still visible.
- **Audio**: "I'll double down. I'll explain it again."

**0:10 - 0:11**: A black screen appears with white text: "WHY DEPRESSION IS NOT REAL".

**0:11 - 0:14**: The scene changes to a bald man with a beard and sunglasses, wearing a cream-colored cardigan over a black shirt, sitting in a brown leather chair, speaking into a microphone.
- **Audio**: "If I don't believe in depression, I cannot become depressed."

**0:14 - 0:16**: The video cuts to a man and a woman standing in a dimly lit room with a bookshelf behind them. A reflection of a third person is visible in a glass door behind them. The woman looks concerned.
- **Audio**: "If you have two people inside of a haunted house..."

**0:16 - 0:18**: The scene returns to the bald man speaking into the microphone. He holds up two fingers.
- **Audio**: "...and there's a noise in the night."

**0:18 - 0:20**: The bald man, wearing a cream blazer and sunglasses, is walking down a well-lit hotel hallway with doors on either side. He looks confident.
- **Audio**: "The one who doesn't believe in ghosts..."

**0:20 - 0:21**: The bald man, shirtless and wearing red and white striped shorts, walks past a dark building with red lighting. He has a determined expression.
- **Audio**: "...will assume it is wind..."

**0:21 - 0:22**: A black screen appears with white text: "AND GO BACK TO SLEEP".

**0:22 - 0:24**: A woman with long reddish-brown hair, wearing a light blue shirt and a beige knitted vest, sits on a couch, reading a book. A bookshelf filled with books is behind her.
- **Audio**: "The one who believes in ghosts..."

**0:24 - 0:25**: A woman with long dark hair lies in bed, looking intensely at the camera with wide eyes. The room is dark.
- **Audio**: "...will assume it's a ghost."

**0:25 - 0:27**: A woman with dark hair is shown on the phone, looking panicked and scared, with her hand gesturing near her face.
- **Audio**: "He will then wake up and panic and he will be afraid..."

**0:27 - 0:28**: A man with a beard, wearing a black clerical-style outfit, walks through a doorway with other people in the background.
- **Audio**: "...and he'll call an exorcist."

**0:28 - 0:30**: The bald man with sunglasses is back, speaking into the microphone.
- **Audio**: "It is the belief in the ghost that gives it power..."

**0:30 - 0:31**: The bald man gestures with his hands while speaking.
- **Audio**: "...not the noise itself."

**0:31 - 0:35**: The bald man continues speaking. The scene then cuts to him in a gym, shirtless, with a tattoo on his torso, resting with one knee on the ground while other men box in the background.
- **Audio**: "The reason I say depression isn't real is because now I can no longer become depressed because I don't believe it's real."

**0:35 - 0:39**: The bald man is shown lying shirtless on a top bunk bed in a sparse room with another bunk bed and a small toilet. He then stands up and looks at a poster on the wall.
- **Audio**: "So if I get sent to a Romanian jail cell and I have no idea how long I'm gonna be in there for."

**0:39 - 0:40**: The bald man is shirtless, looking up and flexing his muscles, with wooden beams in the background.
- **Audio**: "And I'm suffering in there."

**0:40 - 0:44**: The bald man is back in the studio, speaking into the microphone.
- **Audio**: "I can't become a depressed person because I don't believe in the idea or the mental model of depression."

**0:44 - 0:47**: The bald man continues speaking and gesturing.
- **Audio**: "Believing in it gives it power, and I refuse to believe in things that take away power from me."

**0:47 - 0:50**: A black screen appears with white text: "THAT TAKE AWAY POWER FROM ME". Then, a man with blue hair and a beard, wearing a blue shirt, speaks into a microphone, with a city skyline in the background.
- **Audio**: "And anybody who sits at home and argues with me that depression is real..."

**0:50 - 0:51**: A black screen appears with white text: "THAT DEPRESSION IS REAL".

**0:51 - 0:57**: The bald man is back, speaking into the microphone.
- **Audio**: "Why would I adopt your thinking if it makes me less powerful? Why would I adopt your thinking if it makes me less capable of dealing with difficult scenarios?"

**0:57 - 0:59**: The bald man is sitting shirtless outdoors, smoking a cigar and looking at his phone. Dumbbells and a coffee mug are on a table in front of him.
- **Audio**: "This isn't even about being correct anymore."

**0:59 - 1:02**: The bald man is shirtless, lifting dumbbells in a dark room with a red motorcycle visible.
- **Audio**: "This is about adopting a mental model which makes me as competitive as possible."

**1:02 - 1:04**: The bald man, wearing a patterned blue shirt, walks quickly through a modern city environment at night, with buildings and cars blurred in the background.
- **Audio**: "Which makes me as competitive as possible."

**1:04 - 1:09**: The bald man is speaking into the microphone again.
- **Audio**: "If me believing depression is real will put me in a position where I believe I'm a depressed person and I might wanna kill myself, why would I believe it?"

**1:09 - 1:11**: A black screen appears with white text: "WHY WOULD I BELIEVE IT". Then, a man (possibly the bald man) stands in front of a fire outdoors at night.
- **Audio**: "Sounds like setting myself on fire."

**1:11 - 1:23**: The bald man is speaking into the microphone.
- **Audio**: "I refuse. Depression is not real, so I cannot become depressed. That is what I believe and you cannot change my mind because changing my mind will make me weaker. And why would I adopt the thinking or the mental model of somebody weaker than me? I don't believe in depression, that's the bottom line of it, so it's not real."

**1:23 - 1:27**: A black screen with a white "Tate" logo and a golden chess knight, and the text "COBRATATE.COM" below it.The video opens with a man in a black suit and white shirt, with dark hair, speaking into a microphone. He says, "There was this one take that you had early on that I really loved and it was..." His expression becomes serious, then he smiles slightly as he adds, "Depression isn't real." He continues, explaining, "And over time, you've kind of stepped back on that a little bit, because people have gotten so crazy." The man appears to be in a studio or podcast setting.

Suddenly, the video cuts to a different speaker, a bald man with a beard and sunglasses, wearing a cream-colored cardigan over a black shirt, also seated and speaking into a microphone. This man, identified as Tate, forcefully states, "I'll double down. I'll explain it again."

A black screen with white text appears, proclaiming, "WHY DEPRESSION IS NOT REAL."

Tate is then shown again, asserting, "If I don't believe in depression, I cannot become depressed." The video transitions to a scene from a movie, showing a man and a woman looking concerned in a dimly lit room with a bookshelf. Tate's voice continues, "If you have two people inside of a haunted house and there's a noise in the night..."

The scene returns to Tate, who gestures with two fingers, saying, "The one who doesn't believe in ghosts will assume it is wind..." The screen goes black with the text "AND GO BACK TO SLEEP."

Next, a woman is seen sitting on a couch, reading a book, while Tate's voice continues, "...The one who believes in ghosts will assume it's a ghost." A quick cut shows a woman in bed looking terrified, followed by another shot of a woman on the phone with a panicked expression. Tate concludes, "...He will then wake up and panic and he will be afraid and he'll call an exorcist." A man in black, possibly an exorcist, is shown walking purposefully through a doorway.

Tate reappears on screen, stating, "It is the belief in the ghost that gives it power, not the noise itself." He then connects this analogy to his previous statement: "The reason I say depression isn't real is because now I can no longer become depressed because I don't believe it's real."

The video shifts to various clips of Tate: shirtless in a gym while others box, then lying on a bunk bed in what appears to be a jail cell, and later standing beside the bunk. He explains, "So if I get sent to a Romanian jail cell and I have no idea how long I'm gonna be in there for, and I'm suffering in there, I can't become a depressed person because I don't believe in the idea or the mental model of depression."

Returning to the interview setting, Tate emphatically states, "Believing in it gives it power, and I refuse to believe in things that take away power from me." The screen briefly shows a man with blue hair speaking into a microphone, then text appears: "THAT TAKE AWAY POWER FROM ME."

Tate continues, "And anybody who sits at home and argues with me that depression is real, why would I adopt your thinking if it makes me less powerful? Why would I adopt your thinking if it makes me less capable of dealing with difficult scenarios?"

The video again shows quick cuts of Tate in different settings: shirtless, smoking a cigar while looking at his phone outdoors, then lifting weights in a home gym, and finally walking through a city at night. His voiceover states, "This isn't even about being correct anymore. This is about adopting a mental model which makes me as competitive as possible."

Back in the interview, Tate reiterates his core belief: "If me believing depression is real will put me in a position where I believe I'm a depressed person and I might wanna kill myself, why would I believe it?" A brief image of a man by a fire appears. "Sounds like setting myself on fire. I refuse. Depression is not real, so I cannot become depressed. That is what I believe and you cannot change my mind because changing my mind will make me weaker. And why would I adopt the thinking or the mental model of somebody weaker than me? I don't believe in depression, that's the bottom line of it, so it's not real."

The video ends with a black screen displaying the "Tate" logo, a golden chess knight, and the website "COBRATATE.COM".

caption.video0:
The video opens inside a hospital hallway, with a man in a light blue kurta and glasses walking purposefully while holding a phone. Other men, some in shirts and trousers, follow him. As he walks, the man in the kurta asks, in English, "Who's the CMO here?" then repeats in Konkani, "CMO Konna re?" meaning "Who is the CMO?". He continues walking, past a woman in a green top and mask, and other medical staff.

At 0:08, a female doctor in a white lab coat and blue mask appears on the left, looking surprised and then distressed, covering her mouth with her hand. The man in the kurta reiterates, "Who's the CMO?" and then, with more urgency, "Where is the CMO, all of you, where is the CMO? Where is he?" He points his finger in the air while speaking. He then says, "Call him."

At 0:17, the man walks into an office area with a desk and chairs, still holding his phone. He sits down at a desk. At 0:21, a doctor in a white lab coat and a blue mask approaches the desk where the man is seated. The man points his finger at the doctor and says, in Konkani, "Ulo Pao sarke Ritte na. Atam presser tu ek Munsal injetion makapuk you cannot guide him properly. Tu learn to control your tongue. You're a doctor. Don't force me to act. I generally don't lose my cool. But you have to behave yourself. However much you're loaded, you will talk properly to the patient. And guide the patient." (English: "You are not talking properly now. Now you will inject a person under pressure. You cannot guide him properly. You learn to control your tongue. You're a doctor. Don't force me to act. I generally don't lose my cool. But you have to behave yourself. However much you're loaded, you will talk properly to the patient. And guide the patient.") He then says in Konkani, "Urban health center and chal. Ngaa chal mhnon tu kithea karavona. Kithea katar job diata governmentan. Ulo bag rri kason jaithu tu? Tu doctor tu?" (English: "You said Urban Health Centre and go. How will you do that? What job will the government give you? How will you talk badly? You're a doctor!") He removes his mask while speaking. The man continues speaking in Konkani, "Mask kad bairi. Maka taan bolong zai tu. Sambhalun. Eka suspension order kadd. You're a doctor to serve the poor people here." (English: "Take off the mask. I want to talk to you. Be careful. Get a suspension order. You're a doctor to serve the poor people here.")

At 1:07, other men in shirts and trousers, and some medical staff, gather around. The man in the kurta says, "I am suspending you. When the inquiry is initiated, you give your explanation. Suspension. I want him relieved right now from here. Bhasha au vaparta ekdum naram. Ani jyamond..." (English: "I am suspending you. When the inquiry is initiated, you give your explanation. Suspension. I want him relieved right now from here. The language I use is very soft. And the person...") The man then tells the doctor, "I am telling you, you listen. Go home. Go home. You can go home. They'll let the residents and senior residents manage. You go home."

At 1:28, the man in the kurta is outside the hospital, walking and speaking to someone off-camera. He says, "I visited the GMC sir. I visited. I visited for one patient specially. I saw the behavior. And the main thing is, time to time our doctors, all of them do good work, what do they lack. We were hearing complaints of the people about a CMO, a Dr. Rudresh, so I gave orders for his suspension. Such doctors who cannot serve the poor people, they have no work to do in government service. We are ministers, so we work, but such situations should not happen." He continues, "And the doctors of Goa Medical College work very well, but sometimes such strange creatures are also born, what will they do?" The video ends.The video begins inside a hospital hallway. A man in a light blue kurta and glasses walks briskly, holding a phone. Several other men, some in shirts and trousers, follow him. He speaks in English, asking, "Who's the CMO here?", then repeats the question in Konkani: "CMO Konna re?" (Who is the CMO?). He continues past a woman in a green top and mask, and other medical personnel.

At 0:08, a female doctor in a white lab coat and blue mask appears on the left, looking visibly surprised and then distressed, bringing her hand to her mouth. The man in the kurta again asks, "Who's the CMO?" and with increased urgency, "Where is the CMO, all of you, where is the CMO? Where is he?" He points his finger to emphasize his words and then instructs, "Call him."

At 0:17, the man enters an office area within the hospital, which contains a desk and chairs. He sits down at a desk, still holding his phone. At 0:21, a doctor wearing a white lab coat and a blue mask approaches the desk. The man, pointing his finger at the doctor, begins speaking in Konkani: "Ulo Pao sarke Ritte na. Atam presser tu ek Munsal injetion makapuk you cannot guide him properly. Tu learn to control your tongue. You're a doctor. Don't force me to act. I generally don't lose my cool. But you have to behave yourself. However much you're loaded, you will talk properly to the patient. And guide the patient." (English translation: "You are not talking properly now. Now you will inject a person under pressure. You cannot guide him properly. You learn to control your tongue. You're a doctor. Don't force me to act. I generally don't lose my cool. But you have to behave yourself. However much you're loaded, you will talk properly to the patient. And guide the patient.") He then continues in Konkani: "Urban health center and chal. Ngaa chal mhnon tu kithea karavona. Kithea katar job diata governmentan. Ulo bag rri kason jaithu tu? Tu doctor tu?" (English translation: "You said Urban Health Centre and go. How will you do that? What job will the government give you? How will you talk badly? You're a doctor!") While speaking, the doctor removes his mask. The man adds, "Mask kad bairi. Maka taan bolong zai tu. Sambhalun. Eka suspension order kadd. You're a doctor to serve the poor people here." (English translation: "Take off the mask. I want to talk to you. Be careful. Get a suspension order. You're a doctor to serve the poor people here.")

At 1:07, other individuals, including more medical staff and men in casual attire, gather around. The man in the kurta states, "I am suspending you. When the inquiry is initiated, you give your explanation. Suspension. I want him relieved right now from here. Bhasha au vaparta ekdum naram. Ani jyamond..." (English translation: "I am suspending you. When the inquiry is initiated, you give your explanation. Suspension. I want him relieved right now from here. The language I use is very soft. And the person...") He then directly addresses the doctor, saying, "I am telling you, you listen. Go home. Go home. You can go home. They'll let the residents and senior residents manage. You go home."

At 1:28, the man in the kurta is now outside the hospital building, walking and speaking to an unseen individual off-camera. He explains, "I visited the GMC sir. I visited. I visited for one patient specially. I saw the behavior. And the main thing is, time to time our doctors, all of them do good work, what do they lack. We were hearing complaints of the people about a CMO, a Dr. Rudresh, so I gave orders for his suspension. Such doctors who cannot serve the poor people, they have no work to do in government service. We are ministers, so we work, but such situations should not happen." He concludes by adding, "And the doctors of Goa Medical College work very well, but sometimes such strange creatures are also born, what will they do?" The video concludes at this point.

caption.video0:
The video opens at 0:00 with a medium shot of a large crowd of people gathered outdoors. The camera is positioned at ground level, capturing the backs and sides of numerous individuals, mostly adults. Many are wearing casual clothing. In the foreground, on the left, there are two tall blue banners with Japanese text. A Japanese flag (white with a red circle) is visible to the right of these banners, waving gently. Another smaller Japanese flag and a yellow-green flag with a red circle are also present further back on the left. A modern, light-colored building with many windows stands in the background, behind green trees. On the far left, a watermark with "@POLITICAL AWAKE" and "COLONEL VITARO GATEKEEPER" is overlaid on a stylized image of a person with a sun-like symbol behind their head.

From 0:00 to 0:06, a male voice is speaking in Japanese over a loudspeaker, addressing the crowd. The crowd is listening, with some people turning their heads.

At 0:06, the camera pans slightly to the right, revealing more of the large gathering. Several more blue banners are now clearly visible, each bearing the Japanese text "パンデミック条約絶対阻止！" which translates to "Pandemic Treaty Absolutely Oppose!". The crowd extends deeply into the background, filling a wide outdoor space. Some individuals are seen holding up phones, likely recording the event. The male speaker continues to address the crowd, giving instructions or making announcements.

At 0:21, the camera perspective dramatically shifts to a high-angle, aerial view looking down on the same large crowd. The crowd is immense, densely packed, and fills a wide pathway or street that winds through a park-like setting. Lush green trees line both sides of the path. White blossoms, possibly cherry blossoms, are visible on the trees to the left of the frame. In the distance, several tall buildings are visible. People in the crowd appear to be moving forward, creating a river of humanity.

At 0:25, a female voice, also amplified, shouts a rallying cry in Japanese. The specific words are a bit muffled, but the crowd immediately responds with a loud, enthusiastic cheer and shout at 0:26, "いるからぞ!" (Because we're here! / We exist!).

At 0:28, the female speaker continues, shouting "政府は" (The government is...). The crowd's cheers gradually subside as she speaks.

At 0:30, the female voice clearly shouts, "緊急ワクチン の 全国民 を 廃止せよ!" (Abolish emergency vaccines for all citizens!). The crowd loudly echoes her call at 0:33, shouting "廃止せよ!" (Abolish!).

The high-angle view of the massive crowd continues from 0:30 to 0:37.

At 0:37, the female speaker shouts, "ワクチン接種止めろ！" (Stop vaccine mandates!). The crowd responds with a resounding "止めろ！" (Stop it!) at 0:39.

Immediately after, at 0:40, the female speaker exclaims, "増加死亡激増！" (Increase in deaths skyrocketed!). The crowd powerfully repeats, "激増！" (Skyrocketed!) at 0:41.

Finally, at 0:43, the female speaker cries out, "WHO脱退せよ！" (Withdraw from WHO!). The crowd, with great fervor, shouts back, "脱退せよ！" (Withdraw!) at 0:44, as the video ends.

caption.video0:
The video features an interview with California Governor Gavin Newsom, interspersed with various clips related to the COVID-19 pandemic. The interview segments show two men seated in what appears to be a hotel suite or office. The interviewer, on the left, has brown hair and is wearing a dark suit and white shirt. Governor Newsom, on the right, has dark, swept-back hair and is wearing a dark suit with a white shirt, unbuttoned at the collar. A recurring logo "THE ISSUE IS." is visible in the bottom left corner of the screen throughout the video.

Here's a detailed breakdown of the video's content:

- **00:00-00:05**: The interviewer, seated on a brown leather chair, speaks, gesturing with his hands. He is wearing a grey suit with a red tie and a small green pin on his lapel. Governor Newsom is visible from the side, listening. The interviewer says, "One thing that's been brought up a lot this week is COVID, uh, within the context of the fact that there hasn't been as much communication between the two countries because of COVID and because of the politics of COVID afterwards."
- **00:05-00:08**: The video cuts to a close-up of Governor Newsom, listening intently and nodding slightly. He says, "Yeah."
- **00:08-00:10**: The video shows two medical professionals in a hospital setting. They are wearing blue caps and green N95 masks. One points to an X-ray displayed on a digital screen.
- **00:10-00:12**: A wider shot in a hospital shows multiple medical professionals, also in blue caps, green masks, and white lab coats, working at computers. One stands in the middle, speaking to the group.
- **00:12-00:13**: The video returns to the interviewer speaking in the interview setting. He continues, "Um, you've thought about a lot what we did right and what we did wrong."
- **00:13-00:17**: The video cuts to Governor Newsom, listening and nodding. The interviewer's voiceover continues, "Because there could be another pandemic when you're governor, and you got to do this again."
- **00:17-00:21**: The video transitions to a hospital scene with medical staff in full white PPE suits, including face shields and masks, tending to a patient in a bed.
- **00:21-00:22**: Another medical worker in full PPE pushes a medical cart with equipment through a hospital hallway.
- **00:22-00:26**: The video returns to the interview setting. The interviewer asks, "What do you think was the biggest lesson learned in terms of what we did wrong that maybe you would do differently the next time around?"
- **00:26-00:28**: The video cuts back to Governor Newsom, who begins to speak. He says, "I'm not consumed by what we did wrong."
- **00:28-00:30**: Governor Newsom continues, "I'm consumed a little bit more by what we did right."
- **00:30-00:35**: Newsom animatedly explains, "I mean, we led the nation in terms of, I mean, there's no large state that out-performed California."
- **00:35-00:41**: He gestures with both hands, saying, "One of the top performing states in terms of health, wealth, and education. Let me be specific about that."
- **00:41-00:49**: Newsom uses his hands to emphasize his points. "On a per capita basis, the number of people lost their lives in California is substantially lower than places like Texas and Florida. We out-performed in wealth. We contracted at a smaller rate in 2020."
- **00:49-00:54**: Newsom continues, "We expanded at a faster rate our economy in 2021." He points his finger to emphasize.
- **00:54-00:59**: He further states, "And we had less learning loss than states like Florida and elsewhere. So we out-performed the rest of the nation in that respect."
- **00:59-01:03**: Newsom nods and gestures. "I think we should acknowledge that."
- **01:03-01:09**: He holds his hands out, palms up, then shrugs, saying, "Number two, we also acknowledge at the time, we didn't know what we didn't know. And we're experts, we're geniuses in hindsight."
- **01:09-01:17**: Newsom continues, gesturing with his hands. "Though, I'm not sure that we're as smart as we could be because we've never really done the kind of exercise of humility and accountability."
- **01:17-01:19**: The video transitions to a "Sorry We're Closed" sign hanging in a window, reflecting a street scene.
- **01:19-01:21**: Inside a restaurant, chairs are stacked upside down on tables, indicating it's closed.
- **01:21-01:24**: A shot of a closed "Laundryworld" business with a locked door and paper covering the windows.
- **01:24-01:26**: People in face masks are seen waiting in a socially distanced line outside a Best Buy store.
- **01:26-01:30**: The video returns to Governor Newsom, gesturing with clasped hands. He says, "Uh, to really look back. Right now, the only things that have been done to look back on what could have been done better or smarter, are done in a partisan lens, save what we're trying to do in California."
- **01:30-01:36**: Newsom holds his hands out as if explaining. "I led an effort about nine months ago, maybe it was a year ago, down in Sunnylands."
- **01:36-01:42**: He continues, gesturing with his hands, "Brought world experts, we interviewed people around the world about California's approach to COVID. We said, 'What did we do right? What did we do wrong?' convened for three days with these world-leading experts."
- **01:42-01:48**: He continues to gesture, explaining, "And we have a report that we're looking forward to publishing on areas where we could approve to substantively answer that question."
- **01:48-01:52**: The interviewer points a finger, listening. Newsom continues, "Not emotionally answer that question, through an objective lens, not a partisan."
- **01:52-01:56**: The interviewer, gesturing with open hands, asks, "And some of it, I mean, schools shut down too long?"
- **01:56-02:00**: Newsom, gesturing, says, "I mean, in certain parts of the state, in certain parts of the state they were open."
- **02:00-02:02**: The video shows an empty classroom with desks spaced apart and a "MAX OCCUPANCY 16" sign on the door.
- **02:02-02:04**: Students, some wearing masks, walk outside a school building.
- **02:04-02:10**: The video returns to Governor Newsom, who gestures with hands. "Uh, and they we gave local control. Remember, California has 1,050 school districts. We have a local system. The local control funding formula."
- **02:10-02:14**: Newsom continues, "It's a constitutional construct, and it allows localism."
- **02:14-02:19**: He gestures with both hands, "And we advanced localism. We didn't have command and control. So the question is, do we want to shift that policy and allow a dominant force in Sacramento?"
- **02:19-02:26**: Newsom clasps his hands, then spreads them, saying, "To determine the fate of local school districts. We allowed that local control."
- **02:26-02:30**: He continues, "And in some cases, it was, yes, they waited too long. In other cases, they didn't."
- **02:30-02:34**: Newsom points his finger while speaking, "But, the point is this, if the purpose was to keep people in schools because we wanted to aid in their learning."
- **02:34-02:40**: He gestures with his hands, "It's an interesting fact, isn't it, that states like Florida are out there saying, 'We got, we opened up earlier.'"
- **02:40-02:46**: Newsom emphasizes his point, "And yet they did worse in academic achievement. They they had more learning loss."
- **02:46-02:49**: The interviewer nods while Newsom speaks.
- **02:49-02:55**: Newsom raises his finger to make a point, "In every single category, fourth-grade reading, fourth-grade math, eighth-grade reading, eighth-grade math. Forgive me."
- **02:55-03:00**: He gestures with his hands, "In one category we tied. In every other category, their students fared worse than in California."
- **03:00-03:06**: Newsom points to his head and gestures. "These are facts, and that's why this is not an emotional topic. We have to I think come out of this with a sense of humility."
- **03:06-03:08**: The video cuts to students in a classroom, wearing masks and seated at desks with clear plexiglass dividers between them.
- **03:08-03:10**: A closer shot of students in the classroom, all wearing masks, with plexiglass dividers.
- **03:10-03:14**: The video returns to Governor Newsom, who gestures with his hands. "Absolutely, I'm not naive about things we could have done differently or better."
- **03:14-03:18**: The interviewer points a finger. Newsom continues, "But also a little grace, uh, that California tended to do a little bit better, uh, than most other states."
- **03:18-03:22**: Newsom points his finger and nods, concluding the segment.

**00:00-00:20** The man states, "Right, well, I was involved with building a project, uh, helped with implementing a vaccine payment system for our providers. It's called a pay per dose system. So that means that every time someone gets vaccinated, they get a payment for it as a provider."

**00:20-00:39** He continues, "And, um, I helped build it, I implemented it. And when I was looking at the data, which is part of my job, I noticed some discrepancies with the dates of death, people getting people dying within a week of being vaccinated."

**00:39-01:07** The woman asks, "So what did you notice when you first started to look at this data as you were building it?" The man replies, "Well, as soon as the system went live, we noticed that, um, people were dying almost straight away after being injected. So that sort of prompted my curiosity a bit, and, um, so I dug a little deeper. And I am a scientist by nature. I love science. It's my all time favorite. I've got a master's degree in science."

**01:07-01:19** The woman interjects, "So we don't have potentially." The man continues, "Nobody knows this data. Nobody but me in New Zealand has seen this data. Now I'm giving it to the world." The woman asks, "Explain why. Explain that."

**01:19-02:13** The man explains, "Because it's a payment system and I'm the database administrator for it. I'm the only one. Because New Zealand is you can get away with one database administrator to do this. So I'm in a unique position. And because New Zealand is a tier one country with really good IT, I was able to manage and build the system and be the only database administrator needed to look after it. In other countries like America or Britain, you'd need a whole team of people. So it would be very difficult for one person to get access to all of this information. But in New Zealand, because of its size and because it's got really good IT, I happen to be the one." The woman says, "A lot of people want to know, what was my batch? What can you do to help Kiwis with that?" The man responds, "Okay, so what I did with the data was, um, look at the top ten."

**02:14-02:44** A table titled "10 Worst Sites for Batches" appears on screen. It has four columns: "BATCH ID", "TOTAL VACCINATED", "DEATH COUNT", and "RATIO PERCENTAGE".
*   Row 1: Batch ID 1, Total Vaccinated 711, Death Count 152, Ratio Percentage 21.38%
*   Row 2: Batch ID 8, Total Vaccinated 221, Death Count 38, Ratio Percentage 17.19%
*   Row 3: Batch ID 3, Total Vaccinated 310, Death Count 48, Ratio Percentage 15.48%
*   ...and so on, down to Batch ID 71 with 4.52% mortality.
Below the table, text reads: "CHANCES OF THESE BATCHES NOT BEING A KILLER ARE 100 BILLION TO ONE!"
The man explains, "...batches that were had a high death count, a high mortality rate, and I put them on a chart, which you can see up there. So it's got a batch ID, which is our internal, um, number for a batch. But you can easily get the Pfizer batch ID from that. It shouldn't be too difficult." The woman asks, "Tell us more about that, because a lot of people will say, 'Hey, that's only the top ten. I want to know my batch.' What would you advise?"

**02:44-03:31** The man replies, "Yep, you can do that. There's a, um, there's a website, find my batch for the whole world that can actually find that. Um, and I, we can list this on a website. The whole number of batches, there are 119 individual batches so far in New Zealand." The woman clarifies, "119 of the Pfizer." The man corrects, "Of the Pfizer, actually, no, of all of them, because we have, we have Moderna, we have AstraZeneca, but they're only few and far between. Mostly it's Pfizer. Mostly it's Pfizer in New Zealand. So people can find that, find my batch? Yes, they should be able to, yes. So what I did was our internal batch ID, I counted the number of vaccinated within that batch and then I found out who was dead."

**03:31-03:59** The woman exclaims, "Wow. Let's have a look." She gets up and walks towards a wall-mounted TV displaying the "10 Worst Sites for Batches" table. "And so we then look at the percentages ratio. So do we know if these are all Pfizer, the top ten?" The man confirms, "Yes, they are." The woman points at the table, "And this is Pfizer's batch number one, we've had 711 from batch number one vaccinated. 152 of those died, which makes a 21% percentage death. Death rate from mortality rate from that batch. They are high."

**03:59-04:49** The man continues, "Now, there are different ways to look at the data. You can slice it and dice it. So another way I looked at it was, are the vaccinators themselves, um, what are they doing? Are we looking at some who have got a higher mortality rate than others? And sure enough, if we look at the next one, unfortunately, there are." The screen transitions to another table titled "VACCINATOR NAME", with columns "VACCINATOR NAME", "TOTAL VACCINATED", "DEATH COUNT", and "RATIO PERCENTAGE". The names are redacted (V1, V2, etc.).
*   Row 1: V1, Total Vaccinated 246, Death Count 60, Ratio Percentage 24.39%
*   Row 2: V2, Total Vaccinated 437, Death Count 100, Ratio Percentage 22.88%
*   Row 3: V3, Total Vaccinated 621, Death Count 104, Ratio Percentage 16.75%
*   ...and so on, down to V10 with 10.53% mortality.
The man explains, "So what we have here are the top ten vaccinators who have the highest ratios of mortality." The woman adds, "For privacy reasons, we have redacted the names of those jabbed and the names of those jabbing." The man confirms, "We have to. So we've just called it vaccinator one."

**04:49-06:26** The woman points to the table, "Yeah, but these are individuals, these are real people. These are real numbers. This is government data. So the top V one has vaccinated 246 people, and 60 of them are now no longer with us. That is nearly 25%. One in four. Nearly one in four that that person vaccinated is now dead. And you can come up with any number of reasons for it, but this should never happen. This should never happen. If if they were all doing their job correctly, if there's a normal vaccine, it would be zero point 75." The woman says, "And if you were arguing, okay, there was one vaccinator who was incompetent, not doing the job properly, that's an aberration. But look at the other numbers." She gestures to the other rows in the table. "Yeah. So it looks then we start to say, what is it they were putting into people's bodies? Because the uniformity is what they were putting in. Look at this one. 1621 by vaccinator, the third highest vaccinator, 621, 104 people dead, nearly 17% of the people they jabbed." The man adds, "Yeah. And unless they they're going around, um, terminally ill, cancer wards and injecting people, um, who they know are going to die, then there is no other explanation for this death. And why would they be doing that anyway? You know, it doesn't make any sense. The vaccine is meant to protect those people."

**06:26-07:25** The screen changes to a line graph titled "Daily deaths of more than 120 people from January 2011 to October 2023." The Y-axis is "Deaths" and the X-axis is "Date" (ranging from 2011 to 2024). A red vertical line is labeled "Christchurch earthquake" at around 2011, showing a high spike in deaths. Several black vertical lines are scattered before 2020. A green line, representing "Monthly rate", slowly rises across the graph.
The man explains, "On the left there, the big red line, is the Christchurch earthquake event. So that is the outlier, and that's a lot of people died on that day. From there, the next line where you get more than 120 people dying is sometime 2012. Then you get nothing for a year. 2013 doesn't have any. 2014, you got a couple, then nothing. 2016, you got one, then nothing until 2018, where you get a whole bunch, a whole cluster that's probably due to a flu epidemic, because all these these spikes that you see, these black lines previously on the left hand side, they're all due to the winter flu season. So you got to remember that all these deaths happening June, July, August. So in 2018, we had a big flu epidemic."

**07:25-08:12** The camera zooms in on the right side of the graph, showing more black lines becoming denser and higher after 2020. A red text label "Peak deaths with covid" appears over the highest cluster of black lines around 2022. The man continues, "Moving on to the right, you get another one. Then the next red one, the Christchurch massacre, which was in 2019, in March, but from that you get a cluster in 2020, a whole bunch there. There may have been another bad flu season, we don't know." The camera continues to zoom in further, showing the dense black lines extending into 2021 and 2022.

**08:12-09:13** The screen changes to a new line graph titled "Daily New Deaths in New Zealand" with the subtitle "Novel Coronavirus Daily Deaths". The Y-axis is "Novel Coronavirus Daily Deaths" (0 to 80), and the X-axis is "Date" (Feb 15, 2020 to Sep 30, 2023). A large cluster of grey vertical bars representing daily deaths peaks in mid-2022. Above the graph, text states: "Data retrieved from: https://www.worldometer.com/coronavirus/country/new-zealand/ July 30, 2022, was peak Covid, which coincidentally and conveniently, matches with the Winter flu season."
The man says, "But then from 2021, midway, you see a black line there appearing, and then it's like a flick of a switch. Suddenly the black lines get closer together, and there's more of them, very thick black lines there. So this one here, peak deaths with COVID, with or from COVID. But then the deaths from COVID, if you look at the Worldometer website, it correlates to that. That is the same time. And they say that over 60 people died with or from COVID. Then after that, it suddenly drops off. So after that, you only get a couple of people a day dying with or from COVID. So if you if you go back to the chart there, in that case, you would you would expect to see the black lines disappear because no one's dying from COVID anymore, right? But you're absolutely not. You're seeing the black lines. There are more people dying than ever before. These are the biggest number of deaths we've seen in history of New Zealand. The death rate has gone up 6%, 8% year on year, even after COVID wasn't a thing. The death rate is still going up. And that's illustrated here by these black lines, which are still going on." The video ends.

He states, "That, uh, it's really a fulfillment of a dream that we had together with my leadership team when we started in '19, uh, the first week we met in January of '19 in California and to set up the goals for the next five years. And one of them was by 2023, we will reduce the number of people in the world by 50%. I think today, this dream is becoming reality."

**00:26-00:36:** As the man finishes his statement, an applause erupts from the audience. The camera pulls back to a wider shot, revealing a stage bathed in blue light. The man who was speaking is now visible sitting in a white armchair on the right side of the stage. Another man, also in a suit, is seated in a white armchair facing him on the left. In the center of the stage is a large, circular, elevated platform that glows blue. The background features a large curved screen displaying a pattern of white circles on a blue background, and to the left, another screen shows the "WORLD ECONOMIC FORUM" logo. The background behind her is a textured stone wall, appearing light brown or beige.

From **0:00 to 0:17**, a text overlay appears at the bottom of the screen with a light blue background and white text that reads: "Did you see what they found inside new cancer cells?".

The woman begins by saying, "Dr. Ryan Cole, out of Idaho. Check him out. He's always in trouble with the big machine. They are always trying to discredit him." (0:00-0:07)

She continues, "His most recent antics, he's been working with some doctors out of Germany, and they've been looking at the unusual cancers that are exploding right into stage four." (0:07-0:16)

From **0:17 to 0:33**, the first text overlay disappears, and a new text overlay appears on a black background with white text, positioned slightly higher than the previous one. This text changes several times to build anticipation:
*   At **0:17**, it says "Wait for it ."
*   At **0:21**, it changes to "Wait for it .."
*   At **0:26**, it changes to "Wait for it ."
*   At **0:28**, it changes to "Wait for it ..."
*   At **0:30**, it changes to "Wait for it ."
*   At **0:32**, it changes to "Wait for it .."

During this time, the woman says, "And they're finding in very unusually young ages, as well as those who have been in remission for 10, 15 plus years, because the immune system is being degraded. And can you imagine as they put these cancer cells underneath the microscope, what they're finding?" (0:16-0:33)

From **0:33 to 0:37**, the "Wait for it .." text disappears, and a rectangular microscopic image is overlaid on the lower part of the screen, covering a portion of the woman's torso. The image displays a field of numerous cells, some large and oval/circular with white centers, and others smaller, granular, and brownish, scattered between the larger cells. The woman concludes, "Right in the middle of those cells, they're finding the spike protein." (0:33-0:37)

The video ends with the woman finishing her statement, looking intently at the camera with the microscopic image displayed.
